topics.title: Basic Elements of Discrete Data Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Elements of Discrete Data Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Discrete Data Control System (DDCS)**, also commonly referred to as a **Digital Control System (DCS)**, is a control system in which the reference input, the feedback signal, or the control signal (or any combination thereof) is represented, processed, or transmitted in **discrete-time (sampled-data) form** using digital signals, even though the plant being controlled may be inherently continuous in nature.

Mathematically, the defining characteristic is the presence of a **sampler** (a switch that closes instantaneously at periodic intervals of period $T$ seconds), which converts a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence $x(kT)$ (or $x[k]$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A digital control system is fundamentally a **hybrid (mixed) system**. It contains both continuous-time elements (the physical plant, actuators, sensors) and discrete-time elements (sampler, ADC, digital computer, DAC, hold). Understanding the *interfaces* between these two domains is the single most important learning outcome of Module 1.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car (the **plant**) but you are blindfolded. Your friend standing at the finish line shouts out your current speed only **once every 10 seconds** through a megaphone (the **sensor + sampler**). You, in the car, have a small digital speedometer (**the digital computer/ADC**) that displays the number, and based on that single data point, you press the accelerator or brake for the next 10 seconds (**DAC + Hold**). You never receive continuous information; you only get *snapshots*. This act of decision-making based on *periodic snapshots* is the essence of a Discrete Data Control System.

In real engineering, instead of a blindfolded driver:
- The **plant** is a motor, furnace, robot arm, or chemical process.
- The **sampler** is an electronic switch (FET/MOSFET) gated by a clock.
- The **digital computer** is a microcontroller, DSP, or PLC.
- The **hold device** is a ZOH (Zero-Order Hold) that extrapolates the discrete number into a continuous voltage for the actuator.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in DDCS (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Engineering Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Period | $T$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s/2$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Quantization Step Size | $q = \dfrac{FS}{2^n}$ | **Volts (V)** or **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** |
| Quantization Error (max) | $e_q = \pm q/2$ | **Same units as the signal being quantized** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ‚Äî Quantization:** The process of mapping a continuous range of amplitude values into a finite set of discrete amplitude levels. The difference between the actual analog value and its nearest quantized level is called **quantization error**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Functional Building Blocks of a DDCS

A canonical discrete data control system contains the following six fundamental elements, arranged in a closed feedback loop:

1. **Reference Input Element** ‚Äî generates the desired setpoint $r(t)$ (continuous) or $r[k]$ (discrete).
2. **Error Detector / Summer** ‚Äî computes the actuating error $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ (or $e[k] = r[k] - b[k]$).
3. **Sampler (and Quantizer / ADC)** ‚Äî converts the continuous error signal into a digital number at instants $t = kT$.
4. **Digital Controller (Digital Computer / Microprocessor / DSP)** ‚Äî implements the control algorithm, e.g., $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i e[k]$ (a PI algorithm in discrete form).
5. **DAC + Hold Device (ZOH)** ‚Äî converts the digital control number $u[k]$ into a piecewise-constant continuous signal $u(t)$ that drives the actuator.
6. **Plant (Process) + Actuator + Sensor** ‚Äî the physical system under control, with its associated transducer for feedback.

### 2.2 The Ideal Sampler ‚Äî The Heart of the Discrete System

The ideal sampler is modelled mathematically as a **modulator** that produces a train of weighted impulses:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)$$

where $\delta(t)$ is the **Dirac delta function** and $T$ is the sampling period.

**Key Properties of the Ideal Sampler:**
- It captures the *value* of the signal only at discrete instants.
- Between samples, the sampler output is **mathematically zero** (an impulse has zero width).
- In practice, a **finite-width pulse sampler** (with pulse width $\tau \ll T$) is used, and its output closely approximates the ideal impulse train when $\tau \to 0$.

### 2.3 The Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

The ZOH is the most common **reconstruction device** used after the DAC. It holds each sample value constant for the entire interval $T$ until the next sample arrives.

**Impulse Response of ZOH:**

$$g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t - T)$$

where $1(t)$ is the unit step function. This is a rectangular pulse of height **1** and width **$T$ seconds**.

**Transfer Function of ZOH (in s-domain):**

$$G_{h0}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Frequency Response of ZOH** (magnitude is a sinc function):

$$\vert G_{h0}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \frac{\vert \sin(\omega T / 2) \vert}{\omega T / 2} = T \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ZOH introduces a **phase lag** (delay of approximately $T/2$ seconds on average) and **attenuates high-frequency components**. This lag is the primary reason digital controllers are not as "fast" as continuous controllers at the same gain, and must be compensated for in the design phase.

### 2.4 The Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) and DAC

- **ADC** performs **two operations simultaneously**: (i) *sampling* in time, and (ii) *quantization* in amplitude. Its output is a **finite-precision binary word** (typically $n = 8, 10, 12, 16$ bits).
- **DAC** performs the inverse: converts a binary word into a discrete voltage level, which is then smoothed by the ZOH.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampled signal | $x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t-kT)$ | Impulse train |
| 2 | Laplace of sampled signal | $X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-kTs}$ | $s$ in rad/s |
| 3 | ZOH impulse response | $g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t-T)$ | Rectangular pulse, width $T$ |
| 4 | ZOH transfer function | $G_{h0}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | First-order approximation |
| 5 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (or $\omega_s \geq 2 \omega_{\max}$) | Reconstruct without aliasing |
| 6 | Quantization step | $q = \dfrac{FS}{2^n}$ | $FS$ = full-scale range |
| 7 | Max quantization error | $\vert e_q \vert \leq q/2$ | $e_q$ in LSBs or signal units |
| 8 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | For full-scale sine input |
| 9 | Sampling period ‚Üí frequency | $f_s = 1/T$ | Hz |
| 10 | ZOH average phase delay | $\tau_d \approx T/2$ | seconds |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of the Ideal Sampled Signal

**Given:** The sampler output is a train of weighted impulses.

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the definition of the Laplace transform.** Recall that the Laplace transform of a delayed impulse is:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} = e^{-kTs}$$

This is a fundamental property because the impulse at $t = kT$ "carries" a phase lag of $e^{-kTs}$ in the s-plane.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Use the linearity of the Laplace transform** to move the summation outside:

$$\begin{aligned}
X^*(s) &= \mathcal{L}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)\right\} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-kTs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Final compact form.** This is the standard expression for the Laplace transform of a sampled signal:

$$X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-kTs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** Because the impulse is at $t = kT$, the system "remembers" the input for $kT$ seconds. The exponential $e^{-kTs}$ is the mathematical representation of this time delay. If $k = 0$, $e^0 = 1$ (no delay, present instant). If $k = 1$, $e^{-Ts}$ (one period of delay), and so on.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

**Given:** The impulse response of the ZOH is a rectangular pulse:

$$g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t - T)$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Recall Laplace transform of a step function.** The Laplace transform of $1(t)$ is $1/s$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the time-shifting property.** $\mathcal{L}\{1(t - T)\} = \dfrac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$ (assuming zero initial conditions and using the time-shift theorem: a delay of $T$ multiplies the transform by $e^{-Ts}$).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Subtract to obtain the ZOH transfer function:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(s) &= \mathcal{L}\{1(t)\} - \mathcal{L}\{1(t - T)\} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Approximation for small sampling periods** (used in design analysis). Using the Taylor series expansion $e^{-Ts} \approx 1 - Ts + \dfrac{(Ts)^2}{2!} - \cdots$, we get:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(s) &\approx \frac{1 - (1 - Ts)}{s} = \frac{Ts}{s} = T \quad (\text{zero-order approx.})
\end{aligned}$$

Including the first-order lag term:

$$G_{h0}(s) \approx \frac{1}{1 + sT/2} \quad (\text{first-order Pade approximation})$$

This is **highly significant**: the ZOH behaves like a pure time delay of $T/2$ seconds plus a first-order low-pass filter, and this delay is the dominant reason for performance degradation in digital control loops.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantization Error and SQNR

**Given:** An $n$-bit ADC with full-scale range $FS$ (in Volts).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Number of quantization levels.** For an $n$-bit converter, there are $2^n$ discrete levels spanning the full scale.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Step size (resolution).**

$$q = \frac{FS}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Maximum quantization error.** Since the true value can lie anywhere within $\pm q/2$ of the nearest quantization level:

$$\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{q}{2} = \frac{FS}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for a full-scale sinusoidal input. The standard engineering result is:

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

For example, a **12-bit** ADC provides approximately $6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \approx 74$ dB of dynamic range, while a **16-bit** ADC provides $\approx 98$ dB ‚Äî close to CD-quality audio.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Computing ZOH Output for a Step Input

**Problem:** The digital computer outputs a value $u[0] = 5$ V at $t = 0$, and $u[1] = 3$ V at $t = T = 0.1$ s. The DAC + ZOH converts this into a continuous signal. Sketch $u(t)$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= 5 \cdot [1(t) - 1(t - 0.1)] + 3 \cdot [1(t - 0.1) - 1(t - 0.2)] + \cdots \\[4pt]
u(t) &= 
\begin{cases}
5 \text{ V}, & 0 \leq t < 0.1 \\
3 \text{ V}, & 0.1 \leq t < 0.2 \\
\vdots
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

The ZOH **holds** each value constant for one sampling period, producing a staircase waveform.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Discrete Data Control System

The diagram below maps the **complete signal flow** through all six basic elements of a DDCS, including the dual-domain (continuous/discrete) nature of the signals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R REFERENCE INPUT\nContinuous Setpoint r of t"]:::ct --> SUM1((+)):::node
    BFB["B FEEDBACK PATH\nSampled Feedback b of kT"]:::dt --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> E["E ERROR SIGNAL\ne of t = r minus b of kT\nContinuous"]:::ct
    E --> SMP["SAMPLER\nImpulse Modulator\nPeriod T seconds"]:::node
    SMP --> ADC["ADC\nSample and Quantize\nn bit binary word"]:::node
    ADC --> DC["DIGITAL CONTROLLER\nMicroprocessor or DSP\nImplements control law u of k"]:::dt
    DC --> DAC["DAC\nConvert binary to voltage\nDiscrete levels"]:::node
    DAC --> ZOH["ZERO ORDER HOLD ZOH\nPiecewise constant output\n1 minus e to the minus Ts over s"]:::node
    ZOH --> ACT["ACTUATOR\nAmplifier and Power Stage"]:::ct
    ACT --> PLT["PLANT\nProcess or Motor or Furnace\nContinuous dynamics G of s"]:::ct
    PLT --> OUT["OUTPUT y of t\nControlled Variable"]:::ct
    OUT --> SEN["SENSOR\nTransducer and Signal\nConditioning"]:::ct
    SEN --> BFB
    classDef ct fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dt fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of the ZOH (Mathematical Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DELAY["DELAY ELEMENT\nInput is u of t\nOutput is u of t minus T\nTransfer function e to the minus Ts"]:::node
    SUB((1 minus)):::node
    GAIN["INTEGRATOR 1 over s\nHold integrator"]:::node
    SUB --> GAIN
    IN["u of t Input Sample Stream"]:::ct --> SUB
    IN --> DELAY
    DELAY --> SUB
    GAIN --> OUT["uZOH of t\nStaircase Reconstruction"]:::ct
    classDef ct fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Signal Domain Map ‚Äî Where Does Each Element Live?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CT["CONTINUOUS TIME DOMAIN s plane"]
        R1["Reference r of t"]:::ct
        S1["Sensor Output"]:::ct
        P1["Plant G of s"]:::ct
        Z1["ZOH 1 minus e to the minus Ts over s"]:::ct
    end
    subgraph DT["DISCRETE TIME DOMAIN z plane"]
        AD1["ADC n bit Quantizer"]:::node
        DC1["Digital Controller D of z"]:::node
        DA1["DAC Output"]:::node
    end
    R1 --> SAMPLE1["SAMPLER\nClock T"]:::node
    SAMPLE1 --> AD1
    S1 --> SAMPLE2["SAMPLER\nClock T"]:::node
    SAMPLE2 --> AD1
    AD1 --> DC1
    DC1 --> DA1
    DA1 --> Z1
    Z1 --> P1
    P1 --> S1
    classDef ct fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Blue blocks operate in the **continuous-time (s-domain)**. Orange blocks operate in the **discrete-time (z-domain)**. Purple circles are the **domain-crossing interfaces** (sampler, ADC, DAC, ZOH). This dual-domain view is exactly how KTU board examiners expect you to draw the DDCS block diagram in your answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define a Discrete Data Control System. List any four basic elements of a DDCS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Discrete Data Control System (DDCS)** is a control system in which one or more signals (reference, feedback, or control) are in **discrete-data (sampled) form**, while the plant may be continuous. The digital computer acts as the controller.

The four basic elements are:
1. **Sampler** (with periodic switch of period $T$)
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** ‚Äî performs sampling and quantization
3. **Digital Computer / Controller** ‚Äî implements the control algorithm
4. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) with Hold (ZOH)** ‚Äî reconstructs a continuous signal

*(Additional elements: Plant, Sensor/Transducer, Reference input, Error detector ‚Äî award full credit if any four are listed correctly.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*What is a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)? Write its transfer function and impulse response.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is a reconstruction device that converts a discrete signal $u[k]$ into a piecewise-continuous signal by **holding each sample value constant** for the duration of one sampling period $T$.

**Impulse response:** A rectangular pulse of height 1 and width $T$:

$$g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t - T)$$

**Transfer function:**

$$G_{h0}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Question A:*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of a digital control system and explain the function of each block. Why is a Hold circuit necessary after the DAC?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the transfer function of an ideal Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) from its impulse response. Mention any two limitations of the ZOH.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Function of Each Block (7 marks):**

The block diagram is shown below (refer to Section 4.1 for the full schematic).

| Block | Function | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Reference input $r(t)$ | Generates desired setpoint | Continuous |
| Summer/Error detector | Computes $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ | Continuous |
| Sampler | Captures $e(t)$ at instants $kT$ | Interface |
| ADC | Quantizes and encodes to binary | Interface |
| Digital controller | Computes $u[k] = f(e[k])$ | Discrete |
| DAC + ZOH | Reconstructs $u(t)$ staircase | Interface |
| Actuator | Amplifies power to drive plant | Continuous |
| Plant $G(s)$ | Physical process under control | Continuous |
| Sensor $H(s)$ | Measures output for feedback | Continuous |

**Why a Hold circuit is necessary after the DAC:**

The DAC output is a sequence of voltage levels (numerical values) that exist only at discrete instants $kT$. The plant, however, requires a **continuous input signal** $u(t)$ to function. Without a hold device, the plant input would be a train of impulses or zero between samples, which is physically unrealizable. The ZOH holds each value constant for $T$ seconds, producing a realizable **piecewise-constant staircase** waveform that can drive the actuator.

**(b) Derivation of ZOH Transfer Function (7 marks):**

The impulse response of the ZOH is:

$$g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t - T)$$

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace transform of the unit step:

$$\mathcal{L}\{1(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the time-shift theorem for $1(t-T)$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{1(t - T)\} = \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(s) &= \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Two Limitations of the ZOH:**

1. **Phase lag / Time delay:** The ZOH introduces an effective time delay of approximately $T/2$ seconds, which reduces the phase margin of the closed-loop system and can cause instability.

2. **High-frequency attenuation:** The ZOH acts as a low-pass filter with a sinc-shaped frequency response, attenuating high-frequency content. It is not an ideal reconstruction filter (the ideal is the sinc interpolator, which is non-causal).

3. *(Optional, 3rd point)* **Staircase waveform contains harmonics** of the sampling frequency, which may excite unmodelled plant dynamics.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Module 1]**
*Question B (Internal Choice Alternative):*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the function of the **Sampler** in a discrete data control system. Derive the Laplace transform of the sampled signal $x^*(t)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An 8-bit ADC is used in a digital control loop with full-scale range $FS = 10$ V. Compute (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the SQNR in dB.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sampler Function and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Function of the Sampler:** The sampler is an **electronically controlled switch** (typically a MOSFET or JFET switch gated by a clock of period $T$) that closes instantaneously at regular intervals $t = 0, T, 2T, \ldots, kT$ and remains open otherwise. Its purpose is to **convert a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence of values $x(kT)$**, which can then be processed by a digital computer. The sampler is the **gateway between the continuous and discrete domains**.

**Derivation of $X^*(s)$:**

The output of an ideal sampler is a train of weighted impulses:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)$$

Taking the Laplace transform of both sides and using the linearity property:

$$\begin{aligned}
X^*(s) &= \mathcal{L}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)\right\} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \cdot \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-kTs}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the closed-form expression for the Laplace transform of a sampled signal.

**(b) ADC Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $FS = 10$ V.

**(i) Quantization step size $q$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{FS}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} \\[4pt]
q &= 0.0390625 \text{ V} \approx 39.06 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum quantization error:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert e_q \vert_{\max} &= \frac{q}{2} = \frac{0.0390625}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.01953 \text{ V} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SQNR_{dB} &= 6.02 \, n + 1.76 \\[4pt]
&= 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 \\[4pt]
&= 48.16 + 1.76 \\[4pt]
&= 49.92 \text{ dB} \approx 50 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the sampler with the ADC.** A sampler is a *time-domain* operation only; an ADC is a *time + amplitude-domain* operation (sample + quantize). Forgetting the quantization step is a guaranteed 1‚Äì2 mark deduction.
> 2. **In the ZOH derivation**, many students incorrectly write the impulse response as $g_{h0}(t) = 1(t-T)$ (only the second term). The correct expression is the **difference of two step functions**: $g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t-T)$.
> 3. **The Laplace transform of a sampled signal is NOT $X(s) = X(z)$** with $s = z$. The correct relation involves the substitution $z = e^{Ts}$ only for the *starred* transform: $X(z) = X^*(s) \vert_{z=e^{Ts}}$. Board examiners *will* deduct marks for sloppy notation.
> 4. **For the SQNR formula**, the coefficient is **6.02**, not 6 or 6.4. A common student error is using 6 dB/bit, which is an approximation valid only for large $n$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Basic Elements of Discrete Data Control Systems**

- A **DDCS** is a hybrid system with **continuous-time and discrete-time** signals coexisting in one closed loop.
- The **six basic elements** are: Reference input ‚Üí Error detector ‚Üí Sampler ‚Üí ADC ‚Üí Digital Computer ‚Üí DAC + ZOH ‚Üí Plant ‚Üí Sensor ‚Üí back to error detector.
- The **sampler** is mathematically modelled as an **impulse modulator**: $x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)$.
- The **Laplace transform of a sampled signal** is $X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-kTs}$.
- The **ZOH impulse response** is a rectangular pulse: $g_{h0}(t) = 1(t) - 1(t - T)$.
- The **ZOH transfer function** is $G_{h0}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$.
- The **ZOH** is a low-pass filter with sinc-shaped magnitude response and introduces a **phase delay of $\approx T/2$** seconds.
- **Quantization step size**: $q = FS / 2^n$.
- **Maximum quantization error**: $\vert e_q \vert \leq q/2$.
- **SQNR for a full-scale sine input**: $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- **Nyquist sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ is required for lossless reconstruction.
- The **ADC** combines sampling (time) + quantization (amplitude); the **DAC** converts binary to voltage.
- The **ZOH is non-ideal**: it adds phase lag and attenuates high frequencies ‚Äî these effects must be compensated during controller design.
- **Domain mapping rule**: $z = e^{sT}$ is the bridge between s-plane (continuous) and z-plane (discrete).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Discrete Data Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Discrete Data Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Discrete Data Control System** (also known as a **Digital Control System** or **Sampled-Data Control System**) is a control system in which the reference input, the feedback signal, and/or the control error signal are processed at discrete instants of time, typically using digital computers, microcontrollers, digital signal processors (DSPs), or programmable logic controllers (PLCs). The continuous-time plant is interfaced to the digital controller through **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)** at the input and **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC)** at the output, often accompanied by **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the discrete (sampled) domain, signals are represented as sequences $x(kT)$ or $x(k)$ where $T$ is the **sampling period** (in **seconds**). The entire analysis migrates from the **Laplace domain ($s$)** to the **Z-domain ($z$)** using the transformation $z = e^{sT}$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are driving a car on a long highway, but instead of continuously adjusting the steering wheel and accelerator with every tiny bump, you **check the road conditions once every 5 seconds** and make corrections only at those instants. This "check-and-correct at regular intervals" approach is exactly how a discrete data control system works.

The car is the **plant (process)**. Your eyes are the **sensors** (analog input). The 5-second interval is the **sampling period ($T$)**. Your brain processing what it sees is the **digital controller**. Your hands on the steering wheel are the **actuator (DAC + ZOH)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sample at all?** Because digital computers cannot process *infinitely fast*. They take a snapshot of the continuous signal, convert it to a binary number, crunch the math, and output a new value. The key insight is that this *intermittent* approach is not only **cheaper and more reliable**, but for many practical systems (motors, heaters, robotic arms), it is **more than sufficient**.

### 1.3 Core Distinction: Continuous vs. Discrete Data Control

| Feature | Continuous (Analog) Control | Discrete (Digital) Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Nature** | Defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Defined only at $t = kT, k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| **Hardware Core** | Op-amps, RLC networks, analog ICs | Microprocessors, microcontrollers, DSPs |
| **Implementation of Laws** | Hardwired (resistors, capacitors) | Software (re-programmable) |
| **Drift Susceptibility** | High (component aging) | Negligible (software-based) |
| **Noise Immunity** | Low‚ÄìModerate | Very High (binary thresholds) |
| **Cost for Complex Logic** | Exponentially rising | Linear / near-constant |
| **Data Logging & Telemetry** | Requires extra circuitry | Native to digital processors |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ten Pillars of Advantage ‚Äî Structured Logic

The superiority of discrete data control systems over their analog counterparts can be rigorously organized into the following ten engineering pillars. Each pillar is paired with the **"Why it matters"** rationale to cement intuitive understanding.

#### **Pillar 1: Superior Accuracy and Numerical Precision**
- **How:** Control actions are computed using floating-point or fixed-point arithmetic, achieving effective precision of **16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit** resolution.
- **Why it matters:** Analog components (resistors, capacitors) drift due to temperature, aging, and humidity, causing accuracy losses of 1‚Äì5%. Digital precision is limited only by the ADC/DAC bit width.

#### **Pillar 2: Software-Based Flexibility & Reconfigurability**
- **How:** The control law (e.g., PID, state-feedback, fuzzy, neural) is implemented in software. Changing gains, structure, or even the entire algorithm requires only a code update.
- **Why it matters:** In automotive ECUs, a single hardware platform can support different vehicle variants through firmware changes ‚Äî a near-impossible feat with pure analog hardware.

#### **Pillar 3: Excellent Noise Immunity**
- **How:** Signals are quantized into discrete voltage levels. Small noise perturbations below the quantization threshold are inherently rejected.
- **Why it matters:** In industrial environments laden with electromagnetic interference (EMI) from motors and relays, digital signals remain robust where analog signals would corrupt.

#### **Pillar 4: Implementation of Complex Mathematical Operations**
- **How:** Multiplications, divisions, integrations, and differentiations are executed using arithmetic logic units (ALUs) at nanosecond speeds.
- **Why it matters:** Algorithms like **Kalman filtering, Model Predictive Control (MPC), and Adaptive Control** are practically infeasible in pure analog form.

#### **Pillar 5: Reduced Physical Size, Weight, and Power (SWaP)**
- **How:** A single microcontroller chip replaces hundreds of discrete analog components.
- **Why it matters:** Critical in **aerospace, drones, satellites, and portable medical devices** where SWaP directly translates to mission capability.

#### **Pillar 6: Reliable Long-Term Operation (No Drift)**
- **How:** Digital memory (ROM/Flash) and digital logic are immune to the thermal drift and component aging that plague analog circuits.
- **Why it matters:** A digital thermostat maintains calibration for **10+ years** without manual trimming; an analog equivalent requires periodic recalibration.

#### **Pillar 7: Seamless Integration with Communication Networks**
- **How:** Native compatibility with **CAN, Modbus, Ethernet, SPI, I¬≤C, UART, and wireless protocols (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, LoRa)**.
- **Why it matters:** Enables **Industry 4.0**, IoT-enabled factories, and centralized SCADA monitoring.

#### **Pillar 8: Native Data Logging, Telemetry, and Diagnostics**
- **How:** Time-stamped data can be stored in non-volatile memory or transmitted to cloud servers.
- **Why it matters:** Facilitates **predictive maintenance, fault detection, and post-incident forensic analysis** in plants, aircraft, and power grids.

#### **Pillar 9: Cost-Effectiveness at Scale**
- **How:** Mass-produced microcontrollers cost a few dollars each, and software development cost is **amortized** across millions of units.
- **Why it matters:** Brings sophisticated control to consumer products (washing machines, drones, EVs) that would otherwise be economically infeasible.

#### **Pillar 10: Repeatability, Reproducibility, and Standardization**
- **How:** Identical software binary produces identical behavior across all units.
- **Why it matters:** Critical in **regulated industries (medical devices, aviation DO-178C, automotive ISO 26262)** where unit-to-unit consistency is a safety mandate.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampled signal | $x(kT) = x(t)\big\vert_{t=kT}$ | Time ($t$ or $kT$) | $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| 3 | Sampling angular frequency | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Frequency | **rad/s** |
| 4 | Nyquist‚ÄìShannon rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Frequency | **Hz**; $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. |
| 5 | Ideal sampler (impulse train) | $x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t-kT)$ | Continuous time | $\delta$ is Dirac delta |
| 6 | Z-transform (sampled signal) | $X(z) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,z^{-k}$ | Complex $z$ | $|z| > $ ROC radius |
| 7 | Z-OH output | $u(t) = u(kT), \quad kT \leq t < (k+1)T$ | Time | $T$ in **seconds** |
| 8 | Z-OH transfer function | $G_{ZOH}(s) = \dfrac{1-e^{-sT}}{s}$ | Laplace ($s$) | Used in **w-plane** mapping |
| 9 | Starred Laplace transform | $X^*(s) = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X(s+j n \omega_s)$ | Frequency | Aliasing relation |
| 10 | Sampling theorem condition | $\omega_s \geq 2\,\omega_{max}$ | Frequency | **rad/s**; violation ‚Üí **aliasing** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every formula above is used industrially in **designing motor speed controllers in CNC machines, flight control surfaces in drones, cruise control in EVs, and temperature loops in semiconductor cleanrooms**. Understanding the sampling theorem and ZOH behavior is essential to prevent **aliasing** and **stability loss**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Signal to Sampled Sequence

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is converted to a discrete sequence by an **ideal impulse sampler**. Let us derive its mathematical form step by step.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the impulse train.**
The sampling switch is modeled as a periodic train of unit impulses (Dirac delta functions), each separated by the sampling period $T$:

$$
\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Multiply the continuous signal by the impulse train.**
The sampled (or "starred") signal $x^*(t)$ is the product of the continuous signal $x(t)$ and the impulse train:

$$
x^*(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t)
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the sifting property of the delta function.**
Since $x(t) \cdot \delta(t - kT) = x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$, the sampled signal becomes:

$$
x^*(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)
$$

This is the **canonical mathematical representation** of a sampled signal used throughout KTU module 1.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Take the Laplace transform (starred Laplace transform).**
Because the Laplace transform of $\delta(t - kT)$ is $e^{-kTs}$:

$$
X^*(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x^*(t)\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT)\,e^{-kTs}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Introduce the substitution $z = e^{sT}$.**
This is the pivotal step that bridges the Laplace domain and the $z$-domain:

$$
z = e^{sT} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad s = \frac{1}{T}\ln(z)
$$

Substituting $e^{-kTs} = z^{-k}$ into Step 4 yields the **Z-transform**:

$$
X(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT)\,z^{-k}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For one-sided (causal) signals** where $x(kT) = 0$ for $k < 0$, the summation starts at $k = 0$, which is the form most commonly used in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example ‚Äî Why Sampling Frequency Matters

**Problem:** A temperature sensor outputs a sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 10\,t)$ volts, where the frequency is **10 Hz**. The system samples at $T = 0.05$ s.

**Sub-question (a):** Determine whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_{max} = 10 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_s = 20 \text{ Hz} \quad\text{vs.}\quad 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ ‚Äî this is the **boundary case**. The Nyquist criterion is technically satisfied *with equality*, but in practice the system is at the **edge of aliasing**.

**Sub-question (b):** If $T$ is doubled to $T = 0.1$ s, what happens?

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10 \text{ Hz} < 2 f_{max} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** Nyquist criterion is **violated** ‚Üí **aliasing** occurs ‚Üí the digital controller receives a *false* frequency (specifically, an apparent $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{max} \vert = 0$ Hz ‚Äî a DC offset!). The control system would interpret a 10 Hz oscillation as a *steady temperature error* and try to "correct" a non-existent drift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This example is a classic KTU module-1 conceptual question. Always explicitly state the Nyquist condition $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ before drawing conclusions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Mapping Advantages to System Properties

| Advantage Pillar | Hardware Manifestation | Software Manifestation | Failure Mode if Absent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 16-/32-bit ADC | Floating-point math | Quantization noise, offset errors |
| Flexibility | Flash memory, bootloader | Re-programmable firmware | Hardware redesign required |
| Noise immunity | Schmitt triggers, optocouplers | Digital filtering, averaging | False triggering, sensor glitches |
| Complex math | DSP/MCU with FPU | Kalman, MPC, neural nets | System restricted to PID only |
| SWaP | SoC (System-on-Chip) | Optimized firmware | Bulky, heavy, power-hungry |
| No drift | Solid-state memory | Software constants | Frequent recalibration needed |
| Networking | CAN/Ethernet PHY | TCP/IP, MQTT stacks | Isolated, non-IoT systems |
| Data logging | EEPROM/SD card | Timestamped buffers | No audit trail, no analytics |
| Low cost | Mass-produced MCUs | Open-source libraries | Prohibitive for consumer use |
| Repeatability | Identical silicon dies | Identical firmware binary | Unit-to-unit performance variation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Generic Digital Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **canonical architecture** of a digital (discrete data) control system, highlighting the placement of the **ADC**, **Digital Controller**, **DAC**, and **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**. This is one of the most frequently drawn diagrams in KTU Module 1 examinations.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["Reference Input R(s)"] --> SUMA["Summing Junction (+/-)"]
    A["Analog Output Plant Y(s)"] --> ADC["Analog-to-Digital Converter ADC"]
    ADC --> FBK["Feedback Signal Y(kT)"]
    FBK --> SUMA
    SUMA --> E["Error Signal E(kT)"]
    E --> DC["Digital Controller D(z)"]
    DC --> DAC["Digital-to-Analog Converter DAC"]
    DAC --> ZOH["Zero-Order Hold ZOH"]
    ZOH --> ACT["Actuator / Plant Gp(s)"]
    ACT --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The reference input $R(s)$ is in continuous form, but inside the loop the error $E(kT)$ is **sampled**. The ZOH holds the last computed control value constant until the next sampling instant, providing a **staircase approximation** to the controller output.

### 4.2 Comparison Flow ‚Äî Analog vs. Digital Control Philosophy

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Plant Gp(s) Needs Regulation"] --> Q1{"Signal Domain?"}
    Q1 -- "Continuous Time" --> A1["Analog Controller Da(s)"]
    Q1 -- "Discrete Time" --> A2["Digital Controller D(z)"]
    A1 --> A3["Op-amp based PID R, L, C"]
    A2 --> A4["Microcontroller / DSP Software PID"]
    A3 --> Q2{"Change Required?"}
    A4 --> Q2
    Q2 -- "Yes - Hardware Swap" --> A5["Costly, time consuming"]
    Q2 -- "Yes - Software Update" --> A6["Fast, cheap, in-field"]
    A5 --> OUT["Disadvantage of Analog"]
    A6 --> OUT2["Advantage of Digital"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture ‚Äî Advantages Mapped to System Layers

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        A1["Complex Algorithms MPC, Fuzzy, AI"]
        A2["Remote Monitoring via IoT"]
    end
    subgraph FW["Firmware Layer"]
        F1["Re-programmable Control Law"]
        F2["Adaptive Gain Scheduling"]
        F3["Self-Diagnostics and Logging"]
    end
    subgraph HW["Hardware Layer"]
        H1["32-bit Microcontroller with FPU"]
        H2["16-bit ADC with Anti-Aliasing Filter"]
        H3["PWM Output Stage with ZOH Behavior"]
        H4["CAN / Ethernet Communication"]
    end
    APP --> FW
    FW --> HW
    H1 --> ADV["Ten Pillars of Advantage Realized"]
    H2 --> ADV
    H3 --> ADV
    H4 --> ADV
    F1 --> ADV
    F2 --> ADV
    F3 --> ADV
    A1 --> ADV
    A2 --> ADV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** The three Mermaid diagrams above represent the **block-level functional architecture** of the system. For complex physical drawings (e.g., circuit-level schematics of an ADC front-end with anti-aliasing filter), refer to standard op-amp and sampling theorem references covered later in KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a discrete data control system. List any four advantages of digital control systems over analog control systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **discrete data control system** is one in which one or more signals (reference, feedback, or error) exist only at discrete time instants $t = kT$, where $T$ is the sampling period and $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$. The system uses an ADC, a digital controller, and a DAC with a ZOH to interface with the continuous plant.

**Four advantages** (1/2 mark each, plus 1 mark for definition):
1. **High accuracy and precision** ‚Äî limited only by ADC/DAC bit width.
2. **Software-based flexibility** ‚Äî control laws can be modified without hardware changes.
3. **Excellent noise immunity** ‚Äî binary quantization rejects small perturbations.
4. **Implementation of complex control algorithms** ‚Äî Kalman, MPC, adaptive, fuzzy, neural.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*State the Nyquist‚ÄìShannon sampling theorem. Why is it critical in discrete data control systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Nyquist‚ÄìShannon sampling theorem** states that a band-limited continuous signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ (Hz) can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

where $f_s = 1/T$ is the sampling frequency in **Hz** and $T$ is the sampling period in **seconds**.

**Criticality (2 marks):**
- If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the phenomenon of **aliasing** occurs, where high-frequency components fold back into the lower frequency band and become indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency signals.
- In a control loop, aliasing can cause the digital controller to issue **erroneous control actions**, leading to instability, oscillation, or even physical damage to the plant.
- Therefore, **anti-aliasing filters** (low-pass filters placed before the ADC) are mandatory in any practical discrete data control system.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically worth **7 marks each**. Students are given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** within the same module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Advantages, Sampling, and ZOH Behavior*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic structure of a discrete data control system. Identify and briefly describe the function of **five key blocks**.

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reference Input $R(s)$** | The desired value of the controlled variable (e.g., setpoint temperature, speed). |
| 2 | **Error Detector / Summing Junction** | Computes $E(kT) = R(kT) - Y(kT)$, the difference between setpoint and feedback. |
| 3 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | Samples and quantizes the continuous error signal into a binary number with finite resolution (e.g., 12-bit). |
| 4 | **Digital Controller $D(z)$** | Implements the control algorithm (PID, state feedback) in software/firmware on a processor. |
| 5 | **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + ZOH** | Converts the digital control output to a staircase analog signal held constant for one sampling period. |
| 6 | **Plant $G_p(s)$** | The physical process (motor, furnace, robotic arm) being controlled. |
| 7 | **Sensor + Feedback Path** | Measures the output $Y(s)$ and feeds it back to the summing junction. |

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 above (the same Mermaid diagram may be redrawn in the answer script).

**Awarding marks (7):** 1 mark for the block diagram, 6 marks distributed across the five block descriptions (approx. 1.2 marks each).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A position control system has a maximum expected shaft oscillation frequency of **25 Hz**. Determine:
1. The minimum acceptable sampling frequency.
2. The corresponding sampling period.
3. The ZOH transfer function in the $s$-domain for this period.
4. The expected frequency of the reconstructed alias if sampling is done at exactly 30 Hz.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) ‚Äî Minimum sampling frequency:**
Applying the Nyquist criterion:

$$
f_{s,min} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Sampling period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{s,min}} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Part (iii) ‚Äî ZOH transfer function:**

$$
G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-0.02\,s}}{s}
$$

**Part (iv) ‚Äî Alias frequency at $f_s = 30$ Hz:**
First, verify that $f_s = 30$ Hz is **less than** $2 f_{max} = 50$ Hz, so aliasing will occur. The aliased frequency is:

$$
f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{max} \vert = \vert 30 - 25 \vert = 5 \text{ Hz}
$$

The controller would mistakenly see a 5 Hz oscillation and attempt to dampen a phantom signal.

**Awarding marks (7):** 2 marks for (i), 1 mark for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 2 marks for (iv).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Comparative Analysis and Real-World Applications*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare analog and digital control systems under the following heads: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Flexibility, (iii) Noise immunity, (iv) Cost for complex algorithms, (v) Drift, (vi) Data logging, (vii) Communication. State which system is preferred in modern Industry 4.0 applications and justify.

**Model Solution (Tabular Format Expected):**

| Head | Analog Control | Digital Control | Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Accuracy | Limited by component tolerances (~1‚Äì5%) | Limited by ADC bits (~0.001% at 16-bit) | **Digital** |
| (ii) Flexibility | Hardware redesign required | Software update only | **Digital** |
| (iii) Noise immunity | Susceptible to EMI, ground loops | High (binary thresholds) | **Digital** |
| (iv) Cost for complex algorithms | Rises exponentially | Rises linearly | **Digital** |
| (v) Drift | Yes (thermal, aging) | Negligible (digital memory) | **Digital** |
| (vi) Data logging | Requires extra circuitry | Native feature | **Digital** |
| (vii) Communication | Difficult to network | Native CAN/Ethernet/Wi-Fi | **Digital** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Digital control systems are **unambiguously preferred** for modern Industry 4.0 applications due to their native support for IoT, cloud connectivity, edge AI, and predictive maintenance workflows.

**Awarding marks (7):** Approx. 0.8 marks per head + 1 mark for conclusion.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic arm in an automated assembly line uses a digital controller with a sampling period of $T = 0.01$ s. The arm's natural sway frequency is 8 Hz, and engineers want to add **safety vibration monitoring**.
1. Calculate the sampling frequency.
2. Check if Nyquist criterion is satisfied for the 8 Hz sway.
3. If the plant transfer function (joint angle to motor voltage) is $G_p(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+5)}$, write the ZOH-augmented transfer function $G_{ZOH}(s) \cdot G_p(s)$.
4. Recommend a suitable anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i):**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Part (ii):**

$$
2 f_{max} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ Hz}
$$

Since $f_s = 100$ Hz $\gg$ 16 Hz, the **Nyquist criterion is comfortably satisfied** with a large safety margin of 84 Hz. This is good engineering practice.

**Part (iii):**

$$
G_{ZOH}(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-0.01\,s}}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+5)} = \frac{10\,(1 - e^{-0.01\,s})}{s^2(s+5)}
$$

**Part (iv):** A common engineering rule is to set the anti-aliasing filter cutoff at $f_c = 0.4\,f_s$ (the "40% rule") to allow for the **filter roll-off transition band**:

$$
f_c = 0.4 \times 100 = 40 \text{ Hz}
$$

A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with $f_c = 40$ Hz would be an excellent practical choice, providing 12 dB/octave attenuation beyond 40 Hz.

**Awarding marks (7):** 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 marks respectively.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** ‚Äî Sampling period $T$ must be in **seconds**, frequency in **Hz**, angular frequency in **rad/s**. Examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ with $f_s \geq f_{max}$** ‚Äî Halving the sampling rate is the single most common conceptual error. Always write the *full* Nyquist condition first.
> 3. **Writing $G_{ZOH}(s) = 1/s$** ‚Äî Forgetting the $(1 - e^{-sT})$ factor. The full form $\frac{1-e^{-sT}}{s}$ is **mandatory** and frequently tested.
> 4. **Ignoring causality** ‚Äî When writing Z-transforms, ensure the sequence starts at $k = 0$, not $k = -\infty$, unless explicitly stated as a two-sided transform.
> 5. **Not drawing arrows in block diagrams** ‚Äî KTU examiners often deduct marks for unlabeled signals (no $R(s)$, $Y(s)$, $E(kT)$ on the arrows).
> 6. **Omitting anti-aliasing filter discussion** ‚Äî A complete answer must mention that an analog low-pass filter *precedes* the ADC to prevent aliasing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Advantages of Discrete Data Control Systems**
> - **Definition:** A control system where signals are processed at discrete instants $t = kT$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, using digital hardware.
> - **Core block chain:** Reference ‚Üí Error ‚Üí **ADC** ‚Üí **Digital Controller $D(z)$** ‚Üí **DAC + ZOH** ‚Üí Plant ‚Üí Sensor ‚Üí Feedback.
> - **Ten Advantages (memorize in order):** Accuracy, Flexibility, Noise Immunity, Complex Algorithms, SWaP, No Drift, Network Integration, Data Logging, Cost-Effectiveness, Repeatability.
> - **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ ‚Äî violation causes **aliasing**.
> - **Sampling frequency:** $f_s = 1/T$ (**Hz**), where $T$ is the sampling period in **seconds**.
> - **Angular sampling frequency:** $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ (**rad/s**).
> - **ZOH Transfer Function:** $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1-e^{-sT}}{s}$ ‚Äî produces a **staircase** output.
> - **Z-transform definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,z^{-k}$.
> - **Laplace-to-Z bridge:** $z = e^{sT}$, equivalently $s = \frac{1}{T}\ln(z)$.
> - **Practical safeguards:** Always include an **anti-aliasing filter** (typically $f_c = 0.4 f_s$) before the ADC.
> - **Industrial examples:** CNC machines, EV cruise control, drone flight controllers, IoT thermostats, robotic arms in Industry 4.0 factories.
> - **Units mantra:** $T$ in **s**, $f$ in **Hz**, $\omega$ in **rad/s** ‚Äî never mix them up in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Signal Conversion & Processing: Digital Signals & Coding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Digital Signal? A **digital signal** is a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal whose amplitude is constrained to a finite set of values and is defined only at specific instants of time. In the context of digital control systems, a digital signal is most commonly represented as a binary sequence ‚Äî a string of **1s** and **0s** that a microprocessor, microcontroller, or digital controller can process. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Terminology):** > A digital signal is a quantized, time-discrete signal represented by a finite number of amplitude levels (usually two ‚Äî logic HIGH and logic LOW). The process of converting a real-world analog signal into a digital signal involves **sampling, quantization, and coding**. ### 1.2 The Four Classes of Signals | Signal Type | Time | Amplitude | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Analog (Continuous-Continuous) | Continuous | Continuous | Speech, temperature | | Sampled-data (Discrete-Continuous) | Discrete | Continuous | PAM signal | | Quantized (Continuous-Discrete) | Continuous | Discrete | A staircase waveform | | Digital (Discrete-Discrete) | Discrete | Discrete | Binary bitstream | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Light Switch World > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** > Imagine a **light switch** in your room. It is either **ON (1)** or **OFF (0)** ‚Äî there is no in-between state. Now imagine 8 such switches arranged side by side. Together, they can represent $2^8 = 256$ different combinations. This is exactly how a **digital signal** works in a control system: instead of continuously varying voltages (like a dimmer), we snap the signal into a fixed number of levels and represent each level by a unique binary code. A **sampling** event decides *when* we read the signal, and a **coding** scheme decides *how* we represent each level as 1s and 0s. ### 1.4 Key Terms at a Glance - **Bit:** The smallest unit of digital information, representing a **0** or **1**. - **Bit rate (R_b):** Number of bits transmitted per second, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. - **Baud rate:** Number of *signal changes* (symbols) per second. For binary, baud = bps. - **Word:** A group of $N$ bits processed together by the controller. - **LSB (Least Significant Bit) & MSB (Most Significant Bit):** Bit positions in a binary word. - **Quantization Level:** The discrete amplitude step a continuous sample is rounded to. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > The KTU Module 1 explicitly expects students to clearly distinguish between **analog vs. digital signals**, understand the role of **coding schemes** in transmission and storage, and recognize how these foundational concepts feed into **Signal Conversion (A/D & D/A)** blocks of a digital control loop. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Stage Signal Conversion Process A typical digital control system ingests a real-world **analog signal** (temperature, pressure, velocity) and converts it to a digital form for the controller. This requires three sequential operations: **Step 1 ‚Äî Sampling (Discretization in Time)** - The continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is observed at uniform intervals $T$ (the **sampling period**). - Result: a sequence $x(kT)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$. **Step 2 ‚Äî Quantization (Discretization in Amplitude)** - Each sample $x(kT)$ is rounded to the nearest of $2^N$ allowed amplitude levels, where $N$ is the **word length (bits)** of the ADC. - This introduces the **quantization error** $e_q$. **Step 3 ‚Äî Coding (Binary Encoding)** - Each quantized level is mapped to a unique **N-bit binary codeword**. - Common codes: Natural Binary, BCD, Gray, Sign-Magnitude, Two's Complement. ### 2.2 Sampling Theorem ‚Äî The Nyquist Criterion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shannon‚ÄìNyquist Sampling Theorem:** > A bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: > $$f_s \ge 2 f_m$$ - The minimum allowable rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is the **Nyquist rate** ($f_N$). - The minimum allowable interval, $T = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$, is the **Nyquist interval**. - If $f_s < 2f_m$, **aliasing** occurs ‚Äî high-frequency components masquerade as low-frequency ones, distorting the reconstructed signal. ### 2.3 Quantization ‚Äî Mathematics and Error The **quantization step size** (resolution) of a unipolar ADC spanning the range $[0, V_{FS}]$ with $N$ bits is: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$ - Number of distinct quantization levels: $L = 2^N$ - Maximum quantization error (assuming mid-tread/riser rounding): $e_{q,\max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ - The **mean-square quantization noise** for a uniform quantizer is: $$E[e_q^2] = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ The **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** in decibels is the cornerstone KTU formula for ADC design: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by **~6 dB** (a fourfold improvement in noise performance). A 12-bit ADC ($N=12$) provides approximately **74 dB** of dynamic range, which is why industrial-grade controllers use 12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit converters. ### 2.4 Number Systems Used in Digital Control | System | Base | Digits Used | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | Binary | 2 | 0, 1 | Internal CPU operations | | Octal | 8 | 0‚Äì7 | Legacy shorthand for binary | | Hexadecimal | 16 | 0‚Äì9, A‚ÄìF | Memory addresses, machine code | | BCD | 10 (4-bit groups) | 0000‚Äì1001 | Displays, decimal I/O | | Two's Complement | 2 | 0, 1 | Signed arithmetic in DSPs | **Two's complement range for N bits:** $-(2^{N-1})$ to $+(2^{N-1}-1)$ ### 2.5 Line Coding Schemes ‚Äî The "Coding" of the Topic Line codes define how binary 0s and 1s are represented as voltage/current pulses on a wire. The KTU syllabus explicitly requires knowledge of the following: | Code | Binary 1 | Binary 0 | DC Component | Bandwidth | Polarity | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Unipolar NRZ** | +V for full bit | 0 V | High | Low (B = Rb/2) | Single | | **Polar NRZ-L** | +V | ‚àíV | Low | Low | Bipolar | | **Unipolar RZ** | +V for half bit | 0 V | High | High (B = Rb) | Single | | **Bipolar RZ (AMI)** | Alternating +V/‚àíV | 0 V | None | Medium | Bipolar | | **Manchester (Biphase-L)** | High-to-Low transition | Low-to-High transition | None | High (B = Rb) | Bipolar | | **Differential Manchester** | No transition at mid-bit | Transition at mid-bit | None | High (B = Rb) | Bipolar | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Manchester Coding Dominates Control Buses:** > Protocols like **CAN bus** and **Ethernet (10Base-T, IEEE 802.3)** use Manchester coding because every bit contains a guaranteed mid-bit transition. This provides a **self-clocking** property ‚Äî the receiver can recover the clock directly from the data without a separate clock line. This is critical in noisy industrial control environments. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Notes | |---|---|---| | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Minimum sampling frequency | | Sampling Period | $T = 1/f_s$ | Must satisfy $T \le 1/(2f_m)$ | | Quantization Levels | $L = 2^N$ | N = number of bits | | Step Size (Resolution) | $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ | V_FS = full-scale range | | Max Quantization Error | $\vert e_q \vert \le \Delta/2$ | Mid-tread quantizer | | Mean-Square Quantization Noise | $E[e_q^2] = \Delta^2/12$ | Uniform distribution | | SQNR (dB) | $6.02 N + 1.76$ | For full-scale sine input | | Two's Complement Range | $[-2^{N-1},\ 2^{N-1}-1]$ | Signed integer representation | | Bit Rate ‚Üí Baud (Binary) | $R_b = R_{baud}$ | One bit per symbol | | Bit Rate ‚Üí Bandwidth (NRZ) | $B \ge R_b/2$ | Theoretical minimum | | Bit Rate ‚Üí Bandwidth (Manchester) | $B \ge R_b$ | Doubled due to mid-bit transition | | Gray Code Adjacency | Hamming distance = 1 | Used in shaft encoders | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Servo Motor Encoders:** Optical encoders output **Gray code** to avoid multi-bit transition errors. - **Automotive ECU Sensors:** 12-bit SAR ADCs sample crankshaft position at rates exceeding **100 kHz**. - **Industrial PLCs (Siemens, Allen-Bradley):** Use **24-bit Sigma-Delta ADCs** for precision weighing scales. - **Audio CD (Red Book):** 16-bit linear PCM at **44.1 kHz** sampling ‚Äî exactly meeting the Nyquist rate for the 20 kHz audio band, with $6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB SQNR. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Quantization Error RMS Value **Problem Setup:** A unipolar quantizer has full-scale range $V_{FS} = 10$ V and word length $N = 10$ bits. The input is a uniformly distributed random signal. Derive the **RMS quantization noise voltage**. **Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the Step Size (Resolution)** The number of quantization levels for a 10-bit ADC is: $$L = 2^N = 2^{10} = 1024$$ The resolution (step size) is therefore: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{10}{1024} = 9.765625 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 9.766\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Define the Quantization Error** The quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the true sample $x$ and the quantized value $x_q$: $$e_q = x - x_q$$ For a mid-tread quantizer with uniform step size $\Delta$, the error is bounded by: $$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Assume a Uniform Error Distribution** The KTU 2024 standard model assumes $e_q$ is **uniformly distributed** over $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. The probability density function is: $$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the Mean-Square Error** The mean-square quantization noise is the second moment of the uniform distribution: $$E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2\, de_q$$ Evaluating the integral: $$E[e_q^2] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right]$$ $$E[e_q^2] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{4} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ This is the canonical KTU formula. **Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the RMS Noise Voltage** $$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{E[e_q^2]} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta^2}{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$ Substituting $\Delta = 9.766$ mV: $$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-3}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} = 2.819 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 2.82\ \text{mV}$$ **Result:** The RMS quantization noise is **2.82 mV** ‚Äî a small but non-negligible error that defines the noise floor of the conversion. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input **Problem Setup:** A 12-bit ADC digitizes a full-scale sinusoid. Derive the **SQNR in dB**. **Step 1 ‚Äî Signal Power** A full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ has RMS value: $$x_{\text{rms}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$$ The signal power (across a 1-ohm load, by convention) is: $$P_{\text{signal}} = x_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{A^2}{2}$$ For a full-scale bipolar ADC with peak-to-peak range $V_{pp} = 2A$, the peak $A$ corresponds to $V_{FS}/2$. Using $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$: $$A = \frac{V_{FS}}{2} = \frac{2^N \cdot \Delta}{2} = 2^{N-1} \cdot \Delta$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Signal Power in Terms of $\Delta$** $$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{(2^{N-1} \cdot \Delta)^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2N-2} \cdot \Delta^2}{2} = 2^{2N-3} \cdot \Delta^2$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Noise Power** $$P_{\text{noise}} = E[e_q^2] = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Form the SQNR Ratio** $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{2^{2N-3} \cdot \Delta^2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2N-3} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2N}}{2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Convert to Decibels** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)$$ Since $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.0206$: $$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76}$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Numerical Evaluation for $N = 12$** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\ \text{dB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for clearly distinguishing between the **6.02 N** (from binary exponentiation) and the **+1.76** (from the $\frac{3}{2}$ factor for sinusoidal input). Using 1.5 or 3/2 explicitly earns full credit; writing "1.76" alone without origin loses a mark. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Minimum Sampling Rate for an Audio System **Problem Setup:** An audio control system uses a microphone with an upper useful frequency of $f_m = 18$ kHz. Determine the **minimum sampling frequency** and the **maximum allowable sampling period** to avoid aliasing. **Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the Nyquist Criterion** The Nyquist rate is: $$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 18{,}000 = 36{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 36\ \text{kHz}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî State the Sampling Frequency Condition** To avoid aliasing, the actual sampling frequency must satisfy: $$f_s \ge f_N = 36\ \text{kHz}$$ Industry convention (with safety margin) is to sample at **44.1 kHz** (CD standard) or **48 kHz** (professional audio). This corresponds to an oversampling factor of: $$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2 f_m} = \frac{44.1}{36} \approx 1.225$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the Maximum Sampling Period** $$T_{\max} = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{36{,}000} = 27.78\ \mu s$$ The system clock must therefore trigger an ADC read at intervals no longer than **27.78 microseconds**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Derivation | Input Parameters | Result | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | Quantization RMS Noise | $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $N=10$ | $e_{q,\text{rms}} = 2.82$ mV | ADC noise floor analysis | | SQNR Formula | N-bit ADC, full-scale sine | $6.02N + 1.76$ dB | Bit-selection trade-off | | Nyquist Frequency | $f_m = 18$ kHz | $f_s \ge 36$ kHz | Sample-rate planning | | Two's Complement Range | $N = 8$ | $[-128, +127]$ | Signed arithmetic | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî The Analog-to-Digital Conversion Pipeline The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full conversion chain from a physical transducer to the digital controller's CPU: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Sensor
Thermocouple, LVDT, Tachogenerator] --> B[Analog Signal
Conditioning
Amplifier, Filter] B --> C[Sampler
Track and Hold
Circuit] C --> D[Quantizer
2 to the Power N
Discrete Levels] D --> E[Encoder
Binary Gray BCD
Two's Complement] E --> F[Digital Controller
Microcontroller DSP FPGA] F --> G[Reconstruction
DAC Zero Order Hold] G --> H[Analog Actuator
Motor Valve Heater] style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff style B fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style D fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style E fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style F fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style G fill:#f57c00,color:#000000 style H fill:#f57c00,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Blue blocks (A, B):** The analog domain ‚Äî physical phenomena and front-end analog signal conditioning. - **Green blocks (C, D, E):** The conversion engine ‚Äî sample, quantize, encode. These three operations define the boundary between analog and digital. - **Red block (F):** The digital control computer where algorithms (PID, state-space, etc.) execute. - **Orange blocks (G, H):** The reconstruction and actuation ‚Äî the return path to the physical plant. --- ### 4.2 Line Coding Waveform Schematic (Block-Level) The diagram below shows the **logical equivalence** of various line codes for the binary data stream `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`. Because Mermaid cannot draw physical waveforms with precision, we use a **state-transition block** to represent each code's defining rule. ```mermaid flowchart TB Data[Input Bit Stream
1 0 1 1 0 0 1] Data --> NRZ[NRZ L
One equals plus V
Zero equals minus V
No Return to Zero] Data --> RZ[RZ
One equals plus V for half bit
Zero equals zero V
Returns to Zero] Data --> Bipolar[Bipolar AMI
One alternates plus V minus V
Zero equals zero V] Data --> Manch[Manchester
One equals High to Low transition
Zero equals Low to High transition] Data --> Gray[Gray Code
Adjacent codes differ by one bit] NRZ --> N1[Used In
TTL Logic
Short Distance] RZ --> N2[Used In
Optical Telegraphy] Bipolar --> N3[Used In
ISDN T1 Lines] Manch --> N4[Used In
Ethernet 10Base T
CAN Bus] Gray --> N5[Used In
Optical Shaft Encoders
Position Sensors] style Data fill:#283593,color:#ffffff style NRZ fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff style RZ fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff style Bipolar fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff style Manch fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff style Gray fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff style N1 fill:#4e342e,color:#ffffff style N2 fill:#4e342e,color:#ffffff style N3 fill:#4e342e,color:#ffffff style N4 fill:#4e342e,color:#ffffff style N5 fill:#4e342e,color:#ffffff ``` **Key Takeaway from the Diagram:** - The **Data** block (dark blue) is the *only* identical input. - The **green** blocks show *how* each code interprets the same data. - The **brown** blocks show *where* each code is physically used in modern engineering systems. --- ### 4.3 Quantization Staircase ‚Äî Discrete Approximation of a Sine Wave ```mermaid flowchart LR Sine[Continuous Sine Wave
x of t equals A sin omega t] --> SAMP[Sampling at Rate fs
Samples xkT] SAMP --> QUANT[Quantization
1024 levels for 10 bit ADC] QUANT --> STAIRCASE[Discrete Digital Levels
Step Size Delta equals 9.77 mV] STAIRCASE --> BINARY[Binary Codeword
10 bit word per sample] style Sine fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff style SAMP fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style QUANT fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff style STAIRCASE fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff style BINARY fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff ``` This block chain visually explains *why* a digital representation of an analog sine wave looks "stair-stepped" ‚Äî the **Quantization** step rounds each sample to the nearest discrete level, and the **Binary Codeword** stage freezes the value into an immutable 10-bit word. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Question]** *Define a digital signal. How does it differ from a continuous-time analog signal in terms of time and amplitude?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A digital signal is a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal represented by a finite set of amplitude levels, most commonly two levels corresponding to logic 1 and logic 0. - **Time domain (1 mark):** An analog signal is defined for all instants of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$, whereas a digital signal is defined only at discrete instants $t = kT$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. - **Amplitude domain (1 mark):** An analog signal can take any value in a continuous range, whereas a digital signal is constrained to one of $2^N$ discrete quantization levels determined by the ADC word length $N$. --- **Question 2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model Question]** *State the Nyquist‚ÄìShannon sampling theorem. What happens if the sampling frequency is less than the Nyquist rate?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1.5 marks):** A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $f_N = 2 f_m$ is the Nyquist rate. - **Consequence of violation (1.5 marks):** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, **aliasing** occurs. High-frequency components of the original signal fold back into the baseband and appear as false low-frequency components in the reconstructed signal, causing irreversible information loss. An anti-aliasing low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \le f_s/2$ is therefore placed before the sampler in practice. --- ### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A ‚Äî Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Explain the three stages of analog-to-digital conversion with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each stage in a digital control system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 10-bit unipolar ADC has a full-scale range of 0 to 5 V. Compute: - (i) the resolution (step size) in mV, - (ii) the maximum quantization error, - (iii) the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a):** The three stages of ADC, in order, are: **1. Sampling (Time Discretization) ‚Äî 2 marks** A Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit captures the instantaneous amplitude of the continuous signal $x(t)$ at uniform intervals $T$. The output is a Pulse-Amplitude Modulated (PAM) signal $x(kT)$. The sampling period must satisfy $T \le 1/(2 f_m)$ per the Nyquist theorem. In a digital control loop, sampling synchronizes the analog plant to the discrete computational cycle of the controller. **2. Quantization (Amplitude Discretization) ‚Äî 2.5 marks** The sampled amplitude is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^N$ discrete levels. For $N=10$, there are 1024 levels, and the resolution is $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$. The rounding operation introduces an irreversible quantization error $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. This error sets the noise floor of the digital controller and directly limits achievable control precision. **3. Coding (Binary Encoding) ‚Äî 2.5 marks** Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $N$-bit binary codeword (Natural Binary, Gray, or Two's Complement for signed values). The output is a serial or parallel bitstream that enters the controller's data bus. The choice of code (e.g., Gray for encoders to avoid transition errors) is critical for signal integrity in industrial environments. **Block Diagram (text representation ‚Äî students should draw the actual block diagram in the exam):** Sensor ‚Üí Sample-and-Hold ‚Üí Quantizer ‚Üí Encoder ‚Üí Digital Controller --- **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b):** **Given:** $N = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V (unipolar, range 0‚Äì5 V) **Part (i) Resolution (Step Size):** ‚Äî 2 marks $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 4.88\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (ii) Maximum Quantization Error:** ‚Äî 2 marks $$\vert e_{q,\max} \vert = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{4.88}{2} = 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (iii) SQNR in dB:** ‚Äî 3 marks Using the standard formula for a full-scale sinusoid: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.20 + 1.76 = 61.96\ \text{dB} \approx 62\ \text{dB}$$ --- #### **Question B ‚Äî Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]** **(a)** With the help of neat timing diagrams, explain any **three** line coding schemes used to represent digital signals. Compare them in terms of bandwidth requirement and DC component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor outputs an analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_m = 250$ Hz. The digital controller samples this signal using a 12-bit ADC with a full-scale range of 0 to 10 V. - (i) Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. - (ii) Compute the resolution and the maximum quantization error. - (iii) Calculate the SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a):** **Three line coding schemes explained:** **1. Unipolar NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) ‚Äî 2 marks** - Binary 1 is represented by a constant +V level for the entire bit duration. - Binary 0 is represented by 0 V. - The signal does not return to zero between consecutive 1s. - **Disadvantage:** Strong DC component; long strings of 1s produce no transitions (no self-clocking). **2. Manchester (Biphase-L) ‚Äî 2.5 marks** - Binary 1 is represented by a **High-to-Low** transition at the middle of the bit period. - Binary 0 is represented by a **Low-to-High** transition at the middle of the bit period. - **Advantages:** No DC component (equal time at +V and ‚àíV); guaranteed mid-bit transition provides self-clocking. - **Disadvantage:** Doubled bandwidth compared to NRZ. - **Used in:** 10-Mbps Ethernet (10Base-T), CAN bus in automobiles. **3. AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion) ‚Äî 2.5 marks** - Binary 1 alternates between +V and ‚àíV for successive 1s. - Binary 0 is represented by 0 V. - **Advantages:** No DC component; easy error detection (a violation of alternation indicates a bit error). - **Used in:** T1 digital telephone trunks (1.544 Mbps), ISDN. **Comparison Table (to be drawn in the exam):** | Property | Unipolar NRZ | Manchester | AMI | |---|---|---|---| | Bandwidth | $R_b/2$ | $R_b$ | $\sim R_b/2$ | | DC Component | High | Zero | Zero | | Self-Clocking | No | Yes | Limited | | Error Detection | No | No | Yes | --- **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b):** **Given:** $f_m = 250$ Hz, $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V **Part (i) Minimum Sampling Rate:** ‚Äî 2 marks By the Nyquist criterion: $$f_{s,\min} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 250 = 500\ \text{Hz}$$ **Part (ii) Resolution and Maximum Quantization Error:** ‚Äî 2 marks Resolution (step size): $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ Maximum quantization error: $$\vert e_{q,\max} \vert = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{2.44}{2} = 1.22\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (iii) SQNR:** ‚Äî 3 marks $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\ \text{dB}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Using $f_s = f_m$ instead of $f_s = 2 f_m$** ‚Äî The Nyquist rate is *twice* the maximum signal frequency, not equal to it. This is the most common error; expect a **2-mark deduction**. > 2. **Forgetting the "1.76" term in SQNR** ‚Äî Writing only $6.02N$ without the $1.76$ dB offset for a sinusoidal input will lose **1 mark**. State the origin: "from the $3/2$ factor for sine-wave statistics." > 3. **Confusing unipolar vs. bipolar full-scale range** ‚Äî For a bipolar ADC (range $-V$ to $+V$), $V_{FS} = 2A$ (peak-to-peak), not just the peak. For a unipolar ADC, $V_{FS}$ is the total span. Mixing these up will miscalculate $\Delta$. > 4. **Mixing up baud rate and bit rate** ‚Äî In binary transmission they are equal, but the examiner may award or deduct marks based on whether you explicitly state the assumption. > 5. **Manchester waveform drawing errors** ‚Äî The transition for `1` and `0` is frequently swapped by students. Remember the IEEE 802.3 convention: `1` = High‚ÜíLow, `0` = Low‚ÜíHigh. (The G.E. Thomas convention reverses this ‚Äî always state the convention you are using.) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 ‚Äî Digital Signals & Coding:** - **Digital Signal:** Discrete in **both** time *and* amplitude. Represented as binary words of length $N$ bits. - **Three Conversion Stages:** **Sampling ‚Üí Quantization ‚Üí Coding** (in this exact order). - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ ‚Äî sampling rate must be at least **twice** the highest signal frequency. - **Aliasing:** Irreversible artifact produced when $f_s < 2 f_m$. Prevented by anti-aliasing low-pass filter *before* the sampler. - **Number of Levels:** $L = 2^N$, where $N$ is the ADC word length. - **Step Size (Resolution):** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$.
