Smaller $\Delta$ means finer resolution. - **Quantization Error:** Bounded by $\pm \Delta/2$ for mid-tread/riser quantizers. - **Mean-Square Quantization Noise:** $\Delta^2/12$ ‚Äî the standard KTU 2024 result for uniform quantization. - **SQNR (dB):** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB ‚Äî every extra bit adds **~6 dB** of dynamic range. - **Two's Complement Range:** $[-2^{N-1}, 2^{N-1}-1]$ ‚Äî the standard signed integer representation in DSPs and microcontrollers. - **Line Codes ‚Äî Quick Identification:** - *NRZ* ‚Üí no mid-bit transition, low bandwidth, has DC. - *RZ* ‚Üí pulse returns to zero, has DC, higher bandwidth. - *AMI* ‚Üí 1s alternate polarity, no DC, good for long-distance TDM. - *Manchester* ‚Üí mandatory mid-bit transition, no DC, **self-clocking**, used in Ethernet and CAN. - *Differential Manchester* ‚Üí always has mid-bit transition + extra transition for 1s. - **Gray Code Property:** Adjacent codes differ in **only one bit** ‚Äî used in optical encoders to prevent false reads during shaft rotation. - **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A **10-bit ADC with 0‚Äì5 V range gives 4.88 mV resolution** and **~62 dB SQNR** ‚Äî adequate for most industrial temperature/pressure control loops. - **Audio Reference:** CD-quality audio = 16-bit / 44.1 kHz ‚Üí **98 dB SQNR**, covering the full 20 kHz human hearing range. ---

topics.title: Data Conversion & Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Conversion & Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In **Digital Control Systems**, the physical (analog) signals from the plant and the digital signals processed by the controller are fundamentally incompatible. **Data Conversion & Quantization** refers to the set of processes that bridge the analog and digital domains:

- **A/D Conversion (Analog-to-Digital):** Transforms a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code $x[k]$ using three sequential operations ‚Äî **Sampling**, **Quantization**, and **Coding (Encoding)**.
- **D/A Conversion (Digital-to-Analog):** Reconstructs a continuous-time signal $\hat{x}(t)$ from the discrete digital sequence $x[k]$ (typically using a **Zero-Order Hold**).
- **Quantization:** The non-linear, many-to-one mapping process that approximates each sampled amplitude $x(kT)$ by the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels, introducing an irreversible **quantization error** $e_q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The chain of conversion in a closed-loop digital control system is **A/D ‚Üí Digital Controller ‚Üí D/A ‚Üí Plant**. Understanding quantization is mandatory because it is the **only inherently non-linear** step and determines the achievable precision of the entire loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **staircase in a parking garage**:
- The car (analog signal) can be at any continuous height in the air.
- The garage has only fixed parking levels ‚Äî Ground, L1, L2‚Ä¶ each separated by a fixed height.
- **Sampling** = looking at the car‚Äôs height only at specific instants (every 5 seconds).
- **Quantization** = forcing the car to "snap" to the nearest parking level. A car that is 0.4 m above L1 jumps down to L1; one that is 0.6 m above jumps up to L2.
- The vertical gap between consecutive levels is the **resolution ($\Delta$)** of the converter. More levels = finer staircase = better accuracy.
- The difference between the car‚Äôs true height and the snapped level is the **quantization error ($e_q$)** ‚Äî a permanent, irreversible loss of information.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance
- **Number of bits:** $n$ (typical: 8, 10, 12, 14, 16).
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The total analog span the converter can handle, in **Volts (V)**.
- **Resolution ($\Delta$):** The step size in **V/LSB** (Volts per Least Significant Bit).
- **Sampling Period:** $T$ in **seconds (s)**.
- **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s = 1/T$ in **Hertz (Hz)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The A/D Conversion Pipeline
The conversion of an analog signal $x(t)$ to a digital word $x[k]$ happens in three logical stages:

1. **Sampling (S/H with Sample-and-Hold):**
   - The continuous signal is multiplied by an impulse train, yielding a discrete-time sequence $x(kT)$.
   - Governed by the **Shannon-Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
   - A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit freezes the amplitude during the A/D conversion window, eliminating aperture error.

2. **Quantization (Non-linear amplitude discretization):**
   - The sampled amplitude is mapped to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
   - The mapping is **non-uniform** (e.g., A-law, $\mu$-law) in audio, but **uniform (linear)** in most control applications.
   - The step size is $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$ (or $\text{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$, depending on convention).

3. **Coding (Encoding):**
   - Each quantization level is assigned a unique $n$-bit binary code (usually **Natural Binary**, or **Offset Binary** for bipolar signals, or **Two's Complement** in DSP controllers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three separate steps?** Sampling discretizes **time**; Quantization discretizes **amplitude**; Coding translates amplitude into a binary word the CPU can manipulate. Only quantization introduces **irreducible error**.

### 2.2 Quantization Error Characteristics
For **uniform mid-tread (or mid-rise) quantizers**, the quantization error $e_q(k)$ is bounded:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Under the standard assumption that $e_q$ is **statistically independent**, **uniformly distributed**, and **zero-mean**:

- **Mean square error (power of quantization noise):**
$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

- **RMS quantization noise:**
$$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sinusoid:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \;\;\text{dB}$$

This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** ‚Äî every additional bit of resolution improves the SQNR by approximately **6 dB**.

### 2.3 D/A Conversion & Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)
The D/A converter outputs a **staircase waveform** $\hat{x}(t)$ that holds each digital value constant over one sampling period $T$. This is mathematically modelled as a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**:

- **Transfer function (s-domain):**
$$G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

- **Transfer function (z-domain):**
$$G_h(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_h(s)\} = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{z}\cdot\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = \frac{T(z^{-1})}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

- **Frequency response:**
$$G_h(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

The ZOH behaves as a **low-pass filter** with a magnitude roll-off and a **half-sample-period phase lag**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | levels | $n$ = number of bits |
| Resolution (mid-rise) | $\Delta = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$ | V/LSB | Use this in KTU problems |
| Resolution (mid-tread, $2^n-1$ intervals) | $\Delta = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ | V/LSB | Less common |
| Quantization error bound | $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$ | V | Uniform quantizer |
| Mean-square quantization noise | $\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | V$^2$ | Statistical model |
| RMS noise | $e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | V | $\sqrt{1/12} = 1/(2\sqrt{3})$ |
| SQNR (full-scale sine) | $6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | "6 dB per bit" rule |
| Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = 6.02\,n$ | dB | Ratio of FSR to $\Delta$ |
| ZOH s-domain | $(1 - e^{-Ts})/s$ | ‚Äî | Approximation of D/A |
| ZOH z-domain | $T\,z^{-1}/(1 - z^{-1})$ | ‚Äî | Used in digital design |
| Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling freq. |
| A/D conversion time | $t_{conv} \leq T$ | s | $T$ is sampling period |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe workaround:** In the table above, the absolute-value / bound expression is rendered via text (e.g., "$-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$") and not as a raw `|x|` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Motor speed control:** A 12-bit encoder/ADC with a 10 V FSR gives $\Delta = 10/4096 \approx 2.44$ mV, sufficient for sub-RPM precision in servo drives.
- **Audio (CD standard):** 16-bit, 44.1 kHz sampling ‚Üí SQNR $\approx 98$ dB, the benchmark for HiFi.
- **Instrumentation & PLCs:** Often use 12- or 14-bit ADCs with $\pm 10$ V bipolar input ranges.
- **Automotive ECUs:** 10- to 12-bit ADCs are standard for sensor fusion; quantization error dominates sensor noise at low signal levels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Quantization Levels and Resolution

**Statement:** Show that an $n$-bit ADC divides the full-scale range into $2^n$ levels and that the resolution is $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Counting binary words.** An $n$-bit binary number can represent exactly $2^n$ distinct combinations, ranging from $0$ to $2^n - 1$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Mapping to amplitude.** Each combination corresponds to one quantization level, so the number of levels is:

$$L = 2^n$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Defining resolution.** Resolution is the smallest distinguishable change in analog input, which equals the spacing between adjacent levels. With $L$ levels covering FSR, the spacing is:

$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{L} = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Worked numeric example (KTU-style).** For an 8-bit ADC with FSR = 10 V:

$$L = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}, \quad \Delta = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}$$

If upgraded to 12 bits:

$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}, \quad \Delta = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Quantization error bound.** Because the true analog value can lie anywhere within one interval of width $\Delta$, the maximum error of the mid-rise quantizer is bounded:

$$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n+1}}$$

> This is the **error budget** the designer must allocate for A/D conversion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean-Square Quantization Noise and SQNR

**Statement:** For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ and statistically uniform error distribution, derive the RMS noise and the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio for a full-scale sinusoidal input.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Error distribution.** Assume $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. The probability density function is $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ over that interval.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the mean (zero by symmetry):**

$$\mu_{e_q} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q}{\Delta}\, de_q = 0$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the mean-square (variance of noise):**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \frac{2(\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî RMS noise voltage:**

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Signal power (full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$).** The RMS signal value is $A/\sqrt{2}$, and the "full-scale" peak is $A = \text{FSR}/2$, so:

$$P_{signal} = \left(\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{\text{FSR}^2}{8}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Noise power in terms of FSR and $n$:** Since $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$:

$$P_{noise} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{1}{12}\cdot \frac{\text{FSR}^2}{2^{2n}}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî SQNR ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{\text{FSR}^2/8}{\text{FSR}^2/(12\cdot 2^{2n})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Convert to decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20n\log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\;\text{dB}$$

> This is the canonical result: each extra bit of resolution buys ~6 dB of dynamic range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: ZOH Transfer Function in the z-Domain

**Statement:** Derive the pulse transfer function of a Zero-Order Hold used at the output of a digital controller.

**Step 1 ‚Äî ZOH impulse response.** The ZOH outputs a rectangular pulse of height equal to the input sample, of width $T$:

$$h(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Laplace transform:**

$$G_h(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert to z-domain.** The factor $1 - e^{-Ts}$ suggests splitting:

$$G_h(s) = \left(1 - e^{-Ts}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

In the z-domain, multiplication by $e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to a one-sample delay, i.e. multiplication by $z^{-1}$. The $1/s$ block is the integrator, whose sampled output is a ramp $kT$ (Z-transform: $Tz/(z-1)^2$).

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compose:**

$$G_h(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot \frac{Tz}{(z - 1)^2}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Simplify.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$G_h(z) = \frac{T(z - 1)}{(z - 1)^2}\cdot z^{-1}\cdot z = \frac{T\,z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{z - 1}$$

Both forms $G_h(z) = Tz^{-1}/(1 - z^{-1})$ and $G_h(z) = T/(z-1)$ are accepted in KTU solutions.

> **Engineering insight:** $T/(z-1)$ is the discrete-time equivalent of the analog integrator $1/s$. The factor $z^{-1}$ represents the inherent one-step delay introduced by any real hold device ‚Äî a critical point in digital controller stability analysis.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: A/D vs D/A Conversion Trade-offs

| **Parameter** | **A/D Converter (ADC)** | **D/A Converter (DAC)** | **KTU Board Perspective** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Continuous ‚Üí Discrete | Discrete ‚Üí Continuous | Both needed in a digital loop |
| Key non-ideality | Quantization error | Aperture / zero-order hold | Both degrade performance |
| Typical bits (control) | 10 ‚Äì 16 | 10 ‚Äì 16 | Match ADC/DAC to avoid bottleneck |
| Conversion time | $t_{conv} \leq T$ | Settling time $\ll T$ | Limits achievable bandwidth |
| Linearity metric | INL, DNL (LSB) | INL, DNL (LSB) | Specified in datasheets |
| Output form | Binary code (e.g., 2's complement) | Staircase waveform (held) | DAC rarely "smooth" |
| Filter needed after | Anti-aliasing (pre) | Reconstruction (post) | Both are analog LPFs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Conversion Block Diagram in a Digital Control Loop
The following Mermaid block diagram shows the canonical signal flow in a KTU-style digital control system, with explicit annotation of every conversion step.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input r-t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF]
    B --> C[Sampler and Hold S/H]
    C --> D[Quantizer Q-n-bit]
    D --> E[Encoder Binary Code]
    E --> F[Digital Controller D-z]
    F --> G[DAC with ZOH]
    G --> H[Plant G-s]
    H --> I[Sensor H-s]
    I --> J[Feedback Summer]
    J --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is a continuous-time LPF with cutoff $f_c < f_s/2$.
- The **S/H** holds the analog value constant during the A/D conversion aperture.
- The **Quantizer** is the **only non-linear** block ‚Äî this is the focus of Module 1.
- The **ZOH** after the DAC introduces a half-sample phase lag and a sinc-shaped magnitude roll-off.

---

### 4.2 Quantization Error Generation Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Continuous Signal x-t] --> S[Sampler: x-kT]
    S --> Q[Quantizer: 2 to the n levels]
    Q --> XQ[Digital Output: x-hat-k]
    S --> SUB((Subtraction Node))
    XQ --> SUB
    SUB --> E[Quantization Error e-q-k]
    Q -.->|non-linear, many-to-one| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea:** $x[k] = Q\{x(kT)\}$, and $e_q[k] = Q\{x(kT)\} - x(kT)$. Because $Q(\cdot)$ is non-linear, a true LTI (linear time-invariant) analysis is impossible. The standard KTU approach is to **replace the quantizer by an additive white noise source** $e_q[k]$ for performance analysis ‚Äî a justified approximation when the input is "busy enough" to traverse many levels.

---

### 4.3 Zero-Order Hold Waveform Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DAC Input Samples: 2.1, 3.5, 1.2, 4.0] --> B[ZOH Staircase Output]
    B --> C[Continuous Plant Input]
    subgraph time_grid
        T0["t=0"] --> T1["t=T"] --> T2["t=2T"] --> T3["t=3T"]
    end
```

The ZOH holds the digital value $x[k]$ constant over the interval $[kT,\,(k+1)T)$, producing a staircase. The vertical jumps at multiples of $T$ are smoothed by the plant's own low-pass dynamics and by an optional **reconstruction filter** at the output.

---

### 4.4 Resolution vs Number of Bits ‚Äî Trade-off Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["n = 8 bits"] --> L0["L = 256"]
    N1["n = 10 bits"] --> L1["L = 1024"]
    N2["n = 12 bits"] --> L2["L = 4096"]
    N3["n = 14 bits"] --> L3["L = 16384"]
    N4["n = 16 bits"] --> L4["L = 65536"]
    L0 --> S0["Delta = 39.06 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L1 --> S1["Delta = 9.77 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L2 --> S2["Delta = 2.44 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L3 --> S3["Delta = 0.61 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L4 --> S4["Delta = 0.153 mV for 10 V FSR"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
*Define quantization error. Derive the expression for the mean-square value of the quantization error of a uniform quantizer.*

**Model Answer:**
Quantization error is the difference between the actual sampled analog value $x(kT)$ and the quantized (digitized) output $x_Q(k)$ of the A/D converter:
$$e_q(k) = x_Q(k) - x(kT)$$
For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$, the error is bounded by $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$. Assuming the error is uniformly distributed in this interval with pdf $p(e_q)=1/\Delta$, the mean is zero and the mean-square value is:
$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q^2}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$
This is the **quantization noise power** in V¬≤.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
*State the Nyquist‚ÄìShannon sampling theorem. Why is an anti-aliasing filter required before the sampler?*

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist‚ÄìShannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(kT)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**.
An **anti-aliasing filter** (analog low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$) is required before the sampler to remove frequency components above $f_s/2$. Without it, **aliasing** occurs: high-frequency components fold back into the baseband and become indistinguishable from the true low-frequency content, causing permanent loss of information.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the process of Analog-to-Digital (A/D) conversion with a neat block diagram. List the three operations involved and state the role of the Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar input range of 0 to 10 V. Calculate:
- (i) the number of quantization levels,
- (ii) the resolution in mV/LSB,
- (iii) the maximum quantization error in mV,
- (iv) the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The A/D conversion process converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal $x(t)$ into a discrete digital word $x[k]$ using three sequential operations:
1. **Sampling:** The signal is sampled at uniform intervals $T$ by a Sample-and-Hold circuit, producing $x(kT)$.
2. **Quantization:** Each sample is mapped to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
3. **Encoding:** Each level is assigned a unique $n$-bit binary code.

**Role of the S/H circuit:** During the time taken by the A/D converter to resolve the digital code (the **aperture time**), the analog input may change. The S/H freezes the input amplitude to a constant value, ensuring a stable, unambiguous conversion and eliminating **aperture error**.

**Model Solution (b):**
Given: $n = 12$ bits, FSR = 10 V.

**(i) Number of quantization levels:**
$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

**(ii) Resolution (step size):**
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.4414 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**(iii) Maximum quantization error:**
$$e_{q,\max} = \pm\frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

**(iv) SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02\times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) used in D/A conversion. Derive its transfer function in the s-domain and z-domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC quantizes a full-scale sine wave of peak amplitude 5 V. Compute:
- (i) the RMS signal voltage,
- (ii) the RMS quantization noise voltage,
- (iii) the SQNR in dB (verify using the formula and by direct ratio calculation). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The ZOH is a device that holds the digital input constant for the duration of one sampling period $T$, producing a staircase output.

**Block diagram (textual):**
Digital word $x[k]$ ‚Üí DAC latch ‚Üí ZOH circuit ‚Üí Staircase output $\hat{x}(t)$ ‚Üí Reconstruction filter ‚Üí Plant input.

**Derivation of s-domain transfer function:**
The ZOH impulse response is $h(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step.
$$G_h(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Derivation of z-domain transfer function:**
Factor as $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-Ts})\cdot(1/s^2)$. The factor $1/s^2$ corresponds to a ramp, whose Z-transform is $Tz/(z-1)^2$, and $1 - e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to $1 - z^{-1}$. Therefore:
$$G_h(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot\frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{T\,z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{z - 1}$$

**Model Solution (b):**
Given: $n = 10$ bits, $A = 5$ V (peak), FSR $\geq 2A = 10$ V (take FSR = 10 V).

**(i) RMS signal voltage (full-scale sine):**
$$V_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 3.5355 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) RMS quantization noise:**
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{1024} \approx 9.7656 \text{ mV}$$
$$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{9.7656 \text{ mV}}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 2.8192 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR by direct ratio:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{e_{q,rms}^2} = \left(\frac{3.5355}{0.0028192}\right)^2 \approx 1.572 \times 10^6$$
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.572\times 10^6) \approx 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification by formula:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02\times 10 + 1.76 = 60.20 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

Both methods agree to within rounding, confirming the result.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Resolution formula confusion:** Some students write $\Delta = \text{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$ and some write $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$. The KTU board uses the **mid-rise (or "no missing code" standard)** convention $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$. Always state the convention.
> 2. **Confusing peak with RMS:** A full-scale sine has peak $A$ and RMS $A/\sqrt{2}$. The signal power used in the SQNR formula assumes RMS, not peak. Losing 3 dB is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting units in resolution:** Report $\Delta$ in **mV/LSB**, not raw volts. The board specifically checks for unit consistency.
> 4. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter mention:** Any block diagram of an A/D pipeline without an anti-aliasing filter is marked incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong quantizer model:** Students often treat the quantizer as linear. Always note that it is **non-linear** and justify the use of the statistical noise model.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **A/D pipeline:** Sampling ‚Üí Quantization ‚Üí Encoding (S/H is part of sampling).
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$ for an $n$-bit converter.
- **Resolution (mid-rise):** $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$, in **V/LSB**.
- **Quantization error bound:** $\pm \Delta/2$.
- **Mean-square quantization noise:** $\Delta^2/12$ (V¬≤).
- **RMS quantization noise:** $\Delta/(2\sqrt{3})$ (V).
- **SQNR rule of thumb:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB (for full-scale sine) ‚Äî "**6 dB per bit**."
- **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Analog LPF with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ is mandatory before the sampler.
- **D/A + ZOH:** Output is a staircase; the ZOH transfer function in z-domain is $G_h(z) = T/(z-1)$.
- **Inherent ZOH delay:** $T/2$ second phase lag ‚Äî must be accounted for in stability margins (gain/phase margin shrinks).
- **Statistical noise assumption:** Quantization error is treated as zero-mean, white, uniform; this is valid only when the input is sufficiently "active" (e.g., not stuck at a single level).
- **Dynamic range:** $\text{DR} = 6.02\,n$ dB (ratio of FSR to $\Delta$).
- **Conversion time constraint:** $t_{conv} < T$ ‚Äî the ADC must resolve the digital word within one sampling period.
- **Encoder types:** Natural Binary (unipolar), Offset Binary (bipolar), Two's Complement (DSP).
- **Why ZOH is preferred over First-Order Hold (FOH):** Simpler, cheaper, more stable, and sufficient for most control loops. FOH gives better reconstruction at the cost of noise amplification.
- **Aperture error:** The error introduced if the S/H is absent and the input changes during conversion. Always include an S/H in high-speed or high-accuracy systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision mnemonic ‚Äî "Q-S-N-R":** **Q**uantization error squared, divided by **S**ample size gives **N**oise power; **R**MS is its square root. This single sentence gets you through 70% of Module 1 numerical questions on the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample and Hold Devices, Mathematical Modelling of the Sampling Process, Zero Order Hold & First Order Hold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Devices, Mathematical Modelling of the Sampling Process, Zero Order Hold & First Order Hold

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sample and Hold Devices (S/H)

A **Sample and Hold (S/H) device** is a critical interface circuit in digital control systems that captures (samples) the instantaneous value of a continuous-time analog signal at specific, discrete time instants and then maintains (holds) that captured value constant for a finite duration until the next sample arrives.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this device is mathematically modeled as a **Sampler** followed by a **Hold Device (Hold Circuit)**. It acts as the bridge between the continuous-time (analog) plant and the discrete-time (digital) controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Sample and Hold is a system whose output $y(t)$ is equal to the input $r(t)$ during the sampling instants and remains constant at the last sampled value during the interval between two consecutive sampling instants.

**Components of a Real-Time S/H Device:**
- **Analog Switch (Sampling Switch):** A MOSFET or JFET switch that closes briefly at the sampling instant.
- **Hold Capacitor:** Stores the analog voltage value when the switch opens.
- **Buffer Amplifier:** A high input-impedance unity-gain amplifier to prevent the capacitor from discharging through the load.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Snapshot Camera"

Imagine a wildlife photographer who wants to document the slow growth of a plant.
- The plant grows continuously (analog signal $r(t)$).
- The photographer takes a **snapshot (sample)** every 24 hours.
- Until the next day, the photographer **pins the photo (holds it)** on the board so it can be analyzed.

The S/H device behaves exactly like this:
- The **sampler** is the camera shutter clicking at intervals $T$.
- The **hold circuit** is the pinned photo remaining flat (or predicted) until the next click.
- The interval between two clicks is called the **Sampling Period ($T$)**, and its reciprocal is the **Sampling Frequency ($f_s = 1/T$)**.

### 1.3 Types of Hold Devices

| Hold Type | Behavior Between Samples | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** | Output remains **constant** (flat) at the last sampled value | Most common; DAC in microcontrollers |
| **First-Order Hold (FOH)** | Output **extrapolates linearly** between the current and previous samples | Signal reconstruction, smoother tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist‚ÄìShannon Criterion):** To reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ **must be at least twice** the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ present in the signal. Mathematically: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Sampling Process

The mathematical modeling of the sampling process treats the continuous signal as a sequence of weighted **Dirac Delta Impulses** separated by the sampling period $T$.

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**

1. **Continuous Input:** The analog plant generates a smooth, continuous signal $r(t)$ for all $t \geq 0$.
2. **Ideal Sampling:** An ideal sampler modulates the signal $r(t)$ with a unit impulse train $\delta_T(t)$.
3. **Impulse Train Output:** The sampled output $r^*(t)$ is mathematically a series of impulses whose areas (weights) equal the values of $r(t)$ at sampling instants.
4. **Hold Mechanism:** The ZOH or FOH reconstructs a piecewise-constant or piecewise-linear signal $h(t)$ from the impulses.

### 2.2 Mathematical Expression of the Ideal Sampler

The ideal impulse train sampler is expressed as:

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t)$$

where the impulse train is given by:

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

Therefore, the sampled signal expands to:

$$r^*(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$$

### 2.3 Laplace Transform of the Sampled Signal

Taking the Laplace Transform of the impulse-modulated signal gives the **starred transform** notation:

$$R^*(s) = \mathcal{L}\{r^*(t)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot e^{-kTs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starred Notation Convention (KTU standard):** Any function $F(s)$ that has been through an ideal sampler is denoted as $F^*(s)$. The output of the sampler is always a discrete-data sequence expressed as a function of $e^{Ts}$.

### 2.4 Transfer Function of Zero Order Hold (ZOH)

The ZOH takes the value of the signal at the sampling instant $kT$ and holds it constant until $(k+1)T$.

**Impulse Response of ZOH:**

$$g_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

where $u(t)$ is the standard unit step function.

**Transfer Function Derivation (Laplace Domain):**

$$G_{ZOH}(s) = \mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}}$$

### 2.5 Transfer Function of First Order Hold (FOH)

The FOH extrapolates linearly between the current sample $r(kT)$ and the previous sample $r((k-1)T)$.

**Impulse Response of FOH:**

$$g_{FOH}(t) = u(t) + \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right) [u(t) - u(t-T)]$$

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

$$G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{Ts^2} (1 - Ts - e^{-Ts})$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \delta(t - kT)$ | Ideal Sampled Signal | Modulation model |
| $R^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) e^{-kTs}$ | Laplace Transform of Sampled Signal | Stability & analysis |
| $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling Theorem | Anti-aliasing design |
| $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | ZOH Transfer Function | D/A reconstruction |
| $G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{1 + e^{-Ts}}{Ts^2} - \frac{1}{s}$ | FOH Transfer Function | Linear extrapolation |
| $G_h(s) G_p(s) = G_{ZOH}(s) G_p(s)$ | Cascaded Hold-Plant Model | Z-domain analysis |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microcontroller DACs:** Every digital-to-analog converter chip (e.g., in Arduino, STM32) embeds a ZOH as its core reconstruction mechanism.
- **Audio Playback:** CD and MP3 players use oversampling with ZOH reconstruction filters.
- **Industrial PLCs:** PLC analog output modules use S/H circuits to drive 4‚Äì20 mA control valves.
- **Radar & Sonar:** Beamforming arrays rely on precise sample-hold circuits for phase coherence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Laplace Transform of a Sampled Signal

We begin with the continuous input $r(t)$ and the ideal impulse train $\delta_T(t)$.

**Step 1: Define the impulse train.**

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

This represents an infinite series of unit impulses occurring every $T$ seconds.

**Step 2: Multiply with the input signal.**

The sampled signal is the product of $r(t)$ and the impulse train.

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = r(t) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 3: Apply the sampling property of the Dirac delta.**

The sampling property states: $r(t) \cdot \delta(t - kT) = r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$.

$$\therefore r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 4: Take the Laplace Transform term-by-term.**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} = e^{-kTs}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R^*(s) &= \mathcal{L}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)\right\} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot e^{-kTs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \, e^{-kTs}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Zero Order Hold Transfer Function

The ZOH holds the input value constant for the entire sampling period.

**Step 1: Express the impulse response of ZOH.**

A ZOH outputs a unit step of magnitude 1, then drops back to 0 after $T$ seconds. This is the difference between two delayed step functions:

$$g_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

**Step 2: Take the Laplace Transform.**

Recall: $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$ and $\mathcal{L}\{u(t - T)\} = \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{ZOH}(s) &= \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} - \mathcal{L}\{u(t-T)\} \\
&= \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} \\
&= \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: First Order Hold Transfer Function

The FOH linearly extrapolates between the current and previous samples using a triangular-like impulse response.

**Step 1: Construct the impulse response of FOH.**

The FOH response consists of a unit step plus a ramp that is turned off after $T$.

$$g_{FOH}(t) = u(t) + \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right)[u(t) - u(t - T)]$$

**Step 2: Split into Laplace-Transformable components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
g_{FOH}(t) &= u(t) + \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right)u(t) - \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right)u(t - T) \\
&= \frac{t}{T} u(t) - \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right) u(t - T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Take the Laplace Transform of each term.**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s^2}$ and the second shifting theorem:

$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t - T) u(t - T)\} = e^{-Ts} F(s)$$

We obtain:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{FOH}(s) &= \frac{1}{T s^2} - e^{-Ts} \left( \frac{1}{T s^2} - \frac{1}{s} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{T s^2} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{T s^2} + \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into a compact form.**

$$\boxed{G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{T s^2} \cdot \left[\frac{T s}{1 - e^{-Ts}}\right] = \frac{1 + e^{-Ts}}{Ts^2} - \frac{1}{s}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Zero Order Hold | First Order Hold |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reconstruction Shape | Step (flat) | Ramp (linear) |
| Transfer Function | $\frac{1-e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | $\frac{1+e^{-Ts}}{Ts^2} - \frac{1}{s}$ |
| Phase Lag | Lower | Higher |
| Hardware Complexity | Simple (capacitor + buffer) | Complex (requires previous sample memory) |
| Industrial Adoption | Very High (DAC standard) | Low (specialty applications) |
| Frequency Response | Low-pass filter behavior | Smoother but introduces overshoot |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Sample and Hold in a Digital Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Continuous Reference r of t"]
    nodeS["Sampler Ideal Impulse Train"]
    nodeSH["Sample and Hold Block ZOH or FOH"]
    nodeC["Digital Controller D z"]
    nodeP["Continuous Plant Gp of s"]
    nodeY["Plant Output y of t"]
    nodeF["Feedback Sensor H of s"]

    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeSH
    nodeSH --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeR
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Internal Structure of a Sample and Hold IC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput["Analog Input r of t"]
    nodeSwitch["Analog Switch MOSFET"]
    nodeCap["Hold Capacitor CH"]
    nodeBuffer["Unity Gain Buffer Amplifier"]
    nodeOut["Reconstructed Output h of t"]
    nodeCtrl["Control Logic Sampling Clock fs"]

    nodeCtrl --> nodeSwitch
    nodeInput --> nodeSwitch
    nodeSwitch --> nodeCap
    nodeCap --> nodeBuffer
    nodeBuffer --> nodeOut
```

### 4.3 Signal Reconstruction Comparison (ZOH vs FOH)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOrig["Continuous Signal r of t Smooth Curve"]
    nodeSamples["Sampled Values r kT Dots"]
    nodeZOH["ZOH Output Step Reconstruction Flat Plateaus"]
    nodeFOH["FOH Output Linear Reconstruction Ramps"]

    nodeOrig --> nodeSamples
    nodeSamples --> nodeZOH
    nodeSamples --> nodeFOH
```

### 4.4 Frequency Domain Interpretation of the Hold

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeImp["Sampler Output R star of s Impulse Train Spectrum"]
    nodeHold["Hold Block Gh of s Low Pass Filtering Action"]
    nodeCont["Smooth Continuous Output Y of s"]

    nodeImp --> nodeHold
    nodeHold --> nodeCont
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** In the frequency domain, the impulse train sampler replicates the signal spectrum at multiples of $f_s$. The hold device acts as a **crude low-pass filter**, attenuating the high-frequency replicas and retaining only the baseband for the plant. This is precisely why a ZOH physically behaves like a low-pass filter with cutoff frequency around $0.443 / T$ Hz.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
Define a Sample and Hold device. Why is it necessary in a digital control system?

**Model Answer:**
A Sample and Hold (S/H) device is a circuit that captures the instantaneous value of a continuous-time analog signal at discrete sampling instants and maintains that value constant until the next sample arrives.

**Necessity:**
- The ADC requires a **finite aperture time** to convert an analog voltage to a digital code.
- During conversion, the input must not vary, otherwise large errors occur (aperture error).
- The S/H "freezes" the signal, allowing the ADC to perform accurate conversion.
- It also acts as the **D/A reconstruction stage** at the controller output, providing a piecewise-constant signal to drive the continuous plant.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks necessity)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
State the Sampling Theorem. What happens if the theorem is violated?

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** A band-limited continuous signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Violation Effect:** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the replicated spectra in the frequency domain overlap. This phenomenon is called **aliasing** (or fold-over), where high-frequency components masquerade as low-frequency components, causing irreversible loss of information and unstable system behavior.

*(3 marks: 2 marks statement + 1 mark violation effect)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a Zero Order Hold (ZOH). Explain its significance in digital control systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Impulse Response Definition:**
A ZOH holds its input value constant for one sampling period $T$ after an impulse is received. Its impulse response is:
$$g_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Laplace Transform:**
$$G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Significance:**
- It is the **most widely used** reconstruction device in digital-to-analog converters.
- It introduces a **half-sample delay** ($T/2$) in the closed-loop response.
- It acts as a **low-pass filter** that suppresses high-frequency spectral images generated by sampling.
- It allows the entire sampled-data system to be modeled as a continuous-time equivalent for initial design.

*(7 marks: 3 derivation + 2 transfer function + 2 significance)*

**(b)** A continuous signal $r(t) = 5 \sin(10t)$ is sampled with sampling period $T = 0.05$ s. Determine the sampled signal expression and check whether the Sampling Theorem is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify maximum frequency:**
$$\omega = 10 \text{ rad/s} \implies f_{max} = \frac{10}{2\pi} \approx 1.591 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute sampling frequency:**
$$f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Check Nyquist Criterion:**
$$f_s = 20 \text{ Hz} \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 1.591 = 3.183 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

The Sampling Theorem is satisfied.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Sampled Signal:**
$$r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 5 \sin(10 kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 5 \sin(0.5 k) \cdot \delta(t - 0.05 k)$$

*(7 marks: 3 frequency analysis + 2 criterion check + 2 sampled expression)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat impulse response sketch, derive the transfer function of a First Order Hold (FOH). Compare it with the Zero Order Hold. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Impulse Response of FOH:**
The FOH extrapolates linearly between consecutive samples, producing a triangular-like response:
$$g_{FOH}(t) = u(t) + \left(\frac{t}{T} - 1\right)[u(t) - u(t - T)]$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Laplace Transform:**
$$G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{Ts^2}(1 - Ts - e^{-Ts})$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Simplified Form:**
$$G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{1 + e^{-Ts}}{Ts^2} - \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Comparison Table:**

| Feature | ZOH | FOH |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reconstruction | Step-flat | Linear ramps |
| Smoothness | Less smooth | Smoother |
| Complexity | Simple | Requires memory of $r((k-1)T)$ |
| Phase Lag | Lower | Higher |

*(7 marks: 3 derivation + 2 transfer function + 2 comparison)*

**(b)** Explain the mathematical modelling of the sampling process. Obtain the expression $R^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) e^{-kTs}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define Impulse Train:**
$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Modulation with Input Signal:**
$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Laplace Transform:**
$$R^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - kT)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) e^{-kTs}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Importance:**
This expression converts continuous-time analysis into a **discrete-data sequence**, enabling $z$-transform analysis with $z = e^{Ts}$.

*(7 marks: 2 impulse train + 2 modulation + 2 transform + 1 importance)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgot the negative sign in exponent:** Students often write $e^{+kTs}$ instead of $e^{-kTs}$ ‚Äî this changes causality entirely. (Loses 1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing ZOH and FOH impulse responses:** ZOH is a *step* response; FOH is a *triangular/ramp* response. Drawing the wrong waveform loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "why":** Just stating $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1-e^{-Ts}}{s}$ without showing $\mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$ loses 2 marks in derivation.
> 4. **Not verifying units:** Sampling frequency $f_s$ is in **Hz**, while $f_{max}$ must be in **Hz**, not rad/s. Many students mix rad/s and Hz and lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Sampler:** Modulates $r(t)$ with $\delta_T(t)$ to produce $r^*(t)$.
- **Starred Transform:** $F^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} f(kT) e^{-kTs}$ represents discrete data in $s$-domain.
- **ZOH:** Most common; piecewise-constant; $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$.
- **FOH:** Linear extrapolation; $G_{FOH}(s) = \frac{1 + e^{-Ts}}{Ts^2} - \frac{1}{s}$.
- **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ ‚Äî non-negotiable for distortion-free reconstruction.
- **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$; cannot be reversed.
- **Half-Sample Delay:** ZOH effectively introduces $T/2$ delay in the loop.
- **Hold as Low-Pass Filter:** Frequency cutoff $\approx 0.443 / T$ Hz for ZOH.
- **Memory Trick:** $e^{-Ts} \leftrightarrow$ unit delay of $T$ seconds in time domain.
- **Causality Rule:** All practical hold devices are causal ‚Äî output depends only on past and current inputs.
- **Industrial Application:** Every DAC output stage = ZOH. Every ADC input stage = Track-and-Hold (variant of S/H).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Transfer Function, Z-Transform Analysis of Closed Loop and Open Loop Systems, Modified Z-Transfer Function, Steady State Error Analysis of Digital Systems, Examples on Static Error Coefficients, Bilinear Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pulse Transfer Function, Z-Transform Analysis of Closed Loop and Open Loop Systems, Modified Z-Transfer Function, Steady State Error Analysis of Digital Systems, Examples on Static Error Coefficients, Bilinear Transformation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Pulse Transfer Function (PTF) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Pulse Transfer Function):** > The **Pulse Transfer Function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) sampled-data system is defined as the ratio of the **Z-transform of the sampled output sequence** to the **Z-transform of the sampled input sequence**, assuming all initial conditions are zero. > > $$G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \mathcal{Z}\left[\text{sampled output}\right] / \mathcal{Z}\left[\text{sampled input}\right]$$ It characterizes the dynamic behavior of a **sampled system** in the Z-domain, exactly the way a transfer function $G(s)$ characterizes a continuous system in the s-domain. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** > Imagine a **photographer taking snapshots** of a moving car every $T$ seconds. Each snapshot captures the car's position at that instant, but you lose all the motion in between. The Pulse Transfer Function is essentially a *mathematical camera* ‚Äî it takes a continuous-time signal and produces a sequence of "discrete frames" (samples) you can manipulate algebraically using Z-transforms. Just as a camera's *frame rate* ($1/T$) determines smoothness, the **sampling period $T$** fundamentally shapes the behavior of the pulse transfer function. --- ### 1.2 Z-Transform Analysis of Open-Loop & Closed-Loop Sampled Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open-Loop Sampled System:** > A system where the **sampler and zero-order hold (ZOH)** are placed before the continuous plant $G(s)$. The Z-domain representation is obtained as $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right]$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed-Loop Sampled System (Unity Feedback with Digital Controller):** > A standard digital control loop has a digital controller $D(z)$ in the forward path and a continuous plant preceded by a ZOH in feedback. The closed-loop pulse transfer function is: > > $$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z) \, G(z)}{1 + D(z) \, G(z)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Think of an open-loop sampled system like **firing a sprinkler** at regular intervals ‚Äî water shoots out at fixed times, but nothing checks if the garden is wet. The closed-loop system is like a **smart irrigation system** that checks soil moisture (feedback) and adjusts the next water pulse accordingly. --- ### 1.3 Modified Z-Transfer Function > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Modified Z-Transform):** > The Modified Z-Transform introduces a **fractional time delay** $mT$ (where $0 \leq m \leq 1$) into the sampling process. It is used to obtain the response of a sampled system at **time instants that are not integer multiples of the sampling period**. > > $$G(z, m) = \mathcal{Z}_m[G(s)] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT + mT) \, z^{-k}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** If the standard Z-transform gives us snapshots taken at $0, T, 2T, \dots$, the modified Z-transform is like a camera that can be **shifted by a fraction $mT$** within each interval, capturing intermediate moments. This is critical when we need to model **time delays** or **non-integer delays** that occur within the sampling period. --- ### 1.4 Steady-State Error Analysis of Digital Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** > The difference between the desired reference input $r(t)$ and the actual output $c(t)$ as $t \to \infty$. In digital systems, this is evaluated using the **Modified Final Value Theorem**: > > $$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) \, E(z)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Steady-state error is the **"lingering gap"** between where the system *should* be and where it *actually ends up*. For a temperature controller, even after settling, the room may be 0.5¬∞C off from the setpoint ‚Äî that 0.5¬∞C is your steady-state error. --- ### 1.5 Static Error Coefficients ($K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Static Error Coefficients** quantify the system's ability to track standard test inputs: > > | Coefficient | Test Input | Formula | > |---|---|---| > | **Position Constant $K_p$** | Step $r(t) = A$ | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | > | **Velocity Constant $K_v$** | Ramp $r(t) = At$ | $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1 - z^{-1})}{T} G(z)$ | > | **Acceleration Constant $K_a$** | Parabolic $r(t) = \frac{1}{2}At^2$ | $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1 - z^{-1})^2}{T^2} G(z)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** > - $K_p$ measures how well the system holds a **constant position** (like an elevator stopping at a floor). > - $K_v$ measures how well it tracks a **constantly moving target** (like a radar following a plane at constant velocity). > - $K_a$ measures tracking of **accelerating targets** (like a missile chasing a maneuvering jet). --- ### 1.6 Bilinear Transformation (Tustin's Approximation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bilinear Transformation (Tustin Approximation):** > A method to convert continuous-time transfer functions $G(s)$ into discrete-time equivalents $G(z)$ by substituting: > > $$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** The bilinear transformation is like a **map projection** that converts the entire infinite s-plane (left half-plane) into the unit circle in the z-plane. Unlike simple ZOH-based mapping, it **preserves stability** ‚Äî stable poles in s-domain always map to *inside* the unit circle. It does, however, **warp frequencies** (frequency warping / pre-warping is used to compensate near critical frequencies). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation: From s-Domain to z-Domain A **sampler** with sampling period $T$ converts a continuous signal $r(t)$ into an impulse train $r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$. The Z-transform of $r^*(t)$ is: $$R(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot z^{-k}$$ The mapping between s-plane and z-plane is: $$z = e^{sT}$$ | s-Plane Location | z-Plane Location | |---|---| | Left half-plane ($\sigma < 0$) | Inside unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) | | Imaginary axis ($\sigma = 0$) | On unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) | | Right half-plane ($\sigma > 0$) | Outside unit circle ($\vert z \vert > 1$) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** > - **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** IIR/FIR filter design from analog prototypes. > - **Robotics:** Real-time trajectory tracking with discrete feedback. > - **Aerospace:** Missile guidance, autopilot systems in aircraft. > - **Process Control:** Chemical plant temperature/pressure control with PLCs. --- ### 2.2 Pulse Transfer Function Derivation Logic For a system with **zero-order hold (ZOH)** followed by plant $G(s)$: **Step 1:** Output of ZOH in Laplace domain: $$Y(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot G(s) \cdot R^*(s)$$ **Step 2:** Take Z-transform (using the property $\mathcal{Z}[F(s) \cdot e^{-sT}] = z^{-1} F(z)$): $$Y(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right] \cdot R(z)$$ **Step 3:** Define: $$G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right] = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot G_z(z)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** The term $\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right]$ is *NOT* the same as $\frac{G(z)}{z}$. The student must **first divide $G(s)$ by $s$**, *then* take the Z-transform, and *finally* multiply by $(1 - z^{-1})$. This is the most common error in KTU exams. --- ### 2.3 Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function Forms For a **unity feedback** system with digital controller $D(z)$ and plant-with-ZOH $G(z)$: $$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z) G(z)}{1 + D(z) G(z)}$$ For a **non-unity feedback** system with $H(s)$ continuous and sampler after it: $$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$$ where $GH(z) = \mathcal{Z}[G(s) H(s)]$ ‚Äî the sampler is *outside* the loop, so we Z-transform the product first. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** $G(z)H(z) \neq GH(z)$. You can only split the Z-transform when the components are separated by a sampler. --- ### 2.4 Modified Z-Transform Procedure For a system with **transport delay** $\tau = (1 - m)T$ where $0 \leq m \leq 1$: $$G(z, m) = z^{-1} \cdot \mathcal{Z}\left[e^{mTs} \cdot G(s)\right] = z^{-1} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT + mT) \, z^{-k}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property:** When $m = 0$: $G(z, 0) = z^{-1} G(z)$ (one full sample delay). When $m = 1$: $G(z, 1) = G(z)$. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Conditions** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | s-to-z mapping | $z = e^{sT}$ | Stable iff $\vert z \vert < 1$ | | 2 | ZOH + Plant PTF | $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right]$ | Standard sampled-data form | | 3 | Closed-Loop PTF (Unity) | $T(z) = \frac{D(z)G(z)}{1 + D(z)G(z)}$ | Digital controller + plant | | 4 | Closed-Loop PTF (with continuous H) | $T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$ | $GH(z) = \mathcal{Z}[G(s)H(s)]$ | | 5 | Error PTF | $E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + D(z)G(z)}$ | Unity feedback form | | 6 | Modified Z-TF | $G(z, m) = z^{-1}\mathcal{Z}[e^{mTs}G(s)]$ | $0 \leq m \leq 1$ | | 7 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1}(1 - z^{-1})E(z)$ | Apply when no poles on $\vert z \vert = 1$ except $z=1$ | | 8 | Position Constant | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | For step input | | 9 | Velocity Constant | $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1-z^{-1})}{T}G(z)$ | For ramp input | | 10 | Acceleration Constant | $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1-z^{-1})^2}{T^2}G(z)$ | For parabolic input | | 11 | Bilinear (Tustin) | $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ | Preserves stability | | 12 | Pre-warping | $\Omega_a = \frac{2}{T}\tan\left(\frac{\omega_a T}{2}\right)$ | Adjust $\omega_a$ before Tustin | | 13 | Final Value Theorem (Z) | $\lim_{k \to \infty} f(kT) = \lim_{z \to 1}(1-z^{-1})F(z)$ | Poles of $(1-z^{-1})F(z)$ inside unit circle | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Transfer Function of ZOH + First-Order Plant **Problem Setup:** Let $G(s) = \frac{1}{s+a}$ with ZOH. Find $G(z)$. **Step 1:** Compute $\frac{G(s)}{s}$: $$\frac{G(s)}{s} = \frac{1}{s(s+a)}$$ **Step 2:** Partial Fraction Expansion: $$\frac{1}{s(s+a)} = \frac{1/a}{s} - \frac{1/a}{s+a}$$ **Step 3:** Take the Z-transform (using standard Z-transform pairs): $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right] = \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z}{z-1} - \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z}{z - e^{-aT}}$$ **Step 4:** Combine into common denominator: $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right] = \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z(z - e^{-aT}) - z(z - 1)}{(z-1)(z - e^{-aT})}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$= \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z^2 - z e^{-aT} - z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z - e^{-aT})}$$ $$= \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z(1 - e^{-aT})}{(z-1)(z - e^{-aT})}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply by $(1 - z^{-1}) = \frac{z-1}{z}$: $$G(z) = \frac{z-1}{z} \cdot \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{z(1 - e^{-aT})}{(z-1)(z - e^{-aT})}$$ **Step 6:** Simplify (cancel $z$ and $(z-1)$): $$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{1 - e^{-aT}}{a} \cdot \frac{1}{z - e^{-aT}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic:** This is the discrete equivalent of a first-order system with time constant $1/a$. The pole at $z = e^{-aT}$ lies inside the unit circle (since $e^{-aT} < 1$ for $a > 0$), confirming stability. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error for Unit Step Input **System:** $D(z) = K$ (proportional controller), $G(z)$ = plant PTF, unity feedback. **Step 1:** Write the error PTF: $$E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + D(z) G(z)} = \frac{R(z)}{1 + K G(z)}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $R(z) = \mathcal{Z}[u(t)] = \frac{z}{z-1} = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$: $$E(z) = \frac{1/(1-z^{-1})}{1 + K G(z)}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the Modified Final Value Theorem: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) E(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \frac{1/(1-z^{-1})}{1 + K G(z)}$$ **Step 4:** The $(1 - z^{-1})$ cancels: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{1}{1 + K G(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + K \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)}$$ **Step 5:** Define $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} K G(z)$: $$\boxed{e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic:** A **Type-0 system** has finite position error. To make $e_{ss} = 0$, we need $K_p = \infty$, which means $G(z)$ must have a pole at $z = 1$ (i.e., the system must be at least **Type-1**). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp Input **Input:** $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$, so $R(z) = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$. **Step 1:** Write $E(z)$: $$E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + KG(z)} = \frac{Tz/(z-1)^2}{1 + K G(z)}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Modified Final Value Theorem: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \frac{Tz/(z-1)^2}{1 + KG(z)}$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $(1 - z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{z-1}{z} \cdot \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2 [1 + KG(z)]}$$ $$= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{T}{(z-1)[1 + KG(z)]}$$ **Step 4:** For the limit to be finite, $(z-1)$ must be present in $[1 + KG(z)]$ ‚Äî i.e., system must be **Type-1** or higher (so that $KG(z)$ has a pole at $z=1$). **Step 5:** Suppose $KG(z) = \frac{K_1(z)}{(z-1) \cdot (\text{other finite terms})}$, then: $$e_{ss} = \frac{T}{K_1(1) \cdot (\text{other finite terms evaluated at } z=1)} = \frac{1}{K_v}$$ where: $$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{T} KG(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1 - z^{-1})}{T} KG(z)$$ $$\boxed{e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Bilinear Transformation Application **Problem:** Convert $G(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$ to discrete form using Tustin's approximation with $T = 1$ s. **Step 1:** Substitute $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$. With $T = 1$: $$s = 2 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$ **Step 2:** Plug into $G(s)$: $$G(z) = \frac{1}{2 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} + 1}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the denominator: $$2 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} + 1 = \frac{2(z-1) + (z+1)}{z+1} = \frac{2z - 2 + z + 1}{z+1} = \frac{3z - 1}{z+1}$$ **Step 4:** Invert: $$G(z) = \frac{z+1}{3z - 1}$$ **Step 5:** Normalize to monic denominator by dividing numerator and denominator by $3$: $$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{(z+1)/3}{z - 1/3} = \frac{0.3333(z+1)}{z - 0.3333}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic:** The continuous pole at $s = -1$ (stable) maps to $z = 1/3$ in the discrete domain (inside unit circle, stable). The bilinear transformation guarantees stability preservation ‚Äî a critical KTU property to remember. --- ### 3.5 Derivation: Static Error Coefficients ‚Äî Numerical Example **System:** $G(z) = \frac{0.5(z + 0.8)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.6)}$, find $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$. **Step 1:** Position Constant: $$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) = \frac{0.5(1 + 0.8)}{(1 - 1)(1 - 0.6)}$$ Denominator is zero (pole at $z=1$), so: $$K_p = \infty \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{ss}(\text{step}) = 0$$ **Step 2:** Velocity Constant: $$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1 - z^{-1})}{T} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{Tz} \cdot \frac{0.5(z+0.8)}{(z-1)(z-0.6)}$$ $$= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.5(z+0.8)}{Tz(z-0.6)} = \frac{0.5 \cdot 1.8}{T \cdot 1 \cdot 0.4} = \frac{0.9}{0.4T} = \frac{2.25}{T}$$ **Step 3:** Acceleration Constant: $$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1 - z^{-1})^2}{T^2} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z^2} \cdot \frac{0.5(z+0.8)}{(z-1)(z-0.6)}$$ $$= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) \cdot 0.5(z+0.8)}{T^2 z^2 (z-0.6)} = 0$$ (Only one pole at $z=1$, so $(z-1)$ remains ‚Äî limit is zero.) $$\boxed{K_p = \infty, \quad K_v = \frac{2.25}{T}, \quad K_a = 0}$$ This is a **Type-1 system** with finite ramp error and zero acceleration error tracking. --- ### 3.6 Example: Steady-State Error Computation (Complete KTU-Style) **Given:** Closed-loop system with $G(z) = \frac{0.368(z+0.717)}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$, $T = 1$ s. Find $e_{ss}$ for $r(t) = 2 + 5t$. **Step 1:** Decompose the input ‚Äî apply superposition: - $r_1(t) = 2 \cdot u(t)$ (step of magnitude 2) - $r_2(t) = 5t \cdot u(t)$ (ramp with slope 5) **Step 2:** For step input, $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$: $$K_p = \frac{0.368(1+0.717)}{(1-1)(1-0.368)} = \infty \quad \text{(system is Type-1)}$$ So $e_{ss,1} = 0$. **Step 3:** For ramp input, $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1-z^{-1})}{T} G(z)$: $$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{0.368(z+0.717)}{(z-1)(z-0.368)} = \frac{0.368(1.717)}{1 \cdot 0.632} = 1.0$$ **Step 4:** Ramp error: $$e_{ss,2} = \frac{5}{K_v} = \frac{5}{1.0} = 5$$ **Step 5:** Total error by superposition: $$\boxed{e_{ss} = e_{ss,1} + e_{ss,2} = 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ units}}$$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Sampled-Data Open-Loop System with ZOH ```mermaid graph LR inputA["R(s)"] --> sampA["Sampler
Period T"] sampA --> zohA["Zero-Order
Hold ZOH"] zohA --> plantA["Continuous
Plant G(s)"] plantA --> outputA["C(s)"] style inputA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style sampA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style zohA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style plantA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style outputA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The signal flow is $R(s) \to \text{Sampler} \to \text{ZOH} \to G(s) \to C(s)$. The ZOH converts discrete samples into a piecewise-constant continuous signal that drives the plant. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Digital Closed-Loop Control System ```mermaid graph LR ref["R(s) / R(z)"] --> sum1(("+")) sum1 --> dctrl["Digital
Controller D(z)"] dctrl --> zoh2["Zero-Order
Hold ZOH"] zoh2 --> plant2["Plant G(s)"] plant2 --> cout["C(s) / C(z)"] cout --> sampfb["Feedback
Sampler"] sampfb --> sum1 cout --> output2["Sampled
Output C(z)"] sum2(("+")) --> ref style ref fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style dctrl fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style zoh2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style plant2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style cout fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style sampfb fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style output2 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The error $E(z) = R(z) - C(z)$ enters the digital controller $D(z)$, whose output drives the ZOH and plant $G(s)$. The output is sampled and fed back to the summing junction. The closed-loop transfer function is $\frac{D(z)G(z)}{1 + D(z)G(z)}$. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Z-Transform Mapping Between s-Plane and z-Plane ```mermaid graph TB splane["S-PLANE
Continuous Domain"] sleft["Left Half-Plane
Stable Region sigma less than 0"] simag["Imaginary Axis
Marginally Stable sigma equals 0"] sright["Right Half-Plane
Unstable Region sigma greater than 0"] zplane["Z-PLANE
Discrete Domain"] zinside["Inside Unit Circle
vert z vert less than 1
DISCRETE STABLE"] zcircle["On Unit Circle
vert z vert equals 1
MARGINALLY STABLE"] zoutside["Outside Unit Circle
vert z vert greater than 1
DISCRETE UNSTABLE"] splane --> sleft splane --> simag splane --> sright sleft -.->|"z = exp(sT)"| zinside simag -.->|"z = exp(j omega T)"| zcircle sright -.->|"z = exp(sT)"| zoutside style splane fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style zplane fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style sleft fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style simag fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style sright fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style zinside fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style zcircle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style zoutside fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ transforms the entire s-plane into the z-plane. The infinite left half-plane of s maps to the finite unit disk in z. This is the **key geometric insight** behind the stability analysis of discrete systems. --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Steady-State Error Computation ```mermaid graph TD startA["START
Identify input type and system type"] --> checkA{"Input
Type?"} checkA -->|"Step r(t)=A"| stepA["Compute Kp
Kp = lim z to 1 G(z)"] checkA -->|"Ramp r(t)=At"| rampA["Compute Kv
Kv = lim z to 1 of (1-zinv)/T times G(z)"] checkA -->|"Parabolic r(t)=0.5 At squared"| paraA["Compute Ka
Ka = lim z to 1 of (1-zinv) squared / T squared times G(z)"] stepA --> essstepA["ess = A / (1 + Kp)"] rampA --> essrampA["ess = A / Kv"] paraA --> essparaA["ess = A / Ka"] essstepA --> validateA{"Check Final Value
Theorem Conditions?"} essrampA --> validateA essparaA --> validateA validateA -->|"Yes"| resultA["Report ess"] validateA -->|"No - poles on unit circle"| warningA["WARNING
Final Value Theorem
Does Not Apply"] style startA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style checkA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style stepA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style rampA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style paraA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style essstepA fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style essrampA fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style essparaA fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style resultA fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 style warningA fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart guides the systematic computation of steady-state error. First, identify the input form, then compute the corresponding static error coefficient, and finally apply the appropriate formula. **Always validate the Final Value Theorem conditions** before declaring a result valid. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the **Pulse Transfer Function** of a sampled-data system. State the conditions under which it can be defined. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Pulse Transfer Function $G(z)$ of a linear time-invariant sampled-data system is defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the **sampled output** $C(z)$ to the Z-transform of the **sampled input** $R(z)$, assuming all initial conditions are zero. > > $$G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)}\bigg|_{\text{zero IC}}$$ > > **Conditions:** > 1. The system must be **linear and time-invariant (LTI)**. > 2. All initial conditions must be **zero**. > 3. The input and output must be sampled at the **same rate** (synchronous sampling). > 4. The system must be **causal**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the **Modified Z-Transform**? Why is it required when the standard Z-transform is insufficient? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Modified Z-Transform of a function $G(s)$ is defined as: > > $$G(z, m) = z^{-1} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT + mT) \, z^{-k}, \quad 0 \leq m \leq 1$$ > > **Why Required:** > The standard Z-transform can only capture the response of a sampled system at **integer multiples of the sampling period** $T$. However, in many practical systems (e.g., systems with **transport delay** or when output is needed at **non-integer time instants**), we need the response at $t = kT + mT$ where $0 < m < 1$. The Modified Z-Transform provides this capability, making it essential for analyzing systems with time delays, non-uniform sampling, and obtaining **inter-sample response**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Module 2 in PERAT631 typically allows the student to choose between two 14-mark questions. The structure below mirrors this pattern with two complete independent question choices. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Pulse Transfer Function & Steady-State Error* **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** For a sampled-data control system with zero-order hold and plant transfer function $G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$, sampling period $T = 1$ s, determine: (i) The pulse transfer function $G(z)$ of the ZOH + plant combination. (ii) The closed-loop pulse transfer function $\frac{C(z)}{R(z)}$ for unity digital feedback. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $\frac{G(s)}{s}$: $$\frac{G(s)}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}$$ **Step 2:** Partial fraction expansion: $$\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+1}$$ Multiplying both sides by $s^2(s+1)$: $$1 = As(s+1) + B(s+1) + Cs^2$$ At $s = 0$: $1 = B(1) \Rightarrow B = 1$ At $s = -1$: $1 = C(1) \Rightarrow C = 1$ Coefficient of $s^2$: $0 = A + C \Rightarrow A = -1$ So: $$\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} = -\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{1}{s+1}$$ **Step 3:** Take Z-transform (using standard pairs): $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}\right] = -\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{z - e^{-T}}$$ With $T = 1$ s, $e^{-1} = 0.368$: $$= -\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{z-0.368}$$ **Step 4:** Combine and multiply by $(1 - z^{-1}) = \frac{z-1}{z}$: After algebraic simplification (combining over common denominator and multiplying): $$G(z) = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z - 0.368)}$$ **Step 5:** Closed-loop PTF for unity feedback: $$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)} = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368) + 0.368z + 0.264}$$ Expanding the denominator: $$(z-1)(z-0.368) = z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368$$ Adding $0.368z + 0.264$: $$z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.368z + 0.264 = z^2 - z + 0.632$$ $$\boxed{\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 - z + 0.632}}$$ --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For the system obtained in part (a), compute the static error coefficients $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$ and hence determine the steady-state error for the input $r(t) = 3 + 5t + 0.5t^2$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $K_p$: $$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) = \frac{0.368(1) + 0.264}{(1-1)(1-0.368)} = \frac{0.632}{0} = \infty$$ (Pole at $z=1$ in denominator $\Rightarrow K_p = \infty$.) **Step 2:** Compute $K_v$: $$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{Tz} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$$ $$= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z(z-0.368)} = \frac{0.368 + 0.264}{1 \cdot 0.632} = \frac{0.632}{0.632} = 1.0$$ **Step 3:** Compute $K_a$: $$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z^2} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z^2} \cdot \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)} \cdot \frac{1}{T^2}$$ Wait ‚Äî we have $T = 1$ s, so $T^2 = 1$. Simplifying: $$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 (z - 0.368)} = \frac{0.632}{0.632} = 1.0$$ **Step 4:** Apply superposition for the composite input: The input $r(t) = 3 + 5t + 0.5t^2$ can be decomposed as: - Step of magnitude 3: $e_{ss,1} = \frac{3}{1 + K_p} = 0$ (since $K_p = \infty$) - Ramp with slope 5: $e_{ss,2} = \frac{5}{K_v} = \frac{5}{1.0} = 5$ - Parabolic with $\frac{A}{2} = 0.5$, so $A = 1$: $e_{ss,3} = \frac{A}{K_a} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1$ **Step 5:** Total steady-state error: $$\boxed{e_{ss} = 0 + 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ units}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Bilinear Transformation & Modified Z-Transform* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Convert the continuous-time transfer function $G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+5)}$ to a discrete equivalent $G(z)$ using: (i) The **bilinear transformation** with $T = 0.2$ s. (ii) Compare the location of poles in the s-plane and z-plane to verify stability. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Write the bilinear substitution with $T = 0.2$ s: $$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = \frac{2}{0.2} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into $G(s)$: $$G(z) = \frac{10}{10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} \left[ 10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} + 5 \right]}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the inner bracket: $$10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} + 5 = \frac{10(z-1) + 5(z+1)}{z+1} = \frac{10z - 10 + 5z + 5}{z+1} = \frac{15z - 5}{z+1}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute back: $$G(z) = \frac{10}{10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} \cdot \frac{15z-5}{z+1}} = \frac{10 (z+1)^2}{10 (z-1)(15z - 5)}$$ **Step 5:** Simplify (factor 5 from the second term): $$G(z) = \frac{10(z+1)^2}{10 \cdot 5 (z-1)(3z - 1)} = \frac{(z+1)^2}{5(z-1)(3z-1)}$$ **Step 6:** Normalize to monic denominator: $$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{15(z-1)(z - 1/3)} = \frac{(z+1)^2}{15z^2 - 20z + 5}}$$ **Step 7:** Verify stability by checking pole locations: Continuous poles: $s_1 = 0$ and $s_2 = -5$. Both in the **left half-plane** (stable). Discrete poles: $z_1 = 1$ (on the unit circle ‚Äî **marginally stable** pole corresponds to $s = 0$ in continuous domain) and $z_2 = 1/3$ (inside the unit circle ‚Äî stable, corresponds to $s = -5$). > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** The pole at $s = 0$ maps to $z = 1$ because $\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 0$ requires $z = 1$. This is consistent with the integrator behavior in the discrete domain. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For a sampled-data system with $G(s) = \frac{1-e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s+2}$ and $T = 0.5$ s, derive the **Modified Z-Transfer Function** $G(z, m)$ and evaluate it for $m = 0.5$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Recognize the ZOH form: $$G(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s+2}$$ **Step 2:** For modified Z-transform, expand: $$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s(s+2)}$$ **Step 3:** The modified Z-transform of the first term: $$\mathcal{Z}_m\left[\frac{1}{s(s+2)}\right] = z^{-1} \cdot \mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{e^{mTs}}{s(s+2)}\right]$$ **Step 4:** Partial fraction: $$\frac{1}{s(s+2)} = \frac{1/2}{s} - \frac{1/2}{s+2}$$ So: $$\frac{e^{mTs}}{s(s+2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{mTs}}{s} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{mTs}}{s+2}$$ **Step 5:** Z-transform with time-advance property: $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{e^{mTs}}{s}\right] = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$$ $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{e^{mTs}}{s+2}\right] = \frac{e^{-2mT}}{1 - e^{-2T} z^{-1}}$$ **Step 6:** Combining: $$\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{e^{mTs}}{s(s+2)}\right] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{-2mT}}{1 - e^{-2T} z^{-1}}$$ Therefore: $$G(z, m) = z^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2(1 - z^{-1})} - \frac{e^{-2mT}}{2(1 - e^{-2T} z^{-1})}\right]$$ **Step 7:** Substitute $T = 0.5$ s, so $e^{-2T} = e^{-1} = 0.368$, and $m = 0.5$, so $e^{-2mT} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$: $$G(z, 0.5) = z^{-1}\left[\frac{0.5}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{0.6065}{2(1 - 0.368 z^{-1})}\right]$$ $$= z^{-1}\left[\frac{0.5}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{0.3033}{1 - 0.368 z^{-1}}\right]$$ **Step 8:** Common denominator form: $$G(z, 0.5) = \frac{0.5(1 - 0.368z^{-1}) - 0.3033(1 - z^{-1})}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.368z^{-1})} \cdot z^{-1}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$= \frac{0.5 - 0.184z^{-1} - 0.3033 + 0.3033z^{-1}}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.368z^{-1})} \cdot z^{-1}$$ $$= \frac{0.1967 + 0.1193z^{-1}}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.368z^{-1})} \cdot z^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{G(z, 0.5) = \frac{0.1967z^{-1} + 0.1193z^{-2}}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.368z^{-1})}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** When $m = 1$, the modified Z-transform reduces to the standard $G(z)$. When $m = 0$, it represents the response one full sample period after the input pulse ‚Äî useful for analyzing transport delays. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Mixing up $\mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right]$ with $\frac{G(z)}{z}$** ‚Äî These are NOT equal. You must first divide by $s$, then take the Z-transform. Mistake: directly writing $\frac{1-e^{-aT}}{a(z-e^{-aT})}$ for $G(s) = \frac{1}{s+a}$ without the proper $\frac{G(s)}{s}$ step. **Penalty: 2-3 marks.** > > 2. **Forgetting the ZOH factor $(1 - z^{-1})$** ‚Äî When a ZOH is present, the pulse transfer function is $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}[\frac{G(s)}{s}]$, NOT just $\mathcal{Z}[G(s)]$. **Penalty: 2-4 marks.** > > 3. **Incorrectly applying the Final Value Theorem** ‚Äî Students often forget to verify that all poles of $(1-z^{-1})E(z)$ lie **inside** the unit circle (except possibly at $z=1$). **Penalty: 1-2 marks.** > > 4. **Confusing $G(z)H(z)$ with $GH(z)$** ‚Äî In closed-loop systems where $G(s)$ and $H(s)$ are NOT separated by a sampler, you CANNOT split the Z-transform. Always check sampler positions first. **Penalty: 3-4 marks.** > > 5. **Wrong error formulas for ramp/parabolic inputs** ‚Äî Many students write $e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1+K_p}$ for ramp inputs. The correct formula involves $K_v$ and depends on the sampling period $T$. **Penalty: 2-3 marks.** > > 6. **Skipping the steady-state error Final Value Theorem condition** ‚Äî If the system is marginally stable (poles on the unit circle other than $z=1$), the FVT does not apply, and $e_{ss} = \infty$. **Penalty: 1-2 marks.** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2:** - ‚òê **Pulse Transfer Function** = ratio of Z-transforms of sampled output to sampled input, with zero initial conditions. - ‚òê **ZOH + Plant formula**: $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \mathcal{Z}\left[\frac{G(s)}{s}\right]$ ‚Äî always divide by $s$ first. - ‚òê **Stability Mapping**: LHP of s-plane $\leftrightarrow$ Inside unit circle in z-plane ($z = e^{sT}$). - ‚òê **Closed-Loop PTF (Unity Feedback)**: $T(z) = \frac{D(z)G(z)}{1 + D(z)G(z)}$. - ‚òê **Closed-Loop with Continuous H**: $T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$ where $GH(z) = \mathcal{Z}[G(s)H(s)]$ ‚Äî never split without sampler. - ‚òê **Modified Z-Transform**: Use $G(z, m) = z^{-1} \mathcal{Z}[e^{mTs} G(s)]$ for $0 \leq m \leq 1$. - ‚òê **Steady-State Error FVT**: $e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) E(z)$ ‚Äî verify pole conditions first. - ‚òê **Static Error Coefficients**: - $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ ‚Üí for **step** input - $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1-z^{-1})}{T} G(z)$ ‚Üí for **ramp** input - $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(1-z^{-1})^2}{T^2} G(z)$ ‚Üí for **parabolic** input - ‚òê **System Type**: Number of poles at $z = 1$ in $G(z)$.
