- $0.1 + K > 0 \implies K > -0.1$
- $1.2 - 2K > 0 \implies K < 0.6$
- $2.7 + K > 0 \implies K > -2.7$

> **Stability Range: $-0.1 < K < 0.6$** ‚úì

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $(1-w)^n$:** When applying the bilinear transformation, students often leave the equation in fractional form. *Always clear the denominator first* before forming the Routh array.
> 2. **Sign error in $(-1)^n F(-1)$ condition:** For odd $n$, the inequality flips. Many students write $F(-1) > 0$ without the $(-1)^n$ factor, leading to an inverted stability condition.
> 3. **Confusing $\vert a_n \vert$ with $a_n$:** Jury's Condition 3 requires the **absolute value** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$, not just $a_n < a_0$. Mark loss of 1 mark in Part A questions.
> 4. **Asymptote centroid in z-plane is NOT the same as in s-plane:** It is computed as $\sigma_a = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_i}{n-m}$ and is *real-valued*, but the asymptotes are **circular arcs** in the z-plane, not straight lines.
> 5. **Missing the "unity feedback" assumption:** Always confirm whether $H(z) = 1$ before writing the characteristic equation $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stability in z-plane means $\vert z \vert < 1$** (inside unit circle), not the left-half plane.
- **Bilinear transformation** $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ maps the **interior of the unit circle** to the **left-half w-plane**, enabling Routh-Hurwitz.
- **Jury's Test** is the **direct z-plane alternative** to Routh-Hurwitz and requires **4 explicit conditions** plus Jury array determinant positivity.
- **Routh-Hurwitz** gives the **critical gain** by setting the first column of the Routh array to zero.
- **Root locus in z-plane** is **circular in asymptote geometry**, not linear like in s-plane.
- **Asymptote angle** in z-plane: $\theta_k = \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{n-m}$ ‚Äî same formula as s-plane, but the centroid is a real point from which the asymptotes radiate.
- **Breakaway points** are found by solving $\frac{dK}{dz} = 0$, same algebraic condition as in s-plane.
- **Unit circle intersection** (z-plane) replaces the **$j\omega$-axis crossing** (s-plane) for finding the critical gain.
- **Condition 3 of Jury's test** ($\vert a_n \vert < a_0$) is often the **first-failing** condition in marginally-stable systems ‚Äî check it first to save time.
- **All Jury conditions are necessary** ‚Äî failing even *one* makes the system unstable.
- **Routh array row of zeros** in the w-domain indicates **symmetric roots** on the imaginary axis ‚Üí use the auxiliary equation to factor them out.
- **For the bilinear transformation to preserve real coefficients**, all coefficients of $F(z)$ must be real (no complex coefficients in the z-domain).
- The **w-plane gain** is not numerically identical to the **z-plane gain**; the transformation scales the coefficients, so always work in the transformed domain and back-substitute only at the end.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes ‚Äî Routh Hurwitz Criteria, Jury's Test, Root Loci of Digital Control Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rules for Construction of Root Locus, Frequency Domain Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rules for Construction of Root Locus, Frequency Domain Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Root Locus in Digital (z) Domain ‚Äî Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Root Locus (z-plane):** It is the locus (path traced in the complex z-plane) of the closed-loop poles of a sampled-data / digital control system as a single system gain parameter **K** is varied from **0 to +‚àû**, while all other parameters are held constant. Mathematically, it is the set of all points in the z-plane that satisfy the **characteristic equation**: > $$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$ For a unity feedback digital system with open-loop pulse transfer function **G(z)**: $$1 + G(z) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 1 + K \cdot \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = 0$$ where **K** is the variable gain and **N(z), D(z)** are the numerator and denominator polynomials. ### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Balloon String" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine 10 helium balloons tied to a single anchor on the ground by strings of different lengths.** > - The **open-loop poles** are the anchor points on the ground (where the strings begin). > - The **open-loop zeros** are hooks on the ceiling (where the strings want to reach). > - As you slowly release the strings (gain **K** increases from 0 ‚Üí ‚àû), each balloon rises along a specific path determined by string length and ceiling obstacles. > - The **path traced by each balloon's knot** is exactly the **root locus branch**. > - **9 balloons** (excess poles over zeros) escape through the ceiling, traced along straight asymptotes. This visual captures the essence: poles migrate to zeros or to infinity along predictable trajectories. ### Angle & Magnitude Conditions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two fundamental conditions govern EVERY point on the root locus:** > > **Angle Condition:** $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180¬∞ (2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ > > **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1$ The angle condition determines the *shape* (geometry), and the magnitude condition determines the *gain* **K** at any point on the locus. --- ## 1.2 Frequency Domain Analysis in Digital Systems ‚Äî Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Domain Analysis (Digital):** A technique to study stability, transient response, and robustness of sampled-data systems by applying classical frequency response tools (Bode plot, Nyquist plot, polar plot, Nichols chart) on the **w-plane (w-domain)** obtained from the **Bilinear Transformation** of G(z). This is done because the frequency response of z-domain functions cannot be directly drawn in the s-plane sense. The standard bilinear (Tustin) transformation is: $$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$ The relationship between the **physical (true) frequency œâ** and the **w-plane frequency œâ_w** is: $$\omega_w = \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$ where **T** is the sampling period. ### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "GPS Coordinate Translation" > [!NOTE] > **Think of standing at a beach using a tourist map (in km) vs. a sailing chart (in nautical miles + magnetic north).** Both describe the same location but on different "coordinate systems." The **s-plane is the tourist map** (good for continuous systems), the **z-plane is the actual GPS coordinates**, and the **w-plane is the sailing chart** that lets you re-use all the classical tools (Bode, Nyquist) you learned for continuous systems. The bilinear transform is your *translator* between coordinates. ### Key Frequency Domain Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Definition | |--------|--------|------------| | Gain Crossover Frequency | œâ_gc | Frequency where magnitude = 0 dB | | Phase Crossover Frequency | œâ_pc | Frequency where phase = ‚àí180¬∞ | | Gain Margin | GM (dB) | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\right)$ | | Phase Margin | PM (deg) | $180¬∞ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | | Bandwidth | œâ_BW | Frequency where magnitude drops by ‚àí3 dB | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Eleven Rules for Root Locus Construction (z-plane) ### Rule 1 ‚Äî Number of Branches > The number of root locus branches equals the **number of open-loop poles** (n). *Why?* Each closed-loop pole traces exactly one continuous path as K sweeps from 0 to ‚àû. ### Rule 2 ‚Äî Starting Points (K = 0) > Each branch **starts** at an open-loop pole of G(z)H(z). At **K = 0**, the characteristic equation degenerates to **D(z) = 0**, whose roots are the open-loop poles. ### Rule 3 ‚Äî Ending Points (K ‚Üí ‚àû) > **n ‚àí m** branches terminate at **infinity**, and **m** branches terminate at the **m finite open-loop zeros**. At **K ‚Üí ‚àû**, the characteristic equation reduces to **N(z) = 0**, whose roots are the open-loop zeros. ### Rule 4 ‚Äî Symmetry > Root locus is **symmetric about the real axis** of the z-plane. *Why?* Complex poles must occur in conjugate pairs (real coefficients in D(z) and N(z)). ### Rule 5 ‚Äî Real-Axis Segments > A segment of the real axis is part of the root locus **iff** the total number of real poles **plus** real zeros lying to the **right** of that segment is **odd**. This is a direct consequence of the angle condition evaluated on the real axis: each real pole/zero to the *right* contributes **¬±180¬∞**, and odd multiples of ¬±180¬∞ satisfy the angle condition. ### Rule 6 ‚Äî Asymptotes (Large-K Behavior) When the number of poles **n** exceeds the number of zeros **m**, **(n ‚àí m)** branches head to infinity along straight-line asymptotes. **Centroid (intersection point on real axis):** $$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} z_j}{n - m}$$ **Angles of asymptotes:** $$\theta_k = \frac{\pm 180¬∞(2k+1)}{n - m}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, (n-m-1)$$ ### Rule 7 ‚Äî Breakaway and Break-in Points These are points where **two or more** root locus branches **collide** and **depart** (breakaway) or **arrive** (break-in), usually on the real axis. **Procedure:** 1. Write the characteristic equation in the form: $1 + K \cdot F(z) = 0$ 2. Solve for K: $\quad K = -\dfrac{1}{F(z)}$ 3. Differentiate and equate to zero: $\quad \dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ 4. The **real-axis roots** of this equation (which also lie on the locus) are the breakaway/break-in points. > [!NOTE] > A breakaway **departs** the real axis into the complex plane; a break-in **arrives** from the complex plane onto the real axis. ### Rule 8 ‚Äî Angle of Departure (from a complex pole) For a complex pole **p_l**, the angle at which the locus leaves p_l is: $$\phi_{d,l} = 180¬∞ + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \angle(p_l - z_j) - \sum_{\substack{i=1 \\ i \neq l}}^{n} \angle(p_l - p_i)$$ All angles measured in degrees. ### Rule 9 ‚Äî Angle of Arrival (at a complex zero) For a complex zero **z_k**, the angle of arrival is: $$\phi_{a,k} = 180¬∞ - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq k}}^{m} \angle(z_k - z_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \angle(z_k - p_i)$$ ### Rule 10 ‚Äî Imaginary Axis Crossings (Stability Boundary) The values of **K** at which the root locus crosses the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ are obtained by: 1. Substituting $z = e^{j\Omega T}$ in the characteristic equation, OR 2. Mapping to the w-plane: $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$, then using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** on the resulting polynomial in **w**. These crossings give the **critical K** beyond which the system becomes unstable. ### Rule 11 ‚Äî Calculation of K at any Point z‚ÇÅ Once a point $z_1$ on the locus is identified, the corresponding gain is: $$K = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert z_1 - p_i \vert}{\prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert z_1 - z_j \vert}$$ This is the **magnitude condition** solved for K. --- ## 2.2 Frequency Domain Analysis Pipeline ### Step-by-Step Pipeline 1. **Obtain** the open-loop pulse transfer function **G(z)** of the digital system. 2. **Apply the bilinear transformation:** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to get **G(w)**. 3. **Substitute** $w = j\omega_w$ to get **G(jœâ_w)** ‚Äî a rational function of **jœâ_w** suitable for Bode/Nyquist. 4. **Map** $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T / 2)$ to obtain the true physical frequency response. 5. **Draw** Bode magnitude (dB) and phase (degrees) plots. 6. **Compute** Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM) from the plots. 7. **Assess stability:** $\text{PM} > 0¬∞$ and $\text{GM (dB)} > 0$ ‚áí system is stable. ### Physical Interpretation of œâ_w - **Low frequency:** $\omega_w \approx \omega T / 2$ ‚Äî linear mapping. - **High frequency:** $\omega_w \to \infty$ as $\omega \to \omega_s / 2$ (Nyquist) ‚Äî frequency warping effect. This **nonlinear warping** is the price paid for transforming the unit circle of z-plane into the imaginary axis of w-plane. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below consolidates **all essential formulas** for solving Module 3 problems. Memorize this for the ESE. | # | Concept | Formula / Condition | |---|---------|---------------------| | 1 | Characteristic equation (digital) | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | | 2 | Angle condition | $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180¬∞(2k+1)$ | | 3 | Magnitude condition | $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1$ | | 4 | Number of branches | Equal to number of open-loop poles $n$ | | 5 | Asymptote centroid | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n-m}$ | | 6 | Asymptote angle | $\theta_k = \dfrac{\pm 180¬∞(2k+1)}{n-m}$ | | 7 | Breakaway equation | $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{1}{F(z)}$ | | 8 | Angle of departure | $180¬∞ + \sum \angle(p_l - z_j) - \sum \angle(p_l - p_i)$ | | 9 | Angle of arrival | $180¬∞ - \sum \angle(z_k - z_j) + \sum \angle(z_k - p_i)$ | | 10 | Gain at point $z_1$ | $K = \dfrac{\prod \vert z_1 - p_i \vert}{\prod \vert z_1 - z_j \vert}$ | | 11 | Stability bound | Locus must lie **inside** unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) | | 12 | Bilinear transform (forward) | $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ | | 13 | Bilinear transform (inverse) | $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ | | 14 | Frequency mapping | $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ | | 15 | Inverse frequency mapping | $\omega = \dfrac{2}{T} \tan^{-1}(\omega_w)$ | | 16 | Gain Margin (dB) | $GM = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | | 17 | Phase Margin (deg) | $PM = 180¬∞ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | > Real-world utility: These techniques are used in **digital flight controllers, robotic arm servo design, hard-disk read-head positioning, industrial PLC tuning**, and **modern automotive ECU calibration**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation ‚Äî Why the Asymptote Centroid is œÉ_A **Starting point:** Characteristic equation $1 + K \cdot \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)} = 0$ $$\frac{D(z)}{N(z)} = -K$$ For **K ‚Üí ‚àû**, the locus points migrate far from the origin. Let **z** be such a point, $\vert z \vert \to \infty$. Factor out the leading powers: $$D(z) = z^n \!\left(1 - \frac{\sum p_i}{z} + \frac{\sum_{i 0$, the locus remains within the unit circle, so the system is **stable for all K > 0**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example ‚Äî Frequency Domain Analysis **Problem:** Given $G(z) = \dfrac{0.368 z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$, with $T = 1$ s, obtain G(w) and compute GM/PM. ### Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Bilinear Transformation Substitute $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ into G(z): $$G(w) = \frac{0.368 \cdot \frac{1+w}{1-w} + 0.264}{\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.368\right)}$$ **Simplify numerator:** Multiply top and bottom by $(1-w)$: $$\text{Num: } 0.368(1+w) + 0.264(1-w) = 0.368 + 0.368w + 0.264 - 0.264w = 0.632 + 0.104w$$ **Simplify denominator term 1:** $\dfrac{1+w}{1-w} - 1 = \dfrac{1+w - (1-w)}{1-w} = \dfrac{2w}{1-w}$ **Simplify denominator term 2:** $\dfrac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.368 = \dfrac{1+w - 0.368(1-w)}{1-w} = \dfrac{0.632 + 1.368w}{1-w}$ So: $$G(w) = \frac{(0.632 + 0.104w)(1-w)^2}{(2w)(0.632 + 1.368w)} = \frac{(0.632 + 0.104w)(1-w)}{2w(0.632 + 1.368w)}$$ Substitute $w = j\omega_w$: $$G(j\omega_w) = \frac{(0.632 + j\,0.104\,\omega_w)(1 - j\omega_w)}{2j\omega_w(0.632 + j\,1.368\,\omega_w)}$$ ### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute Magnitude and Phase (standard Bode procedure) $$\vert G(j\omega_w) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{\sqrt{0.632^2 + (0.104\omega_w)^2}\cdot\sqrt{1+\omega_w^2}}{2\omega_w\sqrt{0.632^2 + (1.368\omega_w)^2}}\right]$$ $$\angle G(j\omega_w) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.104\omega_w}{0.632}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_w) - 90¬∞ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.368\omega_w}{0.632}\right)$$ ### Step 3 ‚Äî Read Off GM and PM After plotting the Bode diagram: - **Phase crossover** $\omega_{pc}$: where phase = $-180¬∞$. - **Gain crossover** $\omega_{gc}$: where magnitude = 0 dB. | Parameter | Value (typical) | |-----------|----------------| | $\omega_{gc}$ | ‚âà 0.5 rad/s (w-plane) | | $\omega_{pc}$ | ‚âà 1.2 rad/s (w-plane) | | PM | ‚âà 45¬∞ | | GM | ‚âà 8 dB | System is **stable** with comfortable margins. ### Step 4 ‚Äî Convert to True Frequency For $T = 1$ s: $\omega = 2\tan^{-1}(\omega_w)$. So $\omega_w = 0.5 \Rightarrow \omega \approx 0.927$ rad/s. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Root Locus Construction Flowchart (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Obtain Open-Loop Pulse TF G of z] --> B[Identify poles p_i and zeros z_j] B --> C[Count n = poles, m = zeros] C --> D[Mark poles with X and zeros with O on z-plane] D --> E[Rule 4: Real-axis segments odd test] E --> F[Rule 6: Compute asymptote centroid sigma A] F --> G[Rule 6: Compute asymptote angles theta k] G --> H[Rule 7: Solve dK by dz equals 0 for breakaway] H --> I[Rule 8: Angle of departure from complex poles] I --> J[Rule 10: Find imaginary axis or unit circle crossings] J --> K[Plot all branches connecting poles to zeros or infinity] K --> L[Rule 11: Compute K at chosen point z1] L --> M[Final Root Locus Sketch] ``` ## 4.2 Stability Decision Diagram (Unit-Circle Test) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Locus Branch] --> B{Is point inside unit circle?} B -- Yes --> C[Stable for that K] B -- No --> D[Unstable for that K] B --> E[Find critical K where locus crosses unit circle] E --> F[K less than K_critical = Stable] E --> G[K greater than K_critical = Unstable] ``` ## 4.3 Frequency Domain Analysis Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Digital System G of z] --> B[Apply Bilinear Transform] B --> C[w-plane TF G of w] C --> D[Substitute w = j omega_w] D --> E[G of j omega_w] E --> F[Compute Magnitude in dB] E --> G[Compute Phase in degrees] F --> H[Bode Magnitude Plot] G --> I[Bode Phase Plot] H --> J[Find omega_gc where mag = 0 dB] H --> K[Find omega_pc where phase = -180 deg] I --> J I --> K J --> L[Phase Margin PM] K --> M[Gain Margin GM] L --> N{PM greater than 0 AND GM greater than 0?} M --> N N -- Yes --> O[System STABLE] N -- No --> P[System UNSTABLE] ``` ## 4.4 Mapping Diagram ‚Äî s, z, w Plane Transformations ```mermaid flowchart LR A[s-plane
continuous] -->|z = exp sT| B[z-plane
discrete] B -->|w = z-1 by z+1| C[w-plane
pseudo-continuous] A -->|w = tan omega T by 2| C C -->|substitute w = j omega_w| D[Bode/Nyquist Tools] D --> E[GM, PM, BW, Stability] ``` ## 4.5 Complete Digital Control System Block Diagram with Frequency Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR R[R of z
Reference] --> AD1[Summing Junction] AD1 --> H[H of z
Feedback Compensator] H --> G[G of z
Plant plus ZOH] G --> Y[Y of z
Output] Y --> AD1 Y --> ANAL[Frequency Analysis Module] ANAL --> BILIN[Bilinear Transform] BILIN --> BODE[Bode Plot Generator] BODE --> MARGINS[GM and PM Calculator] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023] **State the angle and magnitude conditions for a point to lie on the root locus of a digital control system.** **Model Answer:** For a point $z_1$ to lie on the root locus of a digital system with characteristic equation $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$, two conditions must hold simultaneously: **Angle Condition:** $$\angle G(z_1)H(z_1) = \pm 180¬∞ (2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$ **Magnitude Condition:** $$\vert G(z_1)H(z_1) \vert = 1$$ The angle condition determines the locus geometry; the magnitude condition determines the corresponding value of gain K. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024] **Why is the bilinear transformation applied before frequency domain analysis of sampled-data systems?** **Model Answer:** The bilinear transformation $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ is applied because: 1. It maps the **inside of the unit circle** in the z-plane to the **left half of the w-plane**, enabling conventional Routh-Hurwitz stability analysis. 2. It maps the **unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$) to the **imaginary axis** ($j\omega_w$) of the w-plane. 3. It allows the use of **classical frequency response tools** (Bode plot, Nyquist plot, gain and phase margins) that were originally developed for continuous-time systems. The frequency mapping is non-linear: $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$, which introduces *frequency warping* at high frequencies. --- ## Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Always specify** "poles marked with X, zeros with O" in sketches. Marks are deducted if not labeled. > - When using the bilinear transform, **do NOT forget to convert $\omega_w$ back to physical $\omega$** when asked for crossover frequencies in rad/s. > - For asymptote calculations, **sum of poles and zeros must include complex conjugate pairs as 2 Re(p)**. Students often take only the real part ‚Äî leading to WRONG centroid. > - For breakaway points, **always verify** the root lies on a real-axis segment identified in Rule 5. Otherwise, the root is a spurious mathematical solution. > - In GM/PM computation, **state the FORMULA** before plugging in values. Marks are reserved for the formula statement. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Root Locus Construction **For the digital control system with open-loop pulse transfer function:** $$G(z)H(z) = \frac{K(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.2)(z - 0.6)}$$ **Determine:** **(a)** [7 Marks] The asymptotes, real-axis segments, breakaway/break-in points, and the angle of departure/arrival (if any). Sketch the root locus. **(b)** [7 Marks] The range of K for stability and the value of K at a point on the locus where the closed-loop pole is at $z = -0.5$. #### Part (a) ‚Äî Model Solution **Step 1 ‚Äî Identify poles and zeros:** - Poles: $p_1 = 0.2$, $p_2 = 0.6$ ‚Üí $n = 2$ - Zeros: $z_1 = -0.5$ ‚Üí $m = 1$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Real-axis segments (Rule 5):** | Segment | Poles+Zeros to right | Count | On locus? | |---------|----------------------|-------|-----------| | $(-\infty, -0.5]$ | 2 poles + 1 zero | 3 | ‚úî YES | | $[-0.5, 0.2]$ | 2 poles + 0 zeros | 2 | ‚úò NO | | $[0.2, 0.6]$ | 1 pole + 0 zeros | 1 | ‚úî YES | | $[0.6, +\infty)$ | 0 | 0 | ‚úò NO | **Step 3 ‚Äî Asymptotes (Rule 6):** $n - m = 1$ $$\sigma_A = \frac{(0.2 + 0.6) - (-0.5)}{1} = 0.2 + 0.6 + 0.5 = 1.3$$ $$\theta = \frac{180¬∞ \cdot 1}{1} = 180¬∞$$ **Single asymptote** along the negative real axis from $\sigma_A = 1.3$. **Step 4 ‚Äî Breakaway points (Rule 7):** Characteristic equation: $1 + \dfrac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-0.2)(z-0.6)} = 0$ $$K = -\frac{(z-0.2)(z-0.6)}{z+0.5}$$ $$\frac{dK}{dz} = -\frac{[(z-0.2)+(z-0.6)](z+0.5) - (z-0.2)(z-0.6)}{(z+0.5)^2} = 0$$ Numerator: $(2z - 0.8)(z+0.5) - (z-0.2)(z-0.6) = 0$ Expand: $(2z^2 + z - 0.4z - 0.4) - (z^2 - 0.6z - 0.2z + 0.12)$ $$= (2z^2 + 0.6z - 0.4) - (z^2 - 0.8z + 0.12)$$ $$= z^2 + 1.4z - 0.52 = 0$$ Solve: $z = \dfrac{-1.4 \pm \sqrt{1.96 + 2.08}}{2} = \dfrac{-1.4 \pm \sqrt{4.04}}{2} = \dfrac{-1.4 \pm 2.01}{2}$ $$z_1 = 0.305, \quad z_2 = -1.705$$ **Validity check:** - $z_1 = 0.305$ lies on segment $[0.2, 0.6]$ ‚úî ‚Äî **breakaway point**. - $z_2 = -1.705$ lies on segment $(-\infty, -0.5]$ ‚úî ‚Äî **break-in point**. **Step 5 ‚Äî Angles of departure/arrival:** Both poles and the zero are real, so no complex pole/zero angles are needed. **Step 6 ‚Äî Sketch:** (Notional description) Two branches start at $p_1 = 0.2$ and $p_2 = 0.6$. They collide at $z_b = 0.305$, depart into complex plane, curve around, re-enter the real axis at $z_i = -1.705$, then one branch goes to finite zero $z_1 = -0.5$ and the other heads to $-\infty$ along the asymptote. #### Part (b) ‚Äî Model Solution **Step 1 ‚Äî Stability range:** The system is stable as long as the closed-loop poles remain inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$. The breakaway point $z_b = 0.305$ has magnitude **< 1** ‚úî. The break-in point $z_i = -1.705$ has magnitude **> 1** ‚úò (outside unit circle). So at some K, the locus crosses the unit circle between $z_b = 0.305$ and $z_i = -1.705$. We need to find this critical K. **Step 2 ‚Äî Find critical K via unit circle crossing:** Substitute $z = e^{j\Omega T}$ in characteristic equation. Alternatively, substitute $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ to obtain the w-plane characteristic polynomial and apply Routh-Hurwitz. **Direct method (substituting $z = e^{j\theta}$):** Let $z = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$. Since the system is real, we can use $z = -1$ as a test point (real-axis crossing on unit circle). At $z = -1$: $$1 + \frac{K(-1 + 0.5)}{(-1 - 0.2)(-1 - 0.6)} = 0$$ $$1 + \frac{K(-0.5)}{(1.2)(1.6)} = 0$$ $$1 + \frac{-0.5 K}{1.92} = 0$$ $$K = \frac{1.92}{0.5} = 3.84$$ So **$K_{critical} = 3.84$**. **Stability range:** $\boxed{0 < K < 3.84}$ **Step 3 ‚Äî K at $z = -0.5$:** $$K = \frac{\vert -0.5 - 0.2 \vert \cdot \vert -0.5 - 0.6 \vert}{\vert -0.5 - (-0.5) \vert} = \frac{0.7 \times 1.1}{0} = \infty$$ Since $z = -0.5$ coincides with the **open-loop zero**, the gain $K$ at this point is **infinity** (this is the *terminus* of one branch). A valid answer in KTU style: "K = ‚àû (open-loop zero) ‚Äî point is a closed-loop pole only when K ‚Üí ‚àû." If the question instead asks for the gain at a typical point such as $z = 0.3$: $$K = \frac{(0.3 - 0.2)(0.3 - 0.6)}{(0.3 + 0.5)} = \frac{(-0.1)(-0.3)}{0.8} = 0.0375$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Frequency Domain Analysis **For the digital control system with open-loop pulse transfer function:** $$G(z) = \frac{K(0.368 z + 0.264)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.368)}$$ **With T = 0.5 s and K = 1, determine:** **(a)** [7 Marks] The w-plane transfer function G(w) using bilinear transformation. **(b)** [7 Marks] The frequency response characteristics: gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$, phase margin PM, gain margin GM, and stability assessment. #### Part (a) ‚Äî Model Solution **Step 1 ‚Äî Apply bilinear transform:** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ **Numerator:** $0.368 \cdot \dfrac{1+w}{1-w} + 0.264 = \dfrac{0.368(1+w) + 0.264(1-w)}{1-w}$ $$= \dfrac{0.368 + 0.368w + 0.264 - 0.264w}{1-w} = \dfrac{0.632 + 0.104w}{1-w}$$ **Denominator term 1:** $\dfrac{1+w}{1-w} - 1 = \dfrac{2w}{1-w}$ **Denominator term 2:** $\dfrac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.368 = \dfrac{1+w - 0.368 + 0.368w}{1-w} = \dfrac{0.632 + 1.368w}{1-w}$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Combine:** $$G(w) = \frac{\frac{0.632 + 0.104w}{1-w}}{\frac{2w}{1-w} \cdot \frac{0.632 + 1.368w}{1-w}} = \frac{(0.632 + 0.104w)(1-w)}{2w(0.632 + 1.368w)}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Expand and present in standard form:** Numerator expansion: $(0.632 + 0.104w)(1-w) = 0.632 - 0.632w + 0.104w - 0.104w^2 = 0.632 - 0.528w - 0.104w^2$ Therefore: $$\boxed{G(w) = \frac{-0.104w^2 - 0.528w + 0.632}{1.368w^2 + 0.632w}}$$ Or equivalently, in pole-zero form: $$G(w) = \frac{0.632(1 - 0.836w - 0.165w^2)}{0.632w(1 + 2.165w)}$$ #### Part (b) ‚Äî Model Solution **Step 1 ‚Äî Substitute $w = j\omega_w$:** $$G(j\omega_w) = \frac{0.632 - 0.632j\omega_w + 0.104j\omega_w - 0.104(j\omega_w)^2}{2j\omega_w(0.632 + 1.368j\omega_w)} = \frac{(0.632 + 0.104\omega_w^2) + j(0.104\omega_w - 0.632\omega_w)}{2j\omega_w(0.632 + 1.368j\omega_w)}$$ Simplifying numerator: $(0.632 + 0.104\omega_w^2) - j\,0.528\omega_w$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Magnitude expression:** $$\vert G(j\omega_w) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{(0.632 + 0.104\omega_w^2)^2 + (0.528\omega_w)^2}}{2\omega_w\sqrt{0.632^2 + (1.368\omega_w)^2}}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Phase expression:** $$\angle G(j\omega_w) = -90¬∞ + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.528\omega_w}{0.632 + 0.104\omega_w^2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.368\omega_w}{0.632}\right)$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Compute $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 1):** Setting $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ and solving numerically: $$\omega_{gc} \approx 0.62 \text{ rad/s (w-plane)}$$ Converting to physical frequency using $\omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan^{-1}(\omega_w)$ with $T = 0.5$ s: $$\omega = 4 \tan^{-1}(0.62) = 4 \times 0.5546 = 2.218 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Compute $\omega_{pc}$ (phase = ‚àí180¬∞):** Solving $-90¬∞ + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.368\omega_{pc}}{0.632}\right) - 90¬∞ = -180¬∞$: $$\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.368\omega_{pc}}{0.632}\right) = 0¬∞$$ This gives $\omega_{pc} \to 0$ for the +90¬∞ branch, so we look for the actual ‚àí180¬∞ crossing at higher frequencies: $$\omega_{pc} \approx 1.85 \text{ rad/s (w-plane)}$$ Physical: $\omega = 4\tan^{-1}(1.85) \approx 4 \times 1.073 = 4.29$ rad/s. At this frequency, $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \approx 0.398$. **Step 6 ‚Äî Compute margins:** $$PM = 180¬∞ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = 180¬∞ + (-135¬∞) = 45¬∞$$ $$GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = -20\log_{10}(0.398) = 8.0 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 7 ‚Äî Stability verdict:** | Metric | Value | Criterion | Verdict | |--------|-------|-----------|---------| | PM | 45¬∞ | > 0¬∞ | ‚úî STABLE | | GM | 8 dB | > 0 dB | ‚úî STABLE | $$\boxed{\text{The system is STABLE with PM} = 45¬∞, \text{GM} = 8 \text{ dB}}$$ --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 3** - [ ] **Root locus is in the z-plane** for digital systems; the stability region is the **inside of the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$), NOT the left half-plane. - [ ] **Angle condition:** $\pm 180¬∞(2k+1)$; **Magnitude condition:** $\vert G(z) \vert = 1$. - [ ] **Number of branches = number of open-loop poles.** - [ ] **Real-axis locus:** odd number of real poles + zeros to the right of the test segment. - [ ] **Asymptote centroid:** $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$. Always include **complex conjugate poles as 2 Re(p)** in the sum. - [ ] **Asymptote angles:** $\theta_k = \dfrac{\pm 180¬∞(2k+1)}{n - m}$, for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n - m - 1$. - [ ] **Breakaway equation:** set $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{D(z)}{N(z)}$. Verify the root lies on a valid locus segment. - [ ] **Angle of departure** from a complex pole $p_l$ uses the formula with $\pm 180¬∞$ plus sum of angles to other poles and zeros. - [ ] **Stability bound in z-plane:** All closed-loop poles must satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$. Crossings of $\vert z \vert = 1$ give the critical K. - [ ] **Bilinear transformation:** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ is the bridge between z-plane and w-plane for frequency analysis. - [ ] **Frequency mapping:** $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ ‚Äî nonlinear warping. Always convert back to physical $\omega$ when reporting results. - [ ] **Bode/Nyquist tools apply on G(jœâ_w)** after substituting $w = j\omega_w$. - [ ] **PM and GM must both be positive** for stability; typical design specs are PM $\geq 45¬∞$ and GM $\geq 6$ dB. - [ ] In sketches: **mark poles with X, zeros with O**, label breakaway points with their z-values, draw asymptote centroid and angles.

topics.title: Bode Plots, Gain Margin and Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bode Plots, Gain Margin and Phase Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bode Plot** is a pair of semi-logarithmic graphical representations of the frequency response of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system. It consists of two plots drawn against a common horizontal axis of angular frequency $\omega$ (plotted on a logarithmic scale):

1. **Magnitude Plot** ‚Äî The logarithmic gain $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert_{dB}$ in decibels (dB) versus $\log_{10}(\omega)$.
2. **Phase Plot** ‚Äî The phase angle $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ in degrees versus $\log_{10}(\omega)$.

The **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** are frequency-domain stability metrics derived directly from the Bode plot of the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The Bode plot is a *graphical* frequency-domain technique, complementary to the Nyquist plot. It is preferred in industry because logarithmic scale permits construction via **straight-line asymptotes** rather than point-by-point computation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Audio Equalizer" Intuition

Imagine you are an audio engineer with a graphic equalizer in front of you. Each slider on the equalizer represents how a sound system amplifies or attenuates a particular frequency band ‚Äî bass (low $\omega$), mid (medium $\omega$), and treble (high $\omega$).

A **Bode plot is essentially a precise mathematical "snapshot" of an equalizer**:

- The **magnitude curve** tells you *how loud* (in dB) each frequency comes out relative to what went in.
- The **phase curve** tells you *how much time delay* each frequency suffers ‚Äî bass notes may exit the speaker slightly later than the treble.

Now, suppose you have a *feedback* amplifier (a microphone picking up the speaker output and feeding it back). If certain frequencies are amplified *too much* (high gain) and *delayed* (phase shift near $-180^\circ$), the system may turn into a screeching, self-sustaining oscillator ‚Äî **instability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gain Margin (GM):** "By how much can I *crank up the volume* (gain) before the system starts oscillating?"
>
> **Phase Margin (PM):** "By how much extra *phase shift* (time delay) can the system tolerate before becoming unstable?"

These two margins are the **safety buffers** engineers design into every closed-loop system ‚Äî from a cruise controller in a car to the autopilot in a Boeing 737.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Decibel** | dB | $20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ |
| **Decade** | ‚Äî | A factor-of-10 change in frequency (e.g., $1$ rad/s to $10$ rad/s) |
| **Octave** | ‚Äî | A factor-of-2 change in frequency (e.g., $100$ rad/s to $200$ rad/s) |
| **Corner / Break Frequency** | $\omega_c$ | Frequency at which the asymptote slope changes |
| **Gain Crossover Frequency** | $\omega_{gc}$ | Frequency at which $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 0$ dB |
| **Phase Crossover Frequency** | $\omega_{pc}$ | Frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$ |
| **Gain Margin** | $GM$ | $-\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB}$ |
| **Phase Margin** | $PM$ | $180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Bode Plots? The Underlying Philosophy

Bode's foundational theorem (Hendrik Wade Bode, 1940) states that for a **minimum-phase system** (no right-half-plane poles or zeros), the magnitude and phase of $G(j\omega)$ are *uniquely related* via the **Bode integral theorem**. This means:

> Knowing the magnitude curve *completely determines* the phase curve (and vice versa) for minimum-phase systems.

The practical engineering value of Bode plots rests on three pillars:

1. **Asymptotic Construction** ‚Äî Magnitude curves can be drawn using *straight lines* with slopes that change only at corner frequencies. No calculators needed for the basic sketch.
2. **Multiplicative Simplicity** ‚Äî Products in the frequency domain become *additions* in dB.
3. **Direct Stability Readout** ‚Äî GM and PM can be read off the plot by inspection.

## 2.2 The Standard Bode Form of a Transfer Function

A general open-loop transfer function is first normalized into **Bode (time-constant) form**:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \left(1 + \dfrac{s}{z_1}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{z_2}\right)\cdots\left(1 + 2\zeta_a \dfrac{s}{\omega_a} + \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_a^2}\right)\cdots}{s^N \left(1 + \dfrac{s}{p_1}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{p_2}\right)\cdots\left(1 + 2\zeta_b \dfrac{s}{\omega_b} + \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_b^2}\right)\cdots}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ allows each factor's contribution to magnitude and phase to be analyzed independently.

## 2.3 Contribution of Each Standard Factor

| Factor Type | Asymptotic Magnitude Slope | Phase Contribution (at $\omega \gg \omega_c$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Constant gain $K$ | $20 \log_{10} K$ dB (flat) | $0^\circ$ |
| Pole at origin, $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $-20$ dB/decade | $-90^\circ$ |
| Zero at origin, $s$ | $+20$ dB/decade | $+90^\circ$ |
| Simple pole, $\dfrac{1}{1+s/\omega_p}$ | $0$ then $-20$ dB/dec after $\omega_p$ | $0^\circ \rightarrow -90^\circ$ |
| Simple zero, $1+s/\omega_z$ | $0$ then $+20$ dB/dec after $\omega_z$ | $0^\circ \rightarrow +90^\circ$ |
| Quadratic pole | $0$ then $-40$ dB/dec after $\omega_p$ | $0^\circ \rightarrow -180^\circ$ |
| Quadratic zero | $0$ then $+40$ dB/dec after $\omega_z$ | $0^\circ \rightarrow +180^\circ$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Slope Conventions Used in KTU Valuation:**
> - $20$ dB/decade $\equiv 6$ dB/octave
> - Phase transitions for simple poles/zeros occur smoothly between $\omega_c/10$ and $10\omega_c$, centered on $\omega_c$. At $\omega = \omega_c$, the phase shift is $\pm 45^\circ$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnitude in dB: } & \vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert \\[4pt]
\text{Phase: } & \angle G(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{G(j\omega)\}}{\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Gain Crossover: } & \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 0 \text{ dB} \\[4pt]
\text{Phase Crossover: } & \angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ \\[6pt]
\text{Gain Margin: } & GM = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \\[4pt]
\text{Phase Margin: } & PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \\[6pt]
\text{Phase of }(1+j\omega/\omega_c): & \phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_c) \\[4pt]
\text{Phase of quadratic (underdamped): } & \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)}{1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Resonant peak } M_r: & M_r = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}, \quad 0 < \zeta < \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{Bandwidth } \omega_b: & \vert G(j\omega_b) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \vert G(j0) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Stability Verdict from Bode Plot:**
> - If $GM > 0$ dB (i.e., $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB} < 0$) **AND** $PM > 0^\circ$ ‚Üí **Stable closed-loop system**.
> - If either margin is negative ‚Üí **Unstable closed-loop system**.
> - Typical design targets in industry: $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM$ between $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bode plots are the **universal language of control engineers** because:

- **Antenna & RF Engineering** ‚Äî Designing amplifiers with flat gain over a wide frequency band.
- **Servo & Robotics** ‚Äî Tuning PID gains to achieve the right balance of speed and overshoot.
- **Audio Engineering** ‚Äî Equalizer and crossover network design.
- **Power Electronics** ‚Äî Stability of DC-DC converters and grid-tied inverters.
- **Aerospace** ‚Äî Phase margin specifications are directly tied to pilot handling qualities (e.g., MIL-F-8785C specifies $PM \geq 45^\circ$ for Level 1 flying qualities).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Phase and Magnitude of Standard Factors

### Case 1: Simple Pole $\dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega/\omega_p}$

Substitute $s = j\omega$ into the pole:

$$G_1(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega/\omega_p}$$

**Magnitude derivation:**

$$\vert G_1(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega/\omega_p)^2}}$$

Taking $20 \log_{10}$:

$$\vert G_1(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = -20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_p)^2}$$

This is **approximated** by two straight-line asymptotes:

- For $\omega \ll \omega_p$: $(\omega/\omega_p)^2 \approx 0$, so $\vert G_1 \vert_{dB} \approx -20 \log_{10}(1) = 0$ dB. **Slope = 0 dB/dec.**
- For $\omega \gg \omega_p$: $\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_p)^2} \approx \omega/\omega_p$, so $\vert G_1 \vert_{dB} \approx -20 \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_p) = -20 \log_{10}\omega + 20\log_{10}\omega_p$. **Slope = $-20$ dB/dec.**

**Phase derivation:**

$$\angle G_1(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega/\omega_p}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_p)$$

The phase smoothly transitions from $0^\circ$ (at low $\omega$) to $-90^\circ$ (at high $\omega$), passing through $-45^\circ$ at $\omega = \omega_p$.

### Case 2: Pole at Origin $\dfrac{1}{s^N}$

$$G_2(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(j\omega)^N} = \frac{1}{\omega^N}\angle(-N \cdot 90^\circ)$$

$$\vert G_2 \vert_{dB} = -20 N \log_{10}(\omega) \quad \text{(a straight line of slope } -20N \text{ dB/dec)}$$

$$\angle G_2 = -90^\circ \cdot N \quad \text{(constant for all } \omega\text{)}$$

### Case 3: Quadratic (Underdamped) Pole

$$G_3(s) = \frac{1}{1 + 2\zeta(s/\omega_n) + (s/\omega_n)^2}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$G_3(j\omega) = \frac{1}{\left(1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right) + j\left(2\zeta\omega/\omega_n\right)}$$

**Magnitude:**

$$\vert G_3(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta\omega/\omega_n\right)^2}}$$

**Phase:**

$$\angle G_3(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\omega/\omega_n}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right)$$

The phase transitions from $0^\circ$ to $-180^\circ$, passing through $-90^\circ$ at $\omega = \omega_n$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gain Margin and Phase Margin

### Gain Margin Derivation

The phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ is defined by:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

At $\omega = \omega_{pc}$, evaluate the magnitude in dB:

$$\text{Magnitude at } \omega_{pc} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB}$$

The **gain margin** is defined as the additional gain (in dB) that would bring the system to the verge of instability, i.e., that would make the magnitude curve cross 0 dB *exactly* at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$GM = 0 - \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB} = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB}$$

In **linear (non-dB) form**:

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

### Phase Margin Derivation

The gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ is defined by:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

The phase angle at $\omega_{gc}$ is generally *more negative* than $-180^\circ$ for a stable system. The **phase margin** is the additional phase lag the system can tolerate before the total phase reaches $-180^\circ$:

$$PM = -180^\circ - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

Equivalently:

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

## 3.3 Worked Example ‚Äî Step-by-Step Bode Plot Construction

**Problem:** Construct the asymptotic Bode plot for

$$G(s) = \frac{10(1 + 0.5s)}{s(1 + 0.1s)(1 + 0.01s)}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert to Bode (time-constant) form:**

$$G(s) = \frac{10 \cdot (1 + s/2)}{s \cdot (1 + s/10) \cdot (1 + s/100)}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Identify corner frequencies and their contributions:**

| Factor | Corner Frequency | Slope Change | Phase Contribution |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $K = 10$ | ‚Äî | $0$ dB/dec | $0^\circ$ |
| $1/s$ | ‚Äî | $-20$ dB/dec (initial) | $-90^\circ$ (constant) |
| $1 + s/2$ (zero) | $\omega_z = 2$ rad/s | $+20$ dB/dec | $0^\circ \rightarrow +90^\circ$ |
| $1/(1 + s/10)$ (pole) | $\omega_p = 10$ rad/s | $-20$ dB/dec | $0^\circ \rightarrow -90^\circ$ |
| $1/(1 + s/100)$ (pole) | $\omega_p = 100$ rad/s | $-20$ dB/dec | $0^\circ \rightarrow -90^\circ$ |

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute initial magnitude at $\omega = 1$ rad/s (low-frequency asymptote):**

For $\omega < 2$ rad/s, the constant gain and the $1/s$ term dominate. At $\omega = 1$ rad/s:

$$\vert G(j1) \vert_{dB} \approx 20 \log_{10}(10) - 20 \log_{10}(1) = 20 \text{ dB}$$

So the low-frequency asymptote starts at **$+20$ dB at $\omega = 1$ rad/s** with a slope of $-20$ dB/decade.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Track slope changes through the frequency range:**

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Active Slope (dB/dec) | Magnitude at End of Decade |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\omega < 2$ | $-20$ | At $\omega = 1$: $20$ dB |
| $2 < \omega < 10$ | $-20 + 20 = 0$ | At $\omega = 10$: $20$ dB (unchanged) |
| $10 < \omega < 100$ | $0 - 20 = -20$ | At $\omega = 100$: $20 - 20\log_{10}(10) = 0$ dB |
| $\omega > 100$ | $-20 - 20 = -40$ | At $\omega = 1000$: $0 - 40\log_{10}(10) = -40$ dB |

**Step 5 ‚Äî Find gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$:**

From the table, the magnitude curve crosses $0$ dB at exactly $\omega_{gc} = 100$ rad/s.

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute phase at $\omega_{gc} = 100$ rad/s:**

$$\angle G(j100) = \angle(1 + j50) - 90^\circ - \angle(1 + j10) - \angle(1 + j1)$$

$$= \tan^{-1}(50) - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(10) - \tan^{-1}(1)$$

$$= 88.85^\circ - 90^\circ - 84.29^\circ - 45.00^\circ$$

$$\angle G(j100) = -130.44^\circ$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j100) = 180^\circ - 130.44^\circ = 49.56^\circ$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Find phase crossover $\omega_{pc}$:**

We need $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$. Since the phase at $\omega = 100$ rad/s is $-130.44^\circ$ and continues to lag further as $\omega$ increases (each simple pole contributes an additional $-90^\circ$ at high frequency), the phase reaches $-180^\circ$ somewhere beyond $\omega = 100$ rad/s. Numerically solving:

$$PM \approx 49.6^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{System is STABLE with reasonable margin}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The example shows how asymptotic Bode plots yield a *closed-loop* performance estimate (PM $\approx 50^\circ$ ‚Üí moderate damping, ~ $17\%$ overshoot) using only a hand sketch and a calculator. This is why the technique is taught universally.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bode Plot Construction Pipeline

The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates the *engineering workflow* for constructing a Bode plot from a given transfer function and extracting GM/PM. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (per the safeguard rules) rather than a hand-drawn graph, since the asymptotic Bode plot itself is a 2D coordinate plot that Mermaid cannot render with full mathematical fidelity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Transfer Function G(s)H(s)] --> B[Step 1: Convert to Bode Time-Constant Form]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Corner Frequencies and Pole-Zero Types]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Low-Frequency Asymptote at omega = 1 rad/s]
    D --> E[Step 4: Plot Straight-Line Asymptotes with Slope Changes]
    E --> F[Step 5: Add 3 dB Corrections Near Each Corner Frequency]
    F --> G[Step 6: Identify Gain Crossover omega_gc from Magnitude Plot]
    G --> H[Step 7: Identify Phase Crossover omega_pc from Phase Plot]
    H --> I{Magnitude at omega_pc Negative?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Gain Margin GM = Positive value in dB]
    I -->|No| K[System Potentially Unstable - GM Negative]
    H --> L[Step 8: Read Phase Angle at omega_gc]
    L --> M{PM = 180 + Phase > 0?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Phase Margin PM Positive - Stable]
    M -->|No| O[System Unstable - PM Negative]
    J --> P[Final Stability Verdict and Design Recommendation]
    K --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Band Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW["Low Frequency Band (omega less than omega_z)"]
        L1[Constant Gain K] --> L2[Integrator Action 1/s]
    end
    subgraph MID["Mid Frequency Band (omega_z to omega_p)"]
        M1[Zero Compensation +20 dB/dec] --> M2[Lead Network Behavior]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["High Frequency Band (omega greater than omega_p)"]
        H1[Pole Roll-off -20 dB/dec] --> H2[Attenuation of Noise]
    end
    LOW --> MID
    MID --> HIGH
    H2 --> S[Closed-Loop Stability Determined by GM and PM]
```

## 4.3 Anatomy of a Bode Plot ‚Äî Annotated Coordinate Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Horizontal Axis: log10 omega] --> A1[Each decade = same physical width]
    B[Vertical Axis Left: Magnitude in dB] --> B1[20 log10 magnitude]
    C[Vertical Axis Right: Phase in degrees] --> C1[Linear in degrees]
    A1 --> D[Reference Grid: 0 dB line and -180 degree line]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Plot Intersection: Gain Crossover omega_gc]
    D --> F[Plot Intersection: Phase Crossover omega_pc]
    E --> G[Phase Margin = Horizontal Distance from -180 line to phase curve at omega_gc]
    F --> H[Gain Margin = Vertical Distance from 0 dB line to magnitude curve at omega_pc]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Bode Plot Correctly (KTU Exam Tip):**
> - The **Gain Margin (GM)** is a *vertical* measurement on the magnitude plot, taken at the *phase crossover* frequency.
> - The **Phase Margin (PM)** is a *horizontal* (phase-axis) measurement on the phase plot, taken at the *gain crossover* frequency.
> - These two margins are measured at *different* frequencies ‚Äî a common student mistake is to confuse them.
