# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain $G(s)H(s)$ may be increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability, i.e., before the Nyquist plot passes through the critical point $(-1, 0)$. In Bode plot terms:

$$GM = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB}$$

where $\omega_{pc}$ is the **phase crossover frequency** at which $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$.

**Phase Margin (PM):** It is defined as the additional phase lag (measured in degrees) that can be introduced into the open-loop transfer function at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. In Bode plot terms:

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

where $\omega_{gc}$ is the **gain crossover frequency** at which $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 0$ dB.

A system is **stable** if $GM > 0$ dB *and* $PM > 0^\circ$.

---

### Question 2: What is a Corner Frequency in a Bode Plot? State its significance. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **Corner Frequency** (also called *break frequency* or *cutoff frequency*) is the frequency at which the asymptotic magnitude plot of a transfer function changes its slope. Mathematically, it corresponds to the magnitude of a pole or zero of the transfer function.

**Significance:**

1. **Slope Change Point** ‚Äî At each corner frequency, the contribution of the corresponding factor (pole or zero) transitions from "negligible" to "dominant", causing the asymptotic slope to change by $\pm 20$ dB/decade (for a simple pole/zero) or $\pm 40$ dB/decade (for a quadratic pole/zero pair).

2. **Phase Transition Center** ‚Äî For a simple pole/zero, the phase contribution is exactly $\mp 45^\circ$ at $\omega = \omega_c$ and approaches $\mp 90^\circ$ asymptotically.

3. **Stability Boundary Indicator** ‚Äî The relative positions of corner frequencies with respect to the gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$ determine whether the system has adequate phase margin.

4. **Design Tuning Knob** ‚Äî Compensator design (lead, lag, lead-lag) is fundamentally about placing new poles and zeros (corner frequencies) to reshape the Bode plot for desired GM/PM.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Bode Plot Construction with Stability Analysis [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is

$$G(s) = \frac{20}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.05s)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the asymptotic Bode magnitude and phase plots. Determine the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ and the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the Bode plot, determine the Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM). Comment on the closed-loop stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert to Bode form:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{20}{j\omega \cdot (1 + j0.5\omega)(1 + j0.05\omega)}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Identify corner frequencies:**

- $\omega_{p1} = 2$ rad/s (from $1 + 0.5s = 1 + s/2$)
- $\omega_{p2} = 20$ rad/s (from $1 + 0.05s = 1 + s/20$)
- Integrator pole at origin contributes constant $-90^\circ$ phase and $-20$ dB/dec slope.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Low-frequency asymptote at $\omega = 1$ rad/s:**

$$\vert G(j1) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(20) - 20 \log_{10}(1) = 26.02 \text{ dB}$$

Initial slope: $-20$ dB/decade.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Track magnitude slopes through frequency bands:**

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Slope (dB/dec) | Magnitude at Range End |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $1$ to $2$ | $-20$ | $26.02 - 20 \log_{10}(2) = 19.99 \approx 20$ dB |
| $2$ to $20$ | $-20 - 20 = -40$ | $20 - 40 \log_{10}(10) = -20$ dB |
| $20$ onwards | $-40 - 20 = -60$ | Continues to roll off |

**Step 5 ‚Äî Find gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$ from magnitude plot:**

The magnitude crosses $0$ dB somewhere between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 20$ rad/s. Linear interpolation:

Starting at $\omega = 2$ rad/s with $20$ dB, slope $-40$ dB/dec:

$$0 = 20 - 40 \log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/2) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/2) = 0.5$$

$$\omega_{gc} = 2 \times 10^{0.5} = 2\sqrt{10} \approx 6.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Phase plot calculation at representative frequencies:**

Using the exact phase expression:

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.05\omega)$$

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | Phase (degrees) |
| :---: | :---: |
| $0.1$ | $-90 - 2.86 - 0.29 = -93.15$ |
| $1$ | $-90 - 26.57 - 2.86 = -119.43$ |
| $2$ | $-90 - 45 - 5.71 = -140.71$ |
| $10$ | $-90 - 78.69 - 26.57 = -195.26$ |
| $20$ | $-90 - 84.29 - 45 = -219.29$ |
| $100$ | $-90 - 88.85 - 78.69 = -257.54$ |

**Step 7 ‚Äî Find phase crossover $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180^\circ$):**

From the table, $\omega_{pc}$ lies between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 10$ rad/s. Linear interpolation between $\omega = 2$ ($-140.71^\circ$) and $\omega = 10$ ($-195.26^\circ$):

$$-180 = -140.71 + \frac{-195.26 - (-140.71)}{10 - 2}(\omega_{pc} - 2)$$

$$-180 = -140.71 - 6.82(\omega_{pc} - 2)$$

$$\omega_{pc} - 2 = \frac{39.29}{6.82} = 5.76 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} \approx 7.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 8 ‚Äî Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} \approx 7.76$ rad/s:**

Using the slope-based calculation. Between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega_{pc} = 7.76$ rad/s, slope is $-40$ dB/dec:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB} = 20 - 40 \log_{10}(7.76/2) = 20 - 40 \log_{10}(3.88) = 20 - 23.57 = -3.57 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Compute Gain Margin:**

$$GM = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB} = -(-3.57) = +3.57 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 10 ‚Äî Phase at $\omega_{gc} \approx 6.32$ rad/s:**

$$\angle G(j6.32) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 6.32) - \tan^{-1}(0.05 \times 6.32)$$

$$= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(3.16) - \tan^{-1}(0.316)$$

$$= -90^\circ - 72.43^\circ - 17.55^\circ = -179.98^\circ \approx -180^\circ$$

**Step 11 ‚Äî Compute Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = 180^\circ - 180^\circ \approx 0^\circ$$

**Step 12 ‚Äî Stability Verdict:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The system has $GM \approx +3.57$ dB and $PM \approx 0^\circ$. The closed-loop system is **marginally stable / practically unstable** (highly oscillatory, sustained ringing, large overshoot). This is *not acceptable* for most engineering applications. A compensator (typically a lead network) is required to increase the phase margin to at least $30^\circ$‚Äì$45^\circ$.

---

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question [Internal Choice Option] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback control system is

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+10)}$$

**(a)** Determine the value of $K$ for which the system has a Phase Margin of $45^\circ$. Use asymptotic Bode plot construction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the value of $K$ obtained in (a), compute the Gain Margin and the resonant peak $M_r$ of the closed-loop system. State whether the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert to time-constant form:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega \cdot (j\omega + 4)(j\omega + 10)} = \frac{K/40}{j\omega \cdot (1 + j\omega/4)(1 + j\omega/10)}$$

Let $K' = K/40$. Initial slope is $-20$ dB/dec starting from $\omega = 1$ rad/s with magnitude $20\log_{10}(K')$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Corner frequencies:** $\omega_{p1} = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_{p2} = 10$ rad/s.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Assume gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$ lies between $4$ and $10$ rad/s:**

For $PM = 45^\circ$, the phase at $\omega_{gc}$ must be:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = 180^\circ - 45^\circ = -135^\circ$$

The phase expression is:

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega/4) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/10)$$

Setting equal to $-135^\circ$:

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}/4) + \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}/10) = 45^\circ$$

Using the tangent addition formula:

$$\tan(45^\circ) = 1 = \frac{\omega_{gc}/4 + \omega_{gc}/10}{1 - \omega_{gc}^2/40} = \frac{(14\omega_{gc}/40)}{(40 - \omega_{gc}^2)/40} = \frac{14\omega_{gc}}{40 - \omega_{gc}^2}$$

Therefore:

$$40 - \omega_{gc}^2 = 14\omega_{gc} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{gc}^2 + 14\omega_{gc} - 40 = 0$$

$$\omega_{gc} = \frac{-14 + \sqrt{196 + 160}}{2} = \frac{-14 + \sqrt{356}}{2} = \frac{-14 + 18.87}{2} \approx 2.43 \text{ rad/s}$$

This is **below** $\omega_{p1} = 4$ rad/s, so our initial assumption was wrong.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Assume $\omega_{gc} < 4$ rad/s:**

In this region, only the integrator and the gain $K'$ matter, so the magnitude asymptote is:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(K') - 20\log_{10}(\omega) = 0 \text{ dB at } \omega = \omega_{gc}$$

Hence $\omega_{gc} = K'$ (in rad/s, on linear scale).

Substituting into the phase equation:

$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(K'/4) - \tan^{-1}(K'/10) = -135^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(K'/4) + \tan^{-1}(K'/10) = 45^\circ$$

$$\frac{K'/4 + K'/10}{1 - K'^2/40} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{14K'/40}{(40-K'^2)/40} = 1$$

$$14K' = 40 - K'^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K'^2 + 14K' - 40 = 0$$

$$K' = \frac{-14 + \sqrt{196 + 160}}{2} = 2.43$$

So $K' = 2.43$ and $\omega_{gc} = 2.43$ rad/s. Since $2.43 < 4$, the assumption is self-consistent.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute $K$:**

$$K = 40 \cdot K' = 40 \times 2.43 \approx 97.2 \text{ (taking } K \approx 97\text{)}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 6 ‚Äî Find phase crossover $\omega_{pc}$:**

We need $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$, which requires:

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/4) + \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/10) = 90^\circ$$

The tangent of $90^\circ$ is undefined, so the denominator of the tangent addition formula must equal zero:

$$1 - \omega_{pc}^2/40 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{40} = 6.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = 6.32$ rad/s:**

$$\vert G(j6.32) \vert = \frac{2.43}{6.32 \cdot \sqrt{1 + (6.32/4)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (6.32/10)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{2.43}{6.32 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 2.50} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.40}}$$

$$= \frac{2.43}{6.32 \cdot 1.871 \cdot 1.183} = \frac{2.43}{13.99} = 0.1737$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Compute Gain Margin:**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \frac{1}{0.1737} = 5.76$$

In dB: $GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5.76) = 15.21$ dB.

**Step 9 ‚Äî Approximate the closed-loop system as a 2nd-order system to find $M_r$:**

The relationship between phase margin and damping ratio for a 2nd-order system is:

$$\zeta \approx \frac{PM \text{ (in degrees)}}{100} = \frac{45}{100} = 0.45$$

(More precisely, $\zeta = \frac{PM}{100}$ for $PM$ in degrees, a commonly used approximation.)

The resonant peak:

$$M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.45)\sqrt{1-0.2025}} = \frac{1}{0.9 \times 0.8930} = \frac{1}{0.804} = 1.244$$

**Step 10 ‚Äî Stability Verdict:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The system has $GM = +15.21$ dB and $PM = 45^\circ$ (by design). The closed-loop system is **stable** with good relative stability. $M_r \approx 1.24$ corresponds to approximately $23\%$ overshoot, suitable for many control applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on Bode Plot questions (KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall List):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$** ‚Äî GM is measured at $\omega_{pc}$ (vertical, on magnitude plot), PM is measured at $\omega_{gc}$ (horizontal, on phase plot). Marks deducted if these are swapped.
>
> 2. **Sign error in PM formula** ‚Äî The correct formula is $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$, *not* $PM = 180^\circ - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$. Since the phase is *negative*, both forms happen to work numerically, but the *sign convention* is frequently marked.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to add 3 dB correction at corner frequencies** ‚Äî Asymptotic Bode plots have a known $-3$ dB dip at each simple pole corner. While the asymptotic approach is acceptable, the *exact* magnitude at the corner should be reported if asked.
>
> 4. **Not converting to Bode time-constant form first** ‚Äî Drawing the plot from the polynomial form $s^2 + 2s + 1$ instead of $1 + 2s + s^2$ leads to wrong corner frequencies.
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting the sign of GM for stability** ‚Äî A *positive* GM (in dB) means stable. A negative GM means unstable. Many students report a "negative GM" as a positive number.
>
> 6. **Phase angle arithmetic errors** ‚Äî Each simple pole contributes $-\tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_c)$; each zero contributes $+\tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_c)$. Sign mistakes here cascade into wrong PM/GM values.
>
> 7. **Skipping the stability verdict** ‚Äî KTU marking scheme allocates at least **1 mark** in 14-mark questions specifically for the *qualitative* statement: "The system is stable / unstable / marginally stable with a margin of X."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Bode Plot Definition:** Pair of semi-log plots ‚Äî magnitude (dB) and phase (degrees) vs. $\log_{10}\omega$.
- **Asymptotic Slope Rule:** Each pole contributes $-20$ dB/dec; each zero contributes $+20$ dB/dec; integrator pole at origin gives $-20$ dB/dec from the start.
- **Corner Frequency:** $\omega_c = 1/T$ where $T$ is the time constant in $(1 + Ts)$. At $\omega = \omega_c$, the phase shift is $\mp 45^\circ$ (for simple pole/zero).
- **Gain Crossover $\omega_{gc}$:** Frequency where magnitude = 0 dB. **Phase Margin is measured here.**
- **Phase Crossover $\omega_{pc}$:** Frequency where phase = $-180^\circ$. **Gain Margin is measured here.**
- **Gain Margin Formula:** $GM_{dB} = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{dB}$. Positive GM ‚Üí stable.
- **Phase Margin Formula:** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$. Positive PM ‚Üí stable.
- **Minimum-Phase Systems:** For these, magnitude and phase plots are *uniquely* related ‚Äî a Bode plot suffices for full stability analysis.
- **Non-Minimum-Phase Warning:** If the system has RHP poles or zeros, the Bode plot may indicate stability even when the closed-loop system is unstable. Always cross-verify with Nyquist or Routh-Hurwitz for non-minimum-phase plants.
- **Industry Standard Design Targets:** $GM \geq 6$ dB; $PM \approx 45^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ (yields ~ $15\%$ to $25\%$ overshoot, well-damped response).
- **Phase Margin vs Damping Ratio (2nd-order approximation):** $\zeta \approx PM/100$ (PM in degrees).
- **Resonant Peak:** $M_r = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for $0 < \zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Bandwidth:** Frequency at which $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \vert G(j0) \vert / \sqrt{2}$ (the $-3$ dB point).
- **Bode Plot Advantages Over Nyquist:** (i) Easy asymptotic construction, (ii) Handles high-order systems comfortably, (iii) GM/PM read off directly, (iv) Cascaded systems add in dB.
- **Bode Plot Disadvantages:** Less intuitive for non-minimum-phase systems; phase plot is less informative for time-delay-dominated systems.
- **Units Reminder:** $\omega$ in rad/s, magnitude in dB, phase in degrees.

### Key Takeaway in One Line

> *"The Bode plot converts a complex frequency-response analysis into straight-line asymptotes, and the Gain Margin / Phase Margin read off this plot are the most widely used engineering metrics for quantifying how 'far' a closed-loop system sits from the brink of instability."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Space Representation of Discrete Time Systems, Transfer Function from State Space Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Space Representation of Discrete Time Systems, Transfer Function from State Space Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **State Space Representation** of a discrete-time (sampled-data) system is a time-domain, matrix-based mathematical model that fully describes the internal dynamic behaviour and the input-output relationship of a linear, time-invariant (LTI) digital control system. It replaces the single, high-order difference equation (or pulse transfer function) by a set of coupled, first-order matrix difference equations.

For a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) discrete-time system, the **standard canonical state-space model** is given by:

$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)$$

where:
- $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **state vector** (snapshot of all internal memory at sample $k$).
- $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the **input vector**.
- $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times 1}$ is the **output vector**.
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **system (state transition) matrix**.
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the **input (control) matrix**.
- $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ is the **output matrix**.
- $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ is the **direct feed-through (feedforward) matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on converting difference equations (the discrete-time equivalent of differential equations) into state-space form, and then deriving the pulse transfer function $G(z)$ back from the matrices $A, B, C, D$. This duality forms the analytical backbone of all modern digital control design (pole-placement, observers, LQR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrete-Time vs Continuous-Time:** Every $t$ in continuous-time becomes $kT$ (or simply $k$ for normalized sampling period $T = 1$). The derivative $\dot{x}$ becomes the forward shift $x(k+1)$, and the Laplace operator $s$ becomes the Z-transform operator $z$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Black-Box Memory" Analogy**
> Imagine a digital vending machine. You cannot tell its state by merely watching the coins go in (input) and the snacks come out (output). Internally, it is tracking the *running total of coins*, *stock remaining*, and *last button pressed*. These three internal counters are its **states**. The **state-space model** is simply the *complete ledger* that tells you exactly how each of these counters updates each time a new coin arrives (the matrix equation $x(k+1) = Ax(k)+Bu(k)$), and how the display reading (output) is computed from these counters (the equation $y(k) = Cx(k)+Du(k)$).

In contrast, the **pulse transfer function** $G(z)$ is like describing the machine only by the *ratio* of snacks dispensed to coins inserted ‚Äî useful for I/O but it hides all internal dynamics. State-space exposes the internal "memory".

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- Sampling Period $T$ (units: **seconds**) ‚Äî must satisfy Shannon/Nyquist criterion: $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_{max}$.
- Order $n$ of the system equals the number of **independent energy storage elements** in discrete-time (e.g., digital delays, accumulators).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Constructing the Discrete State Model

To convert a general $n$-th order linear difference equation into state-space form, we use the **Phase Variable (Controllable Canonical) Method**:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the linear difference equation:**
$$y(k+n) + a_1 y(k+n-1) + a_2 y(k+n-2) + \dots + a_{n-1} y(k+1) + a_n y(k) = b_0 u(k+n) + b_1 u(k+n-1) + \dots + b_{n-1} u(k+1) + b_n u(k)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Define the phase variables (states):**
$$x_1(k) = y(k)$$
$$x_2(k) = y(k+1)$$
$$x_3(k) = y(k+2)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$x_n(k) = y(k+n-1)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Derive the state-update relations (Why?):** From the definitions, $x_1(k+1) = y(k+1) = x_2(k)$, so trivially:
$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = x_3(k)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$x_{n-1}(k+1) = x_n(k)$$

For the final state, use the original difference equation:
$$x_n(k+1) = -a_n x_1(k) - a_{n-1} x_2(k) - \dots - a_1 x_n(k) + b_0 u(k+n) + (b_1 - b_0 a_1)u(k+n-1) + \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Delayed Input" Pitfall:** The input $u(k+n)$ appears in the equation but is *not a state*. It must be shifted into past time using the shift property: this produces a modified $\tilde{B}$ matrix. KTU frequently tests this transformation.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Assemble matrices $A, B, C, D$.**

### 2.2 Deriving the Pulse Transfer Function from State Space

**Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the Z-transform to both state equations** (assuming zero initial conditions $x(0) = 0$):
$$zX(z) = A X(z) + B U(z)$$
$$Y(z) = C X(z) + D U(z)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Solve for $X(z)$ algebraically:**
$$(zI - A) X(z) = B U(z)$$
$$X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} B\, U(z)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute into the output equation:**
$$Y(z) = \left[\,C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D\,\right] U(z)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Identify the pulse transfer function:**
$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\,(zI - A)^{-1} B + D$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Expand the inverse using the Adjugate formula:**
$$(zI - A)^{-1} = \frac{\text{Adj}(zI - A)}{\det(zI - A)}$$

Hence:
$$G(z) = \frac{C\,\text{Adj}(zI - A)\,B + D\,\det(zI - A)}{\det(zI - A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight ‚Äî The Characteristic Polynomial:** The denominator of $G(z)$ is always $\det(zI - A)$. Therefore, the eigenvalues of $A$ are exactly the **poles** of the discrete system. This is a critical relationship for stability analysis: a discrete-time system is **BIBO stable** iff all eigenvalues of $A$ lie strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(k+1) = Ax(k) + Bu(k)$ | Discrete state equation (forward shift) |
| 2 | $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$ | Discrete output equation |
| 3 | $G(z) = C(zI-A)^{-1}B + D$ | Pulse Transfer Function from state space |
| 4 | $\det(zI - A) = 0$ | **Characteristic equation** ‚Üí poles of system |
| 5 | $\text{eigenvalues}(\lambda) \Rightarrow \vert \lambda \vert < 1$ | Discrete-time **stability condition** |
| 6 | $(zI - A)^{-1} = \dfrac{\text{Adj}(zI - A)}{\det(zI - A)}$ | Matrix inverse expansion |
| 7 | $x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B\,u(j)$ | General time-domain solution |
| 8 | $A^k = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\!\left[(zI - A)^{-1} z\right]$ | State transition matrix in $z$-domain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** State-space representation is the gateway to MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output) systems, Kalman filtering, observer design, and optimal control ‚Äî none of which can be handled by classical single transfer-function methods. In modern KTU labs (using MATLAB `ss()`, `ss2tf()`, `tf2ss()`), students routinely convert between these two representations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ‚Äî State Space from a 2nd-Order Difference Equation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Obtain the state-space representation in phase-variable form for the discrete-time system:
$$y(k+2) + 5\,y(k+1) + 6\,y(k) = 2\,u(k+2) + u(k+1)$$

#### Derivation

**Step 1:** Compare with the standard form to identify coefficients.
Here, $n = 2$, so $a_1 = 5$, $a_2 = 6$. On the RHS, $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 1$, $b_2 = 0$ (since no $u(k)$ term exists).

**Step 2:** Define the states as per the phase-variable method.
$$x_1(k) = y(k)$$
$$x_2(k) = y(k+1)$$

**Step 3:** Write the trivial update relations.
$$x_1(k+1) = y(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = y(k+2)$$

**Step 4:** Use the original difference equation to express $y(k+2)$:
$$x_2(k+1) = -5 y(k+1) - 6 y(k) + 2 u(k+2) + u(k+1)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = -5 x_2(k) - 6 x_1(k) + 2 u(k+2) + u(k+1)$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Critical step:** Eliminate the future input $u(k+2)$. The standard trick is to express everything using $u(k+1)$ by defining an *augmented state*, OR more commonly in KTU problems, to bring everything to a "delayed" representation by dividing the original equation by a reference. However, the *cleanest* method that KTU expects is the **Direct Programming (Phase-Variable) approach** which assumes $b_0 = 0$ (proper system, no direct feedthrough shift).

When $b_0 = 0$, the equation becomes:
$$y(k+2) + 5 y(k+1) + 6 y(k) = u(k+1)$$

So the state equations reduce cleanly to:
$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = -6 x_1(k) - 5 x_2(k) + u(k+1)$$

**Step 6:** Write in matrix form and identify $A, B, C, D$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(k) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + 0 \cdot u(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General $b_0 \neq 0$ case:** If $b_0 \neq 0$, KTU examiners often accept the **Observer/Controller Canonical Form** instead, because the simple phase-variable method fails. The controller canonical form yields the $A$ matrix with $\tilde{a}_i = a_i$ and an input matrix $\tilde{B}$ with entries $\tilde{b}_i = b_i - b_0 a_i$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ‚Äî Pulse Transfer Function from State Space

**Problem Statement:**
Given the discrete system:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

Find the pulse transfer function $G(z) = Y(z)/U(z)$.

#### Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1:** Form the resolvent matrix $(zI - A)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
zI - A &= z\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 0.5 & z-1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant (denominator of $G(z)$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(zI - A) &= z(z-1) - (-1)(0.5) = z^2 - z + 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the adjugate (transpose of cofactor matrix).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Adj}(zI - A) &= \begin{bmatrix} z-1 & 1 \\ -0.5 & z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(zI - A)^{-1} B$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(zI - A)^{-1} B &= \frac{1}{z^2 - z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} z-1 & 1 \\ -0.5 & z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 - z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $C$ on the left.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(zI - A)^{-1} B &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{z^2 - z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 - z + 0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Add $D = 0$ (no effect).

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{1}{z^2 - z + 0.5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification ‚Äî poles lie inside unit circle?** Roots of $z^2 - z + 0.5 = 0$ are $z = 0.5 \pm j0.5$, with magnitude $\sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.5^2} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707 < 1$. ‚úÖ System is **stable**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix ‚Äî Common KTU Test Cases

| Scenario | Given | Required | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Difference equation ‚Üí State space | $n$-th order difference eqn. | $A, B, C, D$ matrices | Phase Variable / Controller Canonical |
| (ii) State space ‚Üí Transfer function | $A, B, C, D$ | $G(z)$ rational function | $G(z) = C(zI-A)^{-1}B + D$ |
| (iii) Check discrete stability | $A$ matrix | Are $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$? | Compute $\det(zI - A) = 0$, find roots |
| (iv) State transition $A^k$ | $A$ matrix | $x(k)$ for any $k$ | Use Cayley-Hamilton or Z-transform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Discrete State Space Realization

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the data flow of a generic digital state-space system implemented as a real-time difference equation in a DSP/microcontroller. It depicts how the input $u(k)$ and the delayed state $x(k)$ interact inside the matrices $A$ and $B$, and how the output is formed via $C$ and $D$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uin[Input u of k] --> sumB(("+"))
    xk[State Vector x of k]
    xk --> mulA["Multiply by A matrix"]
    mulA --> sumA(("+"))
    sumA --> sumB
    sumB --> delay1["Unit Delay Z to the power minus 1"]
    delay1 --> xk
    delay1 --> mulC["Multiply by C matrix"]
    mulC --> sumD(("+"))
    uin --> mulD["Multiply by D matrix"]
    mulD --> sumD
    sumD --> yout[Output y of k]
    style uin fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style yout fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style mulA fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333
    style mulC fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333
    style mulD fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333
    style delay1 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The single "Unit Delay" block represents the entire $x(k) \rightarrow x(k+1)$ shift operation. In an actual $n$-th order system, this would be $n$ cascaded delay elements. Each delay stores one sample worth of memory, which physically corresponds to one state variable.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ‚Äî From Difference Equation to Transfer Function

This diagram captures the **bi-directional conversion** that KTU frequently tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Difference Equation nth order"] -->|"Define phase variables x1 to xn"| B["State Equations x k plus 1 = A x k + B u k"]
    B -->|"Take Z transform"| C["Algebraic Equations in z domain"]
    C -->|"Solve for X of z"| D["Matrix Inversion zI minus A inverse"]
    D -->|"Form Y of z over U of z"| E["Pulse Transfer Function G of z"]
    A -->|"Direct Z transform"| E
    E -->|"Factor denominator"| F["Characteristic Equation det zI minus A"]
    F -->|"Find roots"| G["Eigenvalues Poles of system"]
    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style B fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style C fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style D fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#803300
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#843c0c
    style G fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623
```

---

### 4.3 Canonical Form Schematic ‚Äî Controller Canonical Realization

For a 2nd-order example (extending to $n$-th order is straightforward), the **Controller Canonical Form** block diagram of $G(z)$ looks like this:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uk["u of k"] --> d0["Delay to z minus 1"]
    d0 --> sum1(("+"))
    d0 --> a1blk["Gain a1 negative"]
    sum1 --> d1["Delay to z minus 1"]
    d1 --> a2blk["Gain a2 negative"]
    a1blk --> sum1
    a2blk --> sum1
    d0 --> b0blk["Gain b0"]
    d1 --> b1blk["Gain b1"]
    a1blk --> b1blk
    a2blk --> b2blk["Gain b2"]
    d1 --> b2blk
    b0blk --> sumout(("+"))
    b1blk --> sumout
    b2blk --> sumout
    sumout --> yk["y of k"]
    style uk fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style yk fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style d0 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style d1 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each delay block in the canonical form corresponds to **one state variable**. The forward-path gains ($b_i$) and feedback gains ($a_i$) directly map to the $B$ and first column of $A$ matrices respectively. This is the structural heart of every KTU state-space problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**
*What is meant by the state-space representation of a discrete-time system? Write the general form of state and output equations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The state-space representation of a discrete-time system is a time-domain mathematical model that describes the system using a set of first-order matrix difference equations involving the state vector, input vector, and output vector.

General form:
$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k) \quad \text{(State equation)}$$
$$y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k) \quad \text{(Output equation)}$$

where $A, B, C, D$ are constant matrices of appropriate dimensions characterizing the system dynamics. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for equations with explanation)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
*State the formula for obtaining the pulse transfer function from a discrete state-space model. What is the significance of the matrix $(zI - A)$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pulse transfer function is given by:
$$G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D$$

Significance of $(zI - A)$: The determinant $\det(zI - A)$ gives the characteristic polynomial of the system. Its roots are the **eigenvalues** of $A$, which are the **poles** of the discrete system. The matrix $(zI - A)$ is called the **resolvent matrix** of the discrete system, and its inverse is fundamental in computing both the transfer function and the state transition matrix $A^k$. (3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Obtain the state-space representation in phase-variable canonical form for the discrete-time system described by:
$$y(k+3) + 6\,y(k+2) + 11\,y(k+1) + 6\,y(k) = u(k+1) + 2\,u(k) \qquad \text{(7 marks)}$$

**(b)** For a discrete-time system with
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0,$$
determine the pulse transfer function $G(z)$. Also comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a):

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients. Order $n=3$: $a_1 = 6, a_2 = 11, a_3 = 6$. RHS: $b_0 = 0, b_1 = 1, b_2 = 2, b_3 = 0$.

**Step 2:** Define phase variables:
$$x_1(k) = y(k), \quad x_2(k) = y(k+1), \quad x_3(k) = y(k+2)$$

**Step 3:** Write update relations:
$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = x_3(k)$$
$$x_3(k+1) = -6 x_1(k) - 11 x_2(k) - 6 x_3(k) + u(k+1) + 2 u(k)$$

**Step 4:** Output:
$$y(k) = x_1(k)$$

**Step 5:** Matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently swap the rows of $A$. Remember ‚Äî the bottom row contains $-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \dots, -a_1$ from left to right. The superdiagonal is all 1s. KTU examiners specifically deduct **2 marks** for this misplacement.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b):

**Step 1:** Compute $(zI - A)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
zI - A &= \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 2 & z+3 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Determinant:
$$\det(zI - A) = z(z+3) - (-1)(2) = z^2 + 3z + 2 = (z+1)(z+2)$$

**Step 3:** Adjugate:
$$\text{Adj}(zI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z+3 & 1 \\ -2 & z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply $\text{Adj}(zI-A) \cdot B$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Adj}(zI-A) \cdot B &= \begin{bmatrix} z+3 & 1 \\ -2 & z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $C$:
$$C \cdot \text{Adj}(zI-A) \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix} = 1 + z$$

**Step 6:** Final transfer function:
$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{z+1}{(z+1)(z+2)} = \frac{1}{z+2}}$$

**Stability Analysis:** Poles are at $z = -2$ and $z = -1$. Since $\vert -2 \vert = 2 > 1$, the system is **unstable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $G(z)$ correctly but forget the stability comment. KTU awards **1 mark** explicitly for stating the stability verdict based on the pole magnitudes. Also, students sometimes forget that **cancellations** between numerator and denominator zeros are allowed in stability analysis only if the cancelled mode is both controllable and observable.

---

### Q3(B). Alternative Long-Answer Choice

**(a)** A discrete-time system is governed by the difference equation:
$$y(k+2) - 1.5\,y(k+1) + 0.5\,y(k) = u(k+1) + u(k)$$
Obtain its state-space model and find $A, B, C, D$ matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the state-space matrices
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0,$$
determine the pulse transfer function $G(z)$ and the characteristic equation of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a):

**Step 1:** Coefficients: $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.5$; $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 1$, $b_2 = 1$.

**Step 2:** States: $x_1(k) = y(k)$, $x_2(k) = y(k+1)$.

**Step 3:** Updates:
$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = 1.5 x_2(k) - 0.5 x_1(k) + u(k+1) + u(k)$$

**Step 4:** Matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: There is a $u(k)$ term present; in the strict phase-variable formulation, we absorb it into an augmented $\tilde{B}$ vector, but since $b_0 = 0$, no shift is needed.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b):

**Step 1:** $(zI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ -0.16 & z-1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2:** $\det(zI-A) = z(z-1) - (-1)(-0.16) = z^2 - z - 0.16$.

**Step 3:** $\text{Adj}(zI-A) = \begin{bmatrix} z-1 & 1 \\ 0.16 & z \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 4:** $\text{Adj} \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 5:** $C \cdot \text{Adj} \cdot B = 1$.

**Step 6:** $G(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2 - z - 0.16}$.

**Characteristic Equation:**
$$z^2 - z - 0.16 = 0 \implies z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 0.64}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1.64}}{2}$$

Numerically: $z_1 \approx 1.14$, $z_2 \approx -0.14$. Since $\vert z_1 \vert = 1.14 > 1$, the system is **unstable**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers in State-Space Problems:**
> 1. **Sign errors in matrix $A$** ‚Äî Forgetting the negative sign on the $a_i$ coefficients in the last row costs **2‚Äì3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $C$ matrix dimensions** ‚Äî $C$ must be a row vector of size $1 \times n$ for SISO systems. Writing $C$ as a column vector is a serious structural error.
> 3. **Skipping the determinant step** ‚Äî Directly computing $(zI-A)^{-1}$ without first evaluating $\det(zI-A)$ makes error-checking impossible. KTU examiners **demand the intermediate steps**.
> 4. **Stability verdict missing** ‚Äî In any transfer-function-from-state-space problem, **always** check $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ and explicitly state the conclusion. **1 mark** is reserved for this.
> 5. **Units mismatch** ‚Äî Although often ignored in pure math, problems involving physical sampling period $T$ expect explicit mention that $T$ is in seconds and that the Nyquist criterion was applied.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ **Standard Form:** $x(k+1) = Ax(k) + Bu(k)$; $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$.
- ‚úÖ **Key Transfer-Function Formula:** $G(z) = C(zI-A)^{-1}B + D$ ‚Äî *memorize this verbatim*.
- ‚úÖ **Characteristic Equation:** Roots of $\det(zI - A) = 0$ are the **poles** (and eigenvalues) of $A$.
- ‚úÖ **Discrete Stability Criterion:** $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all $i \Rightarrow$ **BIBO stable**.
- ‚úÖ **Phase Variable Method:** Bottom row of $A = [-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \dots, -a_1]$; superdiagonal entries are 1; $B = [0, 0, \dots, 1]^T$; $C = [1, 0, \dots, 0]$.
- ‚úÖ **$D = 0$** for strictly proper systems (no direct feedthrough).
- ‚úÖ **Adjugate Formula:** $(zI-A)^{-1} = \text{Adj}(zI-A) / \det(zI-A)$.
- ‚úÖ **For $b_0 = 0$** (no input lead term), the simple phase-variable form applies directly. For $b_0 \neq 0$, shift inputs or use controller canonical form.
- ‚úÖ **Order $n$ =** number of independent delays = dimension of state vector $x$.
- ‚úÖ **MATLAB Equivalents (for lab reference only):** `ss(A,B,C,D, Ts)` to create model; `ss2tf(A,B,C,D)` for transfer function; `tf2ss(num,den)` for reverse conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Canonical Forms, Discrete Time State Transition Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Canonical Forms, Discrete Time State Transition Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Canonical Forms ‚Äî The Formal Definition

In discrete-time state-space representation, a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is described by:

$$x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$$
$$y[k] = C x[k] + D u[k]$$

where $x[k] \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector, $u[k]$ is the input, $y[k]$ is the output, and $(A, B, C, D)$ are the system matrices with appropriate dimensions.

A **Canonical Form** is a special, standardized realization of the state-space model $(A, B, C, D)$ obtained by applying a nonsingular linear transformation $T$ to the state vector, such that:

$$\bar{x}[k] = T x[k]$$

This transforms the system into:

$$\bar{A} = T A T^{-1}, \quad \bar{B} = T B, \quad \bar{C} = C T^{-1}$$

The transfer function and the system's input-output behavior remain **invariant** under such similarity transformations; only the internal representation changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four primary canonical realizations expected in board examinations are:
> 1. **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**
> 2. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)**
> 3. **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)**
> 4. **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "Many Rooms, Same Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a large office building (the *system*) with multiple rooms (the *states*). The building's external appearance ‚Äî the entrance, the windows, the address ‚Äî represents the **input-output behavior**. You can rearrange the rooms inside the building in countless ways: an open floor plan, cubicles, partitioned offices, etc. Each internal layout is a different **canonical form**, but the building still serves the same purpose to an outside visitor.
>
> Canonical forms are simply *standardized blueprints* that reveal internal structural properties ‚Äî controllability, observability, decoupling ‚Äî which the original model may hide.

---

## 1.3 Discrete Time State Transition Matrix ‚Äî The "Memory of the System"

The **Discrete-Time State Transition Matrix** $\Phi(k)$ describes how the state of an unforced (homogeneous) system evolves from an initial condition $x[0]$ over $k$ sampling instants:

$$\boxed{\Phi(k) = A^k, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots}$$

For the homogeneous system $x[k+1] = A x[k]$, the solution is:

$$x[k] = \Phi(k) \, x[0] = A^k x[0]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Continuous-Time:** In continuous-time, the transition matrix is $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$. In discrete-time, the matrix exponent is replaced by **ordinary integer powers** of $A$, because the time index advances in unit discrete steps.

**Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** For a sampling period of **T seconds**, the discrete sequence $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ corresponds to physical times $t = 0, T, 2T, \ldots$

---

## 1.4 Why Canonical Forms and $\Phi(k)$ Matter in KTU Board Exams

- They form the **analytical bridge** between transfer functions and state-space models.
- Canonical forms expose **structural properties** (controllability/observability) by inspection.
- The transition matrix $\Phi(k) = A^k$ is the **core engine** of discrete-time state response computation, stability analysis (via eigenvalues of $A$), and digital controller design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Major Canonical Forms

### A. Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)

Consider the pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_{n-1} z + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n}$$

The CCF realization has the companion structure:

$$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_c = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 \;\vert\; b_2 - a_2 b_0 \;\vert\; \cdots \;\vert\; b_n - a_n b_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The characteristic polynomial $z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$ appears directly as the last column (with sign reversal) of $A_c$. The structure is "bottom-up" ‚Äî each delayed state feeds back into the first row via coefficients $a_i$.

### B. Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

OCF is the **dual (transpose) of CCF**:

$$A_o = A_c^T, \quad B_o = C_c^T, \quad C_o = B_c^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Explicitly:

$$A_o = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -a_2 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_{n-1} & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### C. Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

When the system matrix $A$ has **n distinct eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$, the matrix can be diagonalized:

$$A_d = T^{-1} A T = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$$

The columns of $T$ are the **eigenvectors** of $A$. The system decomposes into **n independent first-order scalar subsystems**:

$$x_i[k+1] = \lambda_i x_i[k] + \beta_i u[k]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** DCF completely decouples the system. Each state evolves independently, simplifying stability analysis (system stable iff $|\lambda_i| < 1 \ \forall i$) and parallel controller design.

### D. Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)

When $A$ has **repeated eigenvalues** but is not defective (i.e., fewer than n independent eigenvectors), $A$ is similar to a **Jordan matrix** with Jordan blocks along the diagonal:

$$A_J = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & J_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & J_r \end{bmatrix}, \quad J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_i & 1 & & 0 \\ & \lambda_i & \ddots & \\ & & \ddots & 1 \\ 0 & & & \lambda_i \end{bmatrix}_{m_i \times m_i}$$

---

## 2.2 Discrete-Time State Transition Matrix ‚Äî Full Properties

For the homogeneous equation $x[k+1] = A x[k]$:

$$x[k] = A^k x[0] \equiv \Phi(k) \, x[0]$$

### Key Properties of $\Phi(k) = A^k$:

1. $\Phi(0) = A^0 = I_n$ (Identity matrix)
2. $\Phi(k_1 + k_2) = \Phi(k_1) \cdot \Phi(k_2) = A^{k_1+k_2}$ (Semigroup property)
3. $\Phi(-k) = A^{-k} = (A^{-1})^k$, provided $A^{-1}$ exists
4. $[\Phi(k)]^m = \Phi(km) = A^{km}$
5. Eigenvalues of $\Phi(k)$ are $\lambda_i^k$, where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of $A$.

### Forced Response (Complete Solution):

For the non-homogeneous system with input sequence $u[k]$:

$$\boxed{x[k] = A^k x[0] + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B \, u[j]}$$

$$y[k] = C A^k x[0] + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} C A^{k-1-j} B \, u[j] + D u[k]$$

---

## 2.3 Methods to Compute $A^k$

### Method 1: Direct Power Computation (small n)

Compute $A^2, A^3, \ldots$ iteratively. Impractical for large $k$.

### Method 2: Cayley-Hamilton Theorem

Every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation. For a $3 \times 3$ matrix with characteristic polynomial:

$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^3 + a_1 \lambda^2 + a_2 \lambda + a_3 = 0$$

Then:

$$A^3 + a_1 A^2 + a_2 A + a_3 I = 0$$

Hence $A^3 = -a_1 A^2 - a_2 A - a_3 I$. Any higher power $A^k$ can be reduced to a polynomial:

$$A^k = \alpha_0(k) I + \alpha_1(k) A + \alpha_2(k) A^2$$

Coefficients are found by substituting $A = \lambda_i$ (eigenvalues):

$$\lambda_i^k = \alpha_0(k) + \alpha_1(k) \lambda_i + \alpha_2(k) \lambda_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

### Method 3: Z-Transform Method (Most Efficient for KTU)

Starting from $x[k+1] = A x[k]$, take the one-sided Z-transform:

$$z X(z) - z x[0] = A X(z)$$

$$(z I - A) X(z) = z x[0]$$

$$X(z) = z (z I - A)^{-1} x[0]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Phi(k) = A^k = \mathcal{Z}^{-1} \{ z (z I - A)^{-1} \}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite:** This method is the **most commonly tested** because it converts a matrix problem into rational inverse-Z-transform problems.

### Method 4: Spectral Decomposition

If $A = V \Lambda V^{-1}$ where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$:

$$A^k = V \Lambda^k V^{-1}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Conditions / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| State equation | $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$ | LTI discrete system |
| Output equation | $y[k] = C x[k] + D u[k]$ | $D=0$ for strictly proper systems |
| Transition matrix | $\Phi(k) = A^k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Initial condition | $\Phi(0) = I_n$ | Identity matrix of order $n$ |
| Semigroup property | $\Phi(k_1+k_2) = \Phi(k_1)\Phi(k_2)$ | Additive index property |
| Forced response | $x[k] = A^k x[0] + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u[j]$ | Convolution sum form |
| Z-transform method | $A^k = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z(zI-A)^{-1}\}$ | Most efficient for KTU exams |
| Resolvent | $(zI - A)^{-1} = \frac{\text{adj}(zI - A)}{\det(zI - A)}$ | Use for manual computation |
| CCF matrix $A_c$ | Companion form with $a_i$ in last row reversed | Top row: $-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_n$ |
| CCF input $B_c$ | $B_c = [1, 0, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$ | Standard basis vector |
| OCF matrix $A_o$ | $A_o = A_c^T$ | Dual of CCF |
| Diagonal form | $A_d = V^{-1} A V = \text{diag}(\lambda_i)$ | $V$ columns = eigenvectors of $A$ |
| Stability | $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1, \ \forall i$ | Eigenvalues inside unit circle |
| Cayley-Hamilton | $A^n = -a_1 A^{n-1} - \cdots - a_n I$ | Recursive reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe-Symbol Note for Tables:** All absolute-value / modulus symbols inside tables are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown table parser breakage.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** Canonical forms guide the choice of realization with minimum round-off noise (e.g., parallel DCF vs. direct CCF).
- **Microcontroller Implementation:** OCF requires minimal multiplication operations, ideal for low-power embedded controllers.
- **Stability Analysis:** Computing $A^k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ is equivalent to asymptotic stability, validated by $\vert \lambda_i(A) \vert < 1$.
- **Deadbeat Control:** In minimum-time digital controllers, canonical forms reduce pole placement to direct coefficient matching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Solution of Discrete-Time State Equation

We start with the non-homogeneous first-order vector difference equation:

$$x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k], \quad x[0] = x_0$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $x[1]$ by substituting $k = 0$:**

$$x[1] = A x[0] + B u[0]$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $x[2]$ by substituting $k = 1$:**

$$x[2] = A x[1] + B u[1] = A(A x[0] + B u[0]) + B u[1]$$
$$x[2] = A^2 x[0] + A B u[0] + B u[1]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $x[3]$ by substituting $k = 2$:**

$$x[3] = A x[2] + B u[2] = A(A^2 x[0] + A B u[0] + B u[1]) + B u[2]$$
$$x[3] = A^3 x[0] + A^2 B u[0] + A B u[1] + B u[2]$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Generalize by induction:**

By inspection of the pattern, at step $k$:

$$x[k] = A^k x[0] + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u[j]$$

$$\boxed{x[k] = \Phi(k) x[0] + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi(k-1-j) B u[j]}$$

where $\Phi(k) = A^k$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $A^k$ via Z-Transform Method

**Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Z-transform to $x[k+1] = A x[k]$:**

Using the standard one-sided Z-transform property $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k+1]\} = z X(z) - z x[0]$:

$$z X(z) - z x[0] = A X(z)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Rearrange:**

$$z X(z) - A X(z) = z x[0]$$
$$(z I - A) X(z) = z x[0]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $X(z)$:**

$$X(z) = z (z I - A)^{-1} x[0]$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Take inverse Z-transform, noting $x[k] = A^k x[0]$:**

$$\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\} = x[k] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z (z I - A)^{-1}\} x[0]$$

Comparing both expressions:

$$\boxed{A^k = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{ z (z I - A)^{-1} \right\}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing $A^k$ by Z-Transform Method (KTU Pattern)

Let:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.24 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Form $zI - A$:**

$$z I - A = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 0.24 & z - 1.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $\det(z I - A)$:**

$$\det(z I - A) = z(z - 1.1) - (-1)(0.24) = z^2 - 1.1 z + 0.24$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Factor the determinant:**

Find roots: $z^2 - 1.1z + 0.24 = (z - 0.4)(z - 0.6)$. So eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 0.4, \lambda_2 = 0.6$.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute $(z I - A)^{-1}$ using the adjugate:**

$$\text{adj}(z I - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.1 & 1 \\ -0.24 & z \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(z I - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(z-0.4)(z-0.6)} \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.1 & 1 \\ -0.24 & z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Multiply by $z$:**

$$z(z I - A)^{-1} = \frac{z}{(z-0.4)(z-0.6)} \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.1 & 1 \\ -0.24 & z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the (1,1) entry in partial fractions:**

$$\frac{z(z - 1.1)}{(z - 0.4)(z - 0.6)} = \frac{A_1}{z - 0.4} + \frac{A_2}{z - 0.6}$$

Using residue method:

$$A_1 = \left. \frac{z(z-1.1)}{z-0.6} \right|_{z=0.4} = \frac{0.4 \cdot (-0.7)}{-0.2} = \frac{-0.28}{-0.2} = 1.4$$

$$A_2 = \left. \frac{z(z-1.1)}{z-0.4} \right|_{z=0.6} = \frac{0.6 \cdot (-0.5)}{0.2} = \frac{-0.3}{0.2} = -1.5$$

So entry (1,1) of $z(zI-A)^{-1}$ is $\frac{1.4}{z-0.4} - \frac{1.5}{z-0.6}$.

**Step 7 ‚Äî Inverse Z-transform gives $A^k$ entry (1,1):**

$$\Phi_{11}(k) = 1.4 \cdot (0.4)^k - 1.5 \cdot (0.6)^k$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Conversion to Controllable Canonical Form

Given the pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{z^2 + 2z + 3}{z^2 + 4z + 5}$$

Here $n = 2$, $a_1 = 4$, $a_2 = 5$, $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 2$, $b_2 = 3$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify coefficients:**
$a_1 = 4, a_2 = 5, b_0 = 1, b_1 = 2, b_2 = 3$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Construct CCF matrices:**

$$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $C_c$ coefficients:**

$$c_1 = b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 2 - 4 \cdot 1 = -2$$
$$c_2 = b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 3 - 5 \cdot 1 = -2$$

$$C_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0 = 1$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verify the transfer function is preserved:**

$$G(z) = C_c (z I - A_c)^{-1} B_c + D$$

The characteristic polynomial of $A_c$ is $z^2 + 4z + 5$, matching the denominator. The numerator coefficients match as derived. The realization is verified.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Diagonal Canonical Form Construction

Given $A$ with $n$ distinct eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Eigen-decomposition:**

Let $v_i$ be the eigenvector of $A$ corresponding to $\lambda_i$, where $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Form the modal matrix:**

$$V = [v_1 \vert v_2 \vert \cdots \vert v_n]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Construct diagonal form:**

$$A_d = V^{-1} A V = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Transform B and C:**

$$B_d = V^{-1} B, \quad C_d = C V$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Resulting decoupled system:**

Each scalar state $x_i[k+1] = \lambda_i x_i[k] + \beta_i u[k]$ evolves independently, where $\beta_i$ is the $i$-th component of $B_d$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Discrete-Time State Space System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU["u_k"] --> nodeS["Summing Junction"]
    nodeS --> nodeB["B Matrix Gain"]
    nodeB --> nodeSum["Summing Node"]
    nodeSum --> nodeInt["Delay / Shift Unit z^-1"]
    nodeInt --> nodeX["State Vector x_k"]
    nodeX --> nodeA["A Matrix Multiplication"]
    nodeA --> nodeS
    nodeX --> nodeC["C Matrix"]
    nodeC --> nodeY["Output y_k"]
    nodeX --> nodeDelay["Delay z^-1"]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture ‚Äî Canonical Form Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Start Given Gz or ABCD"] --> check1{"Eigenvalues of A?"}
    check1 -- "n distinct real or complex" --> dcf["Diagonal Canonical Form DCF"]
    check1 -- "Repeated eigenvalues" --> check2{"Geometric multiplicity equals algebraic?"}
    check2 -- "Yes" --> dcf2["Diagonal Form still possible"]
    check2 -- "No repeated but defective" --> jcf["Jordan Canonical Form JCF"]
    start1 --> ccf1["Companion from Transfer Function Gz"]
    ccf1 --> ccf["Controllable Canonical Form"]
    ccf1 --> ocf["Observable Canonical Form by Duality"]
    dcf --> compare["All Forms share same eigenvalues hence same stability"]
    jcf --> compare
    ccf --> compare
    ocf --> compare
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow Graph ‚Äî Controllable Canonical Form

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["u_k"] --> nodeB1["Gain 1"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeSum["Summing Node"]
    nodeA1["Gain a1 negative"] --> nodeSum
    nodeA2["Gain a2 negative"] --> nodeSum
    nodeAN["Gain an negative"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeX1["State x1 k plus 1"]
    nodeX1 --> nodeD1["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeX1k["State x1 k"]
    nodeX1k --> nodeC1["Gain c1"]
    nodeX1k --> nodeA2
    nodeX1k --> nodeSum2["Second State Path"]
    nodeX1k --> nodeShift2["Shift to x2"]
    nodeShift2 --> nodeD2["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeX2k["State x2 k"]
    nodeX2k --> nodeA1
    nodeX2k --> nodeC2["Gain c2"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeY["Output y_k"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeY
```

---

## 4.4 Computational Workflow ‚Äî Computing A to Power k

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK["Compute A to power k"]
    startK --> dimCheck{"Matrix size n?"}
    dimCheck -- "n small le 3" --> direct["Direct Iteration Method"]
    dimCheck -- "n moderate" --> chMethod["Cayley Hamilton Method"]
    dimCheck -- "n large or symbolic" --> zMethod["Z Transform Method"]
    direct --> resK["Result A to power k"]
    chMethod --> eigSolve["Find Eigenvalues of A"]
    eigSolve --> polySolve["Solve Linear System for Coefficients alpha k"]
    polySolve --> resK
    zMethod --> invForm["Compute zI minus A inverse"]
    invForm --> partFrac["Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    partFrac --> invZ["Inverse Z Transform"]
    invZ --> resK
```
