# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define state space representation of a control system. Why is it preferred over transfer function model for modern control applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The state space representation of a control system expresses the system dynamics as a set of first-order coupled matrix differential equations of the form $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$, where $x(t)$ is the **$n \times 1$ state vector**, $u(t)$ is the input, $y(t)$ is the output, and $A, B, C, D$ are constant matrices of appropriate dimensions. *(1 mark)*

It is preferred over the transfer function model for the following reasons *(2 marks)*:
1. It captures **internal dynamics** (states), not just input‚Äìoutput behavior.
2. It handles **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** systems naturally, whereas TF is limited to SISO.
3. It forms the foundation for **modern control techniques** like pole placement, observer design, optimal control, and Kalman filtering.
4. It is amenable to **numerical/symbolic computation** in software like MATLAB and Python.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) of a state space model? Write the general $A$, $B$, and $C$ matrices for a third-order strictly proper transfer function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Controllable Canonical Form (CCF), also known as the **Phase Variable Form**, is a specific state space realization in which the system matrix $A$ has a companion structure, the input matrix $B$ has a single 1 in the last entry, and the output matrix $C$ contains the numerator coefficients. It is widely used because it simplifies **controllability analysis** ‚Äî the controllability matrix becomes a lower triangular Toeplitz matrix, which is always full rank for controllable systems. *(1 mark)*

For a third-order strictly proper transfer function

$$G(s) = \frac{b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0}{s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}$$

the matrices are: *(2 marks)*

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often **transpose the $C$ matrix** instead of reading off the numerator coefficients in their natural order. Remember: in CCF, $C = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}]$ ‚Äî *not* transposed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **sign convention** in the last row of $A$. The coefficients $a_i$ appear with **negative signs**: $-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **CCF and OCF**. CCF places 1's on the **super-diagonal** (above the main diagonal); OCF places 1's on the **sub-diagonal** (below the main diagonal).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In DCF, forgetting that $C$ contains **residues from PFE**, not the original numerator coefficients.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to verify that the realization has the correct transfer function by computing $C(sI - A)^{-1}B$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$:**

**(a)** Obtain the state space model in **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the state space model in **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** using duality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ‚Äî CCF (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify coefficients from the given transfer function.** *(1 mark)*

Comparing $G(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$ with the standard form:

$$a_0 = 6, \quad a_1 = 11, \quad a_2 = 6, \quad b_0 = 6, \quad b_1 = 8, \quad b_2 = 2$$

**Step 2: Construct the system matrix $A_{CCF}$ using the companion structure.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Construct the input matrix $B_{CCF}$.** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Construct the output matrix $C_{CCF}$ from the numerator coefficients.** *(2 marks)*

$$C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Set $D = 0$ since $G(s)$ is strictly proper.** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{CCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ‚Äî OCF via Duality (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Recall the duality relationship between CCF and OCF.** *(1 mark)*

$$A_{OCF} = A_{CCF}^T, \quad B_{OCF} = C_{CCF}^T, \quad C_{OCF} = B_{CCF}^T$$

**Step 2: Compute $A_{OCF}$ by transposing $A_{CCF}$.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & -11 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $B_{OCF}$ by transposing $C_{CCF}$.** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $C_{OCF}$ by transposing $B_{CCF}$.** *(2 marks)*

$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Set $D = 0$.** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{OCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & -11 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

**Verification (Bonus):** Both realizations must yield the same transfer function $G(s)$. Using the formula $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$, students should verify that the determinant expansion reproduces $G(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Consider the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$.**

**(a)** Obtain the **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)** state space realization using partial fraction expansion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the **state space model in Phase Variable Form (CCF)** and verify that both realizations yield the same transfer function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ‚Äî DCF (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the poles of $G(s)$.** *(1 mark)*

$$p_1 = -1, \quad p_2 = -2, \quad p_3 = -3$$

All three poles are **distinct and real**, so DCF is applicable.

**Step 2: Perform Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE).** *(3 marks)*

$$G(s) = \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{r_1}{s+1} + \frac{r_2}{s+2} + \frac{r_3}{s+3}$$

Computing each residue using the cover-up method:

$$r_1 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+2)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{12}{(1)(2)} = 6$$

$$r_2 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{12}{(-1)(1)} = -12$$

$$r_3 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)} \right|_{s=-3} = \frac{12}{(-2)(-1)} = 6$$

Therefore:

$$G(s) = \frac{6}{s+1} - \frac{12}{s+2} + \frac{6}{s+3}$$

**Step 3: Construct the $A_{DCF}$ matrix (diagonal of poles).** *(1 mark)*

$$A_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Construct $B_{DCF}$ (column of ones).** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Construct $C_{DCF}$ (row of residues).** *(1 mark)*

$$C_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -12 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{DCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -12 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ‚Äî CCF and Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Expand $G(s)$ into a standard polynomial ratio.** *(2 marks)*

$$G(s) = \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

So: $a_0 = 6$, $a_1 = 11$, $a_2 = 6$, $b_0 = 12$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 0$.

**Step 2: Construct the CCF matrices.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Verify using $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$.** *(3 marks)*

Compute $(sI - A)$:

$$sI - A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 6 & 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}$$

The determinant is:

$$\det(sI - A) = s[s(s+6) + 11] + 1[0 \cdot (s+6) - (-1)(6)] = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6$$

Numerator: $C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B$ picks the **last column** of the adjugate, which equals the cofactor vector:

$$\text{cofactor}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} s \cdot s - (-1)(-1) \\ 0 \cdot s - (-1)(6) \\ 0 \cdot 11 - s \cdot 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s^2 - 1 \\ 6 \\ -6s \end{bmatrix}$$

So:

$$C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s^2 - 1 \\ 6 \\ -6s \end{bmatrix} = 12(s^2 - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$G(s) = \frac{12(s^2 - 1)}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

Wait ‚Äî this is incorrect! Let me re-verify. For the original $G(s) = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, the numerator should be just $12$. The issue is that I incorrectly applied CCF for $b_0 = 12, b_1 = 0, b_2 = 0$. Let me check: $C_{CCF} = [b_0, b_1, b_2] = [12, 0, 0]$.

Recomputing:

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B = \text{last column of adjugate} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

Then $C \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = 12 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0 = 12$. ‚úì

So $G(s) = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, which matches. **Both DCF and CCF realizations are verified.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The earlier calculation showing $12(s^2-1)$ was due to an algebraic slip in the cofactor expansion. The corrected verification demonstrates the importance of *carefully evaluating every term*. KTU examiners will deduct marks for any incorrect intermediate step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** ‚Äî Read this the morning of your exam.

### üìå Core Definitions
- **State:** Minimum set of variables that completely describe the system's future behavior given its current state and future inputs.
- **State Vector $x$:** Column vector of all state variables, dimension $n \times 1$.
- **State Space Model:** $\{A, B, C, D\}$ matrices describing $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$.
- **Canonical Form:** A *specific structure* of the $A, B, C, D$ matrices for a given TF.
- **Strictly Proper TF:** Numerator degree $<$ denominator degree. Yields $D = 0$.
- **Proper TF:** Numerator degree $\leq$ denominator degree.
- **Improper TF:** Numerator degree $>$ denominator degree (rare in physical systems).

### üìå Key Conversion Rules
- **Order of TF = Dimension of state vector = Size of $A$ matrix.**
- **In CCF:** Last row of $A$ = $[-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}]$.
- **In CCF:** $B$ has a single 1 at the *last* entry.
- **In CCF:** $C = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}]$.
- **OCF is the transpose-dual of CCF:** $A_{OCF} = A_{CCF}^T$, $B_{OCF} = C_{CCF}^T$, $C_{OCF} = B_{CCF}^T$.
- **DCF:** $A$ is diagonal with poles; $C$ contains **residues** from PFE.
- **JCF:** Repeated poles yield Jordan blocks with 1's on the super-diagonal.
- **Cascade Form:** TF factored into first/second-order blocks, multiplied in series.
- **Parallel Form:** TF decomposed via PFE into parallel branches.

### üìå Verification Formula
- Always verify using $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.

### üìå Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
1. ‚ùå Forgetting the **negative signs** on $a_i$ in the last row of $A$.
2. ‚ùå Mixing up CCF and OCF structures (super-diagonal vs. sub-diagonal placement of 1's).
3. ‚ùå Using original numerator coefficients instead of PFE residues in DCF.
4. ‚ùå Failing to handle **repeated poles** by using JCF instead of DCF.
5. ‚ùå Assuming state space representation is unique ‚Äî it is **not**, only $G(s)$ is unique.
6. ‚ùå Setting $D = 0$ for proper (but not strictly proper) transfer functions.
7. ‚ùå Confusing "$n$-th order TF" with "n state variables" ‚Äî they are the same!

### üìå MATLAB / Python Cross-Verification Code
- **MATLAB:** `sys = tf(num, den); ss(sys)` yields a default realization.
- **Python (control library):** `sys = control.tf2ss(num, den)` or `control.StateSpace(...)`.

### üìå One-Line Memory Hooks
- **CCF:** "**C**ontrollable has **C**ompany in the last **C**olumn of $B$" ‚Äî i.e., $B = [0, \dots, 0, 1]^T$.
- **OCF:** "**O**bservable has its **O**wn 1 in $C$" ‚Äî i.e., $C = [0, \dots, 0, 1]$.
- **DCF:** "**D**iagonal has the **D**enominator poles on the **D**iagonal."
- **JCF:** "**J**ordan has the **J**umper (1's) above the diagonal for repeated roots."

### üìå Question-Pattern Recognition for KTU 2024
- "Obtain state model in phase variable form" ‚Üí **CCF**
- "Find state model in observable canonical form" ‚Üí **OCF**
- "Find diagonal canonical form" ‚Üí **DCF** (check for distinct poles first!)
- "Obtain state space model" (no form specified) ‚Üí Default to **CCF**
- "Find Jordan form" ‚Üí **JCF** (check for repeated poles)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Digital Control Systems**, the **Characteristic Equation** of a discrete-time (sampled-data) control system is the polynomial equation obtained by equating the denominator of the **closed-loop pulse transfer function** to zero. It encapsulates the intrinsic dynamic behaviour of the system and determines the location of the system's natural modes (characteristic roots) in the complex z-plane.

For a single-loop sampled-data system with forward path pulse transfer function **G(z)** and feedback path pulse transfer function **H(z)**, the closed-loop pulse transfer function is:

$$T(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

The **Characteristic Equation** is therefore defined as:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The roots of the characteristic equation are called the **characteristic roots** (or closed-loop poles). For the system to be **stable**, all characteristic roots must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** of the z-plane (i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all roots $z_i$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the characteristic equation as the **"DNA fingerprint"** of a digital control system. Just as a person's DNA determines their physical traits, the characteristic equation uniquely determines how a digital system will respond over time when disturbed.

**Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Bouncing Ball in a Stadium:**
- Imagine dropping a ball inside a cylindrical stadium with curved walls.
- The characteristic equation is like the **mathematical fingerprint of the stadium's shape**.
- If the equation says the ball's "mode positions" are inside a small circle (the unit circle), the ball eventually **settles at the bottom** ‚Üí **Stable System**.
- If the modes lie on the circle's boundary, the ball **bounces forever at constant height** ‚Üí **Marginally Stable System**.
- If the modes lie outside the circle, the ball **flies out of the stadium** ‚Üí **Unstable System**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Unit Circle Radius:** **1** (in the z-plane, this is the stability boundary).
- **Sampling Period:** **T** (seconds) ‚Äî fundamental discrete-time parameter.
- **Critical Damping Ratio Equivalent:** For digital systems, poles closer to the origin correspond to **faster response** with **shorter settling time**.
- **Bilinear Transformation Constant:** **T = 2** is the standard substitution period for w-plane mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> The characteristic equation is the **bridge between frequency-domain analysis (z-plane)** and **time-domain response** of a discrete-time system. Solving it tells you whether your digital controller will keep the plant stable or drive it to oscillations/divergence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚Äî Why the Characteristic Equation Matters

The characteristic equation governs **three fundamental properties** of a digital control system:

1. **Stability** ‚Äî Determined entirely by the location of the roots.
2. **Transient Response** ‚Äî Pole magnitudes dictate settling time and overshoot.
3. **Natural Modes** ‚Äî Each root corresponds to one exponential mode $z_i^k$ in the time response.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic from Closed-Loop Structure

A sampled-data feedback system has the following structure:
- Reference $R(z)$ ‚Üí Summing junction ‚Üí Error $E(z)$ ‚Üí Digital Plant $G(z)$ ‚Üí Output $C(z)$.
- Output fed back through $H(z)$ to the summing junction.

The error signal equation:

$$E(z) = R(z) - H(z)C(z)$$

Substituting $C(z) = G(z)E(z)$:

$$E(z) = R(z) - H(z)G(z)E(z)$$

$$E(z)[1 + G(z)H(z)] = R(z)$$

Therefore, the closed-loop output becomes:

$$C(z) = \frac{G(z)R(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

The denominator polynomial $\mathbf{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ when set to zero yields the **characteristic equation**.

## 2.2 State-Space Formulation of the Characteristic Equation

For a discrete-time state-space representation:

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

The characteristic equation is given by the **eigenvalue equation** of the state transition matrix $\Phi$:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$$

where $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the state transition matrix and $I$ is the identity matrix of the same order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The eigenvalues of $\Phi$ are precisely the characteristic roots. The system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues satisfy $\vert \lambda_i(\Phi) \vert < 1$.

## 2.3 Stability Conditions in the z-Plane

| Condition on Root $\vert z_i \vert$ | System Behaviour |
|:---:|:---|
| $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (strictly inside unit circle) | **Asymptotically Stable** |
| $\vert z_i \vert = 1$ (on the unit circle, simple roots) | **Marginally Stable** (sustained oscillation) |
| $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ (outside unit circle) | **Unstable** (response grows unbounded) |
| Repeated roots on unit circle | **Unstable** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation (z-domain) |
| 2 | $T(z) = \dfrac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ | Closed-loop pulse transfer function |
| 3 | $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$ | Characteristic equation from state-space |
| 4 | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ | Stability criterion in z-plane |
| 5 | $z = \dfrac{1 + w}{1 - w}$ | Bilinear transformation (z to w) |
| 6 | $w = \dfrac{z - 1}{z + 1}$ | Inverse bilinear transformation (w to z) |
| 7 | $T_s \approx \dfrac{-4}{\ln(\vert z_i \vert_{\max})} \cdot T$ | Approximate settling time formula |
| 8 | $\zeta \approx -\cos(\angle z_i)$ | Equivalent damping ratio from pole angle |
| 9 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Mapping from s-plane to z-plane |
| 10 | $\omega_d = \dfrac{1}{T}\angle z_i$ | Equivalent damped natural frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industries like aerospace, robotics, and process control, the characteristic equation is the **first checkpoint** during controller design. Engineers use root locus techniques in the z-plane to tune digital controllers (PID, lead-lag) so that all characteristic roots remain inside the unit circle for guaranteed stability.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Digital PID Controllers:** Tuning gains by ensuring closed-loop characteristic roots lie within a desired region of the unit circle.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Discrete-state observers (Kalman filters) require characteristic roots of error dynamics inside the unit circle for accurate state estimation.
- **Power Electronics:** Digital SMPS controllers use characteristic equation analysis to avoid subharmonic oscillations.
- **Networked Control Systems:** Delay-induced characteristic root migration is analysed to design stable communication-aware controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Equation from a Standard Second-Order Sampled System

Consider a sampled-data system with forward path:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$$

Sampling period $T = 1$ second. The corresponding z-transfer function is:

$$G(z) = \frac{z(1 - e^{-T})}{(z - 1)(z - e^{-T})} = \frac{0.632z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.368)}$$

Assuming unity feedback $H(z) = 1$, the closed-loop pulse transfer function:

$$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)} = \frac{0.632z}{(z-1)(z-0.368) + 0.632z}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Expand the denominator:**

$$(z-1)(z-0.368) = z^2 - 0.368z - z + 0.368 = z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Add the feedback term $0.632z$:**

$$z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.632z = z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Form the characteristic equation by setting denominator to zero:**

$$z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for the characteristic roots using the quadratic formula:**

$$z = \frac{0.736 \pm \sqrt{0.736^2 - 4(0.368)}}{2}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Evaluate the discriminant:**

$$0.736^2 = 0.541696$$
$$4 \times 0.368 = 1.472$$
$$\Delta = 0.541696 - 1.472 = -0.930304$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the complex square root:**

$$\sqrt{-0.930304} = j\sqrt{0.930304} \approx j\,0.9646$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Final roots:**

$$z_{1,2} = \frac{0.736 \pm j\,0.9646}{2} = 0.368 \pm j\,0.4823$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Check magnitudes (stability test):**

$$\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.368^2 + 0.4823^2} = \sqrt{0.1354 + 0.2326} = \sqrt{0.368} \approx 0.6066$$

Since $\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert \approx 0.6066 < 1$, **the system is stable** ‚úì.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Characteristic Equation from State-Space Representation

Given the discrete state-space model of a DC motor position control with sampling period $T = 0.1$ s:

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9516 & 0.0975 \\ -0.951 & 0.8561 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00484 \\ 0.0975 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Form the matrix $(zI - \Phi)$:**

$$zI - \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.9516 & -0.0975 \\ 0.951 & z - 0.8561 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the determinant:**

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = (z - 0.9516)(z - 0.8561) - (-0.0975)(0.951)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Expand the first product:**

$$(z - 0.9516)(z - 0.8561) = z^2 - 0.8561z - 0.9516z + 0.9516 \times 0.8561$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Evaluate the constant product:**

$$0.9516 \times 0.8561 = 0.8147$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Combine the linear terms:**

$$-0.8561z - 0.9516z = -1.8077z$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Evaluate the second product term:**

$$(-0.0975)(0.951) = -0.09272$$

So $-(0.0975 \times 0.951)$ with the sign flipped gives $+0.09272$.

**Step 7 ‚Äî Form the complete polynomial:**

$$z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.8147 + 0.09272 = z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.9074$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî The characteristic equation is:**

$$z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.9074 = 0$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Solve using the quadratic formula:**

$$z = \frac{1.8077 \pm \sqrt{1.8077^2 - 4(0.9074)}}{2}$$

$$1.8077^2 = 3.2678$$
$$4 \times 0.9074 = 3.6296$$
$$\Delta = 3.2678 - 3.6296 = -0.3618$$

**Step 10 ‚Äî Final roots:**

$$z_{1,2} = \frac{1.8077 \pm j\sqrt{0.3618}}{2} = 0.9039 \pm j\,0.3007$$

**Step 11 ‚Äî Stability check:**

$$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{0.9039^2 + 0.3007^2} = \sqrt{0.8170 + 0.0904} = \sqrt{0.9074} \approx 0.9526$$

Since $\vert z_i \vert \approx 0.9526 < 1$, the **system is asymptotically stable** ‚úì.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bilinear Transformation (z to w) for Routh-Hurwitz

For a discrete system with characteristic equation in z:

$$P(z) = z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the substitution $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:**

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 0.736\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Multiply throughout by $(1-w)^2$:**

$$(1+w)^2 - 0.736(1+w)(1-w) + 0.368(1-w)^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Expand each term:**

$$(1+w)^2 = 1 + 2w + w^2$$
$$(1+w)(1-w) = 1 - w^2$$
$$(1-w)^2 = 1 - 2w + w^2$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute and collect:**

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 0.736(1 - w^2) + 0.368(1 - 2w + w^2) = 0$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Expand the constant and $w$ terms:**

$$= 1 + 2w + w^2 - 0.736 + 0.736w^2 + 0.368 - 0.736w + 0.368w^2$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Group $w^2$, $w$, and constant terms separately:**

$$w^2 \text{ coefficient: } 1 + 0.736 + 0.368 = 2.104$$
$$w \text{ coefficient: } 2 - 0.736 = 1.264$$
$$\text{Constant: } 1 - 0.736 + 0.368 = 0.632$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Final characteristic equation in w-plane:**

$$2.104\,w^2 + 1.264\,w + 0.632 = 0$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Apply Routh-Hurwitz check:**

All coefficients are positive ‚áí **System is stable** in the w-plane ‚áí Original discrete system is **stable in z-plane** ‚úì.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Location of Characteristic Equation in a Sampled Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reference R of z] --> SJ[ Summing Junction ]
    SJ --> E[Error E of z]
    E --> H1[Zero Order Hold G_h0]
    H1 --> P1[Plant G_p of s]
    P1 --> S1[Sampler Switch]
    S1 --> GZ[Pulse Transfer G of z]
    GZ --> C[Output C of z]
    C --> F1[Feedback H of z]
    F1 --> SJ
    GZ -.Characteristic Root.-> CEQ[ 1 plus GzHz equals 0 ]
    F1 -.Forms Denominator.-> CEQ
    CEQ --> STAB[Stability Check: Mod z less than 1 ]
    STAB --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|All inside| OK[Stable System]
    DEC -->|On boundary| MG[Marginally Stable]
    DEC -->|Outside| US[Unstable System]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Characteristic Equation Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Plant Model Gs] --> B[Z Transform with ZOH]
    B --> C[Discrete Model Gz]
    D[Sensor Model Hz] --> C
    C --> E[Form Closed Loop Tz]
    E --> F[Extract Denominator Polynomial]
    F --> G[Set Polynomial to Zero]
    G --> H[Characteristic Equation]
    H --> I[Method 1: Direct Root Finding]
    H --> J[Method 2: Bilinear W Transform]
    H --> K[Method 3: Jury Stability Test]
    I --> L[Stability Verdict]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Controller Tuning Action]
```

## 4.3 Stability Decision Flowchart for z-Plane Roots

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Obtain Characteristic Equation Pz equals 0] --> Q[Find All Roots z_i]
    Q --> R[Compute Magnitudes mod z_i]
    R --> S{All magnitudes less than 1?}
    S -->|Yes| T[ Asymptotically Stable ]
    S -->|No, some equals 1| U[Check Multiplicity]
    S -->|No, some greater than 1| V[ Unstable ]
    U --> W{Simple roots on boundary?}
    W -->|Yes| X[ Marginally Stable ]
    W -->|No, repeated| V
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above follow the **Mermaid syntax safety protocol**: every node identifier is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `P`, `Q`), and labels are kept in raw uppercase alphanumeric text to avoid markdown formatting conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the characteristic equation of a digital control system and state the stability condition based on its roots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic equation of a digital control system is the polynomial obtained by equating the denominator of the closed-loop pulse transfer function to zero, expressed as:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

For **asymptotic stability**, all roots $z_i$ of this equation must lie strictly **inside the unit circle** of the z-plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every $i$.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Write the characteristic equation in the state-space form for a discrete-time system described by $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic equation in the state-space form is given by:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$$

where $z$ is the complex variable, $I$ is the identity matrix of the same order as the state transition matrix $\Phi$, and the roots of this determinant equation are the eigenvalues of $\Phi$ (characteristic roots).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A ‚Äî 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

For a sampled-data control system with forward path pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368}$$

and unity feedback $H(z) = 1$, with sampling period $T = 1$ s:

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the characteristic roots and comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Characteristic Equation**

The closed-loop pulse transfer function is:

$$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)} = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.368z + 0.264}$$

Combining the denominator terms:

$$z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.368z + 0.264 = z^2 - 1.0z + 0.632$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$z^2 - z + 0.632 = 0$$

**(b) Finding the Characteristic Roots**

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1^2 - 4(0.632)}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2.528}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{-1.528}}{2}$$

$$z = \frac{1 \pm j\,1.2361}{2} = 0.5 \pm j\,0.618$$

Stability check ‚Äî magnitude of each root:

$$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.618^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.3819} = \sqrt{0.6319} \approx 0.795$$

Since $\vert z_i \vert \approx 0.795 < 1$, **both characteristic roots lie inside the unit circle**, and the system is **asymptotically stable** ‚úì.

---

### Question B ‚Äî 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`

A discrete-time system is described by the state-space matrices:

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.1 \\ -0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Derive the characteristic equation in the z-domain using the state-space formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the bilinear transformation $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to obtain the w-plane characteristic polynomial and verify stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Characteristic Equation from State-Space**

Form the matrix $(zI - \Phi)$:

$$zI - \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.8 & -0.1 \\ 0.5 & z - 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the determinant:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = (z - 0.8)(z - 0.6) - (-0.1)(0.5)$$

Expanding:

$$= z^2 - 0.6z - 0.8z + 0.48 + 0.05 = z^2 - 1.4z + 0.53$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$z^2 - 1.4z + 0.53 = 0$$

**(b) Bilinear Transformation and Routh-Hurwitz Verification**

Substituting $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 1.4\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.53 = 0$$

Multiplying by $(1-w)^2$:

$$(1+w)^2 - 1.4(1+w)(1-w) + 0.53(1-w)^2 = 0$$

Expanding each term:

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.4(1 - w^2) + 0.53(1 - 2w + w^2) = 0$$

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.4 + 1.4w^2 + 0.53 - 1.06w + 0.53w^2 = 0$$

Grouping by powers of $w$:

$$w^2: \quad 1 + 1.4 + 0.53 = 2.93$$
$$w^1: \quad 2 - 1.06 = 0.94$$
$$w^0: \quad 1 - 1.4 + 0.53 = 0.13$$

The w-plane polynomial is:

$$2.93\,w^2 + 0.94\,w + 0.13 = 0$$

**Routh-Hurwitz Check:** All coefficients $(2.93, 0.94, 0.13)$ are strictly positive, and the polynomial is of order 2. Therefore, **all w-plane roots lie in the left half-plane** ‚áí the original discrete system is **stable** ‚úì.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Characteristic Equation Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Error in State-Space Determinant:** Students often forget the negative sign when forming $(zI - \Phi)$. The correct form is $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$, **not** $\det(\Phi - zI) = 0$. A sign flip will change all root magnitudes and stability verdict ‚Äî **deduct 2‚Äì3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusion Between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation:** The characteristic equation is for the **closed-loop** system, i.e., $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$. Using just the denominator of $G(z)$ is a frequent mistake ‚Äî **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Unit Circle Stability Criterion:** Students sometimes equate z-plane stability to s-plane conditions (Re < 0). Always state explicitly $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for discrete systems ‚Äî **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Bilinear Transformation Algebra Errors:** Sign errors when applying $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ are very common. Verify by substituting $w=0$ and checking $z=1$ ‚Äî **deduct 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not Commenting on Stability:** KTU examiners expect a clear **verdict line** ("system is stable / unstable / marginally stable") with justification based on root magnitudes. A bare numerical answer loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Characteristic equation = $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ for closed-loop discrete systems.
- **State-Space Form:** $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$; roots = eigenvalues of $\Phi$.
- **Stability Criterion:** All roots must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- **Boundary Cases:** Simple roots on unit circle ‚Üí marginally stable; repeated roots on unit circle ‚Üí unstable.
- **Bilinear Transformation:** Use $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to map the unit circle interior to the **left half of the w-plane**, enabling Routh-Hurwitz application.
- **Three Solution Methods:**
  1. Direct root finding (factoring / quadratic formula).
  2. Bilinear transformation + Routh-Hurwitz.
  3. **Jury's stability test** (tableau method, no need to find roots).
- **Jury's Test (Quick Recall):** Necessary conditions for stability of $P(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$:
  1. $P(1) > 0$
  2. $(-1)^n P(-1) > 0$
  3. $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$
- **Settling Time Relation:** $T_s \approx \dfrac{-4T}{\ln(\vert z_{\text{dom}} \vert)}$ where $z_{\text{dom}}$ is the dominant pole.
- **Damping Estimate:** $\zeta \approx -\cos(\theta)$ where $\theta = \angle z_i$.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Place dominant closed-loop poles at $\vert z \vert \approx 0.3 - 0.7$ for **fast yet well-damped** digital response.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always derive the characteristic equation **before** commenting on stability ‚Äî this is the marker for the bulk of the marks.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The characteristic equation is the **denominator of $T(z)$** set to zero, not the numerator. Numerator roots are **zeros**, not characteristic roots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of Discrete State Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solution of Discrete State Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Digital Control Systems**, a discrete-time state-space representation of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is expressed as:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

where $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **state vector**, $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the **input vector**, $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times 1}$ is the **output vector**, and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ are constant matrices.

**Solution of Discrete State Equations** refers to the analytical determination of the state vector $x(k)$ at any arbitrary discrete time instant $k$, given the initial state $x(0)$, the input sequence $u(k)$, and the system matrices $(A, B)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** The solution of the homogeneous equation $x(k+1) = Ax(k)$ is given by $x(k) = A^k x(0)$, where $A^k$ is termed the **State Transition Matrix** (denoted $\phi(k) = A^k$ for the discrete LTI case). For the forced response, the total solution is:
> $$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey elevator system** in a building. The state of the elevator at each floor (i.e., its position and velocity) at the next instant $k+1$ depends **only on**:
1. Its current state at floor $k$ (captured by $A x(k)$ ‚Äî how the system naturally evolves).
2. The control button pressed by the passenger at that instant (captured by $B u(k)$ ‚Äî external push).

The "Solution of Discrete State Equations" is like a **crystal ball** for the elevator operator. Given where the elevator is *now* ($x(0)$) and the entire schedule of button presses ($u(0), u(1), \ldots, u(k-1)$), the equation tells us *exactly* which floor the elevator will be at after $k$ steps. The matrix $A^k$ acts as the **memory of physics** ‚Äî it encodes how each past state and past input continues to influence the present.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why discrete?** Because in digital control, the computer samples the real-world analog system at discrete instants $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. We never get a continuous curve ‚Äî only a sequence of snapshots.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sampling period $T$** (in **seconds**): The fixed time interval between two successive samples. This is the heartbeat of the digital controller.
- **State dimension $n$**: An integer representing the order of the system (number of energy storage elements).
- **Eigenvalues of $A$**: Must lie **inside the unit circle** ($|z_i| < 1$) in the complex $z$-plane for system stability ‚Äî this is a hard constraint in digital control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Methods of Solution (KTU-Listed)

The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **three canonical methods** for solving discrete state equations:

**Method 1: Recursion / Iterative Method (Direct Substitution)**
- Best for short-horizon numerical computation.
- Simply substitute $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ sequentially.
- Closed-form: $x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$

**Method 2: Z-Transform Method**
- Best for deriving analytical closed-form solutions.
- Apply $\mathcal{Z}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides, solve algebraically in the $z$-domain, then apply inverse Z-transform.
- This method leverages the shifting theorem: $\mathcal{Z}\{x(k+1)\} = z X(z) - z x(0)$.

**Method 3: State Transition Matrix Method (Cayley-Hamilton)**
- Used when $A^k$ must be computed efficiently for large $k$.
- Uses Cayley-Hamilton theorem to express $A^k$ as a linear combination of lower powers: $A^k = \alpha_0(k) I + \alpha_1(k) A + \alpha_2(k) A^2 + \ldots + \alpha_{n-1}(k) A^{n-1}$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the problem type.** Is it homogeneous ($B u(k) = 0$) or forced (with input)? Is $A$ time-varying or constant? This determines which formula from the cheat sheet applies.

**Step 2 ‚Äî For homogeneous systems:** Compute $A^k$ (the state transition matrix $\phi(k)$). Use either direct matrix multiplication, Cayley-Hamilton, or Z-transform ‚Äî whichever yields the simplest form.

**Step 3 ‚Äî For forced systems:** Compute the **zero-input response** ($A^k x(0)$) and the **zero-state response** ($\sum A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$) and superimpose them by linearity.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verify stability.** Check that all eigenvalues of $A$ satisfy $|z_i| < 1$. If yes, $x(k) \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ for the unforced case.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Use convolution interpretation.** Recognize that the forced response is a discrete convolution: $x_{zs}(k) = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \phi(k-1-j) B u(j)$, analogous to continuous-time $x(t) = \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Equation / Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Use Case** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Discrete State Equation (Forced) | $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ | General LTI discrete system |
| 2 | Output Equation | $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$ | Output extraction |
| 3 | State Transition Matrix | $\phi(k) = A^k$ | Zero-input response kernel |
| 4 | Total Solution (Forced) | $x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$ | Complete state trajectory |
| 5 | Total Solution (Z-domain) | $X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} z x(0) + (zI - A)^{-1} B U(z)$ | Algebraic solution |
| 6 | Resolvent Matrix | $\Phi(z) = (zI - A)^{-1}$ | Core building block |
| 7 | Property 1: $\phi(0) = I$ | $A^0 = I$ (identity matrix) | Initial condition check |
| 8 | Property 2: $\phi(k_1 + k_2) = \phi(k_1) \phi(k_2)$ | $A^{k_1+k_2} = A^{k_1} A^{k_2}$ | Time-shift composition |
| 9 | Property 3: $\phi^{-1}(k) = \phi(-k)$ | $A^{-k} = (A^k)^{-1}$ | Reverse-time evolution |
| 10 | Time-Varying Generalization | $x(k) = \phi(k, 0) x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \phi(k, j+1) B(j) u(j)$ | Non-stationary systems |
| 11 | Time-Varying Transition Property | $\phi(k_2, k_0) = \phi(k_2, k_1) \phi(k_1, k_0)$ | Cascade composition |
| 12 | Cayley-Hamilton Form | $A^k = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \alpha_i(k) A^i$ | Efficient high-power computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** In the summation $\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$, students often write the exponent as $A^{k-j}$ ‚Äî this is **wrong**. The correct form is $A^{k-1-j}$ because $A^{k-1-j} = A^{(k-1) - j}$, and it must produce $A^0 = I$ when $j = k-1$ (matching the immediate response to the most recent input).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Discrete state equation solutions form the **backbone of modern digital controller design**:
- **Autonomous vehicles:** State = [position, velocity, heading]. $A^k$ predicts the trajectory; $B u(k)$ captures steering/throttle.
- **Robotic manipulators:** State = joint angles and rates. The solution enables **model predictive control (MPC)** by simulating the robot's future over a horizon.
- **Power electronics:** State = inductor currents and capacitor voltages in DC-DC converters.
- **Aircraft autopilot:** State = pitch, yaw, roll rates. Digital state-space simulation is done in flight computers in real time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Total Solution by Iterative Method

We start with the discrete state equation and the initial condition $x(0)$. The goal is to derive the closed-form expression for $x(k)$ in terms of $A$, $B$, $u(j)$, and $x(0)$.

**Step 1:** Write the state equation at time $k = 0$:

$$x(1) = A x(0) + B u(0)$$

This is just direct substitution. The state at sample $1$ equals the natural evolution of $x(0)$ plus the effect of the first input.

**Step 2:** Write the state equation at time $k = 1$ and substitute the result of Step 1:

$$x(2) = A x(1) + B u(1)$$
$$x(2) = A [A x(0) + B u(0)] + B u(1)$$
$$x(2) = A^2 x(0) + A B u(0) + B u(1)$$

Notice how the structure emerges: a pure $A^2 x(0)$ term, plus a sum involving $B u(\cdot)$ scaled by increasing powers of $A$.

**Step 3:** Write the equation at $k = 2$ and substitute the result of Step 2:

$$x(3) = A x(2) + B u(2)$$
$$x(3) = A [A^2 x(0) + A B u(0) + B u(1)] + B u(2)$$
$$x(3) = A^3 x(0) + A^2 B u(0) + A B u(1) + B u(2)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the pattern. After $k$ steps, the general result is:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + A^{k-1} B u(0) + A^{k-2} B u(1) + \ldots + A B u(k-2) + B u(k-1)$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite the summation compactly by changing the index. Let the index of $u$ be $j$, ranging from $0$ to $k-1$. The power of $A$ must produce $A^0 = I$ when $j = k-1$:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** When $j = 0$, the term is $A^{k-1} B u(0)$ ‚úì. When $j = k-1$, the term is $A^0 B u(k-1) = B u(k-1)$ ‚úì. The derivation is correct.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Solution via Z-Transform

The Z-transform method converts the difference equation into algebraic form in the $z$-domain, which is then solved directly.

**Step 1:** Take the one-sided Z-transform of the state equation $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$. Using the standard shift property:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x(k+1)\} = z X(z) - z x(0)$$

Therefore:

$$z X(z) - z x(0) = A X(z) + B U(z)$$

**Step 2:** Collect all $X(z)$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$z X(z) - A X(z) = z x(0) + B U(z)$$
$$(z I - A) X(z) = z x(0) + B U(z)$$

**Step 3:** Pre-multiply both sides by the resolvent $(z I - A)^{-1}$ to isolate $X(z)$:

$$X(z) = (z I - A)^{-1} z x(0) + (z I - A)^{-1} B U(z)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the structure. The first term is the **zero-input response**; the second is the **zero-state response** (forced). We can rewrite the first term using the identity:

$$(z I - A)^{-1} z = (I - z^{-1} A)^{-1}$$

So:

$$X(z) = (I - z^{-1} A)^{-1} x(0) + (z I - A)^{-1} B U(z)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the inverse Z-transform to recover $x(k)$ in the time domain. The first term inverts to $A^k x(0)$ (because $\mathcal{Z}\{A^k\} = (I - z^{-1} A)^{-1}$ for $|z| > |\lambda_{max}|$). The second term involves a discrete convolution in the $z$-domain:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \mathcal{Z}^{-1} \{(z I - A)^{-1} B U(z)\}$$

The inverse Z-transform of a product equals a discrete-time convolution:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$$

This matches the iterative result perfectly ‚Äî confirming the correctness of both derivations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cayley-Hamilton Method for Computing $A^k$

When $k$ is large or symbolic (e.g., $k$ is a variable), direct matrix multiplication is impractical. The Cayley-Hamilton theorem provides a compact closed form.

**Step 1:** Recall that every square matrix $A$ satisfies its own characteristic equation:

$$A^n + a_{n-1} A^{n-1} + \ldots + a_1 A + a_0 I = 0$$

where the $a_i$ are coefficients of the characteristic polynomial $\Delta(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^n + a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \ldots + a_0$.

**Step 2:** This means any higher power $A^k$ (for $k \geq n$) can be reduced to a linear combination of $\{I, A, A^2, \ldots, A^{n-1}\}$:

$$A^k = \alpha_0(k) I + \alpha_1(k) A + \alpha_2(k) A^2 + \ldots + \alpha_{n-1}(k) A^{n-1}$$

**Step 3:** Find the scalar coefficients $\alpha_i(k)$ by substituting $A \to \lambda_i$ (the eigenvalues). For distinct eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_i^k = \alpha_0(k) + \alpha_1(k) \lambda_i + \alpha_2(k) \lambda_i^2 + \ldots + \alpha_{n-1}(k) \lambda_i^{n-1}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 4:** Solve the resulting $n \times n$ linear system for $\alpha_i(k)$ in terms of $k$. The result is then substituted back into Step 2 to obtain $A^k$ as a closed-form function of $k$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, and $u(k) = 0$ (homogeneous case), find $x(1)$, $x(2)$, and $x(3)$ using the iterative method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $x(1)$:**

$$x(1) = A x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $x(2)$:**

$$x(2) = A x(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $x(3)$:**

$$x(3) = A x(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verify using closed form.** Compute $A^2$:

$$A^2 = A \cdot A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then $A^2 x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ ‚úì ‚Äî matches $x(2)$ from Step 2.

### 3.5 Generalization to Time-Varying Systems

For the time-varying case, $A(k)$ and $B(k)$ change with $k$. The state transition matrix becomes a two-argument quantity $\phi(k, k_0)$ defined by:

$$\phi(k_0, k_0) = I, \quad \phi(k+1, k_0) = A(k) \phi(k, k_0)$$

The total solution is:

$$x(k) = \phi(k, 0) x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \phi(k, j+1) B(j) u(j)$$

This reduces to the LTI result when $A(k) = A$ and $B(k) = B$ for all $k$, because then $\phi(k, j+1) = A^{k-1-j}$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix of Methods (Comparison)

| **Aspect** | **Iterative Method** | **Z-Transform Method** | **Cayley-Hamilton Method** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Best for | Short horizons (small $k$) | Symbolic / closed-form | Large or symbolic $k$ |
| Computational cost | High for large $k$ | Moderate (polynomial division) | High setup, low runtime |
| Handles time-varying $A$? | Yes, directly | No (needs modifications) | No |
| KTU typical marks | 4‚Äì6 | 7‚Äì10 | 8‚Äì12 |
| Stability insight | Indirect | Direct (poles of $(zI-A)^{-1}$) | Direct (eigenvalues of $A$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Solution Methods

The following block diagram illustrates how the three solution methods interrelate, all starting from the same discrete state equation and ending at the state trajectory $x(k)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Discrete State Equation x of k plus 1 equals A x of k plus B u of k] --> initCond[Initial State x of 0 and Input u of k]
    
    startA --> m1[Method 1: Iterative Substitution]
    startA --> m2[Method 2: Z Transform]
    startA --> m3[Method 3: Cayley Hamilton]
    
    initCond --> m1
    initCond --> m2
    initCond --> m3
    
    m1 --> res1[x of k equals A to the k x of 0 plus sum A to k minus 1 minus j B u of j]
    m2 --> res2[Inverse Z Transform of zI minus A inverse z x of 0 plus zI minus A inverse B U of z]
    m3 --> res3[Closed form A to k as sum alpha i of k A to the i]
    
    res1 --> out[Final State Trajectory x of k]
    res2 --> out
    res3 --> out
    
    out --> check{All eigenvalues of A satisfy mod lambda less than 1?}
    check -->|Yes| stable[System is Asymptotically Stable]
    check -->|No| unstable[System is Unstable]
    
    style startA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style stable fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style unstable fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the Solution Process

The diagram below shows how the zero-input response and zero-state response combine via the principle of superposition to form the complete state solution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZIR[Zero Input Response Block]
        x0[Initial State x of 0] --> phi[State Transition Matrix phi of k equals A to the k]
        phi --> zir[Zero Input Term A to the k x of 0]
    end
    
    subgraph ZSR[Zero State Response Block]
        u[Input Sequence u of 0 to u of k minus 1] --> bmult[B Matrix Multiplication]
        phi2[State Transition Matrix phi of k minus 1 minus j] --> bmult
        bmult --> sumblk[Summation from j equals 0 to k minus 1]
        sumblk --> zsr[Zero State Term sum A to k minus 1 minus j B u of j]
    end
    
    zir --> add[Superposition Sum]
    zsr --> add
    add --> xk[Total State x of k]
    
    xk --> yk[Output y of k equals C x of k plus D u of k]
    
    style ZIR fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style ZSR fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style xk fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0
    style yk fill:#80cbc4,stroke:#004d40
```

### 4.3 Eigenvalue Stability Check Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a[Compute Characteristic Polynomial of A] --> b[Find Eigenvalues lambda i]
    b --> c{All lambda i satisfy mod lambda i less than 1 in z plane?}
    c -->|Yes, all inside unit circle| d[System is Asymptotically Stable, x of k tends to 0]
    c -->|Some on unit circle| e[Marginally Stable, Oscillations Persist]
    c -->|Some outside| f[Unstable, x of k grows unbounded]
    
    style d fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style e fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style f fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the **state transition matrix** of a discrete-time system. State its two important properties.

**Model Answer:**
The state transition matrix of a discrete LTI system is defined as $\phi(k) = A^k$, where $A$ is the system matrix. It maps the initial state $x(0)$ to the state at time $k$ in the absence of any input.

**Property 1:** $\phi(0) = A^0 = I$ (the identity matrix), meaning that at $k=0$ the state is unchanged.

**Property 2:** $\phi(k_1 + k_2) = \phi(k_1) \cdot \phi(k_2) = A^{k_1} A^{k_2}$, indicating the semi-group property ‚Äî the state transition is composable across consecutive time intervals.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Write the expression for the total solution of the discrete state equation $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ with initial condition $x(0)$.

**Model Answer:**
The total solution is the superposition of the zero-input response and the zero-state response:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$$

The first term $A^k x(0)$ represents the **natural evolution** of the system starting from $x(0)$ with no input. The summation term captures the **convolution** of the past inputs $u(j)$ with the system's dynamics, weighted by the transition matrix.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the discrete system $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ with

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.24 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad u(k) = 1 \text{ (unit step)}$$

Find the state vector $x(1)$, $x(2)$, and $x(3)$ using the iterative method.

**Model Solution:**

**Computing $x(1)$:** Substitute $k = 0$ into the state equation:

$$x(1) = A x(0) + B u(0)$$
$$x(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.24 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1$$
$$x(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) \\ -0.24 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$x(1) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1.24 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -0.24 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Computing $x(2)$:** Substitute $k = 1$:

$$x(2) = A x(1) + B u(1)$$
$$x(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.24 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -0.24 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1$$
$$x(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot (-0.24) \\ -0.24 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot (-0.24) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$x(2) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.24 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.24 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Computing $x(3)$:** Substitute $k = 2$:

$$x(3) = A x(2) + B u(2)$$
$$x(3) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.24 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.24 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1$$
$$x(3) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot (-0.24) + 1 \cdot 1 \\ -0.24 \cdot (-0.24) + 1 \cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$x(3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1.0576 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2.0576 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Answer:** $x(1) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -0.24 \end{bmatrix}$, $x(2) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.24 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $x(3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2.0576 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Verify whether the system in part (a) is stable by examining the eigenvalues of $A$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the characteristic polynomial:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 0.24 & \lambda - 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$= \lambda (\lambda - 1) - (-1)(0.24) = \lambda^2 - \lambda + 0.24$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Find the eigenvalues using the quadratic formula:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4(0.24)}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.96}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.04}}{2}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{1 \pm 0.2}{2}$$
$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Check stability criterion:** Both eigenvalues have magnitude less than 1:

$$|\lambda_1| = 0.6 < 1, \quad |\lambda_2| = 0.4 < 1$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **asymptotically stable**. As $k \to \infty$, the zero-input response $A^k x(0) \to 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] ‚Äî *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a discrete LTI system with

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

and unit step input $u(k) = 1$ for $k \geq 0$, derive the solution using the **Z-transform method** and find $x(2)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Take the Z-transform of the state equation:**

$$z X(z) - z x(0) = A X(z) + B U(z)$$

For a unit step, $U(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$ and $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, so $z x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ z \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Rearrange to solve for $X(z)$:**

$$(z I - A) X(z) = z x(0) + B U(z)$$
$$X(z) = (z I - A)^{-1} \left[ z x(0) + B U(z) \right]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $(z I - A)$:**

$$z I - A = \begin{bmatrix} z - 1 & -1 \\ 0 & z + 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the inverse using the adjugate method:**

$$\det(z I - A) = (z-1)(z+1) - 0 = z^2 - 1$$

$$\text{adj}(z I - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 0 & z-1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(z I - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1} \begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 0 & z-1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the bracket term:**

$$z x(0) + B U(z) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ z \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{z}{z-1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ z + \frac{z}{z-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

Simplify the second component:

$$z + \frac{z}{z-1} = \frac{z(z-1) + z}{z-1} = \frac{z^2 - z + z}{z-1} = \frac{z^2}{z-1}$$

So:

$$z x(0) + B U(z) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{z^2}{z-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Multiply to get $X(z)$:**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1} \begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 0 & z-1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{z^2}{z-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{z^2}{z-1} \\ \frac{z^2(z-1)}{z-1} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{z^2}{z-1} \\ z^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $X_2(z)$ (the second component):

$$X_2(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 1} = \frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z+1)}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Partial fraction expansion of $X_2(z)$:**

$$\frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z+1)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z+1}$$

Multiplying through: $z^2 = A(z+1) + B(z-1)$.

At $z = 1$: $1 = 2A \Rightarrow A = 0.5$.
At $z = -1$: $1 = -2B \Rightarrow B = -0.5$.

$$X_2(z) = \frac{0.5}{z-1} - \frac{0.5}{z+1} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{z}{z-1} \cdot \frac{1}{z} - 0.5 \cdot \frac{z}{z+1} \cdot \frac{1}{z}$$

Wait ‚Äî let me redo this more carefully using the standard $X(z) = \sum \frac{R_i \cdot z}{z - p_i}$ form for inverse Z-transform.

$$\frac{X_2(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z+1)} = \frac{0.5}{z-1} + \frac{0.5}{z+1}$$

$$X_2(z) = \frac{0.5 z}{z-1} + \frac{0.5 z}{z+1}$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Inverse Z-transform:**

$$x_2(k) = 0.5 (1)^k + 0.5 (-1)^k = 0.5 [1 + (-1)^k]$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Compute $x_2(2)$:**

$$x_2(2) = 0.5 [1 + (-1)^2] = 0.5 [1 + 1] = 1.0$$

**Final Answer:** $x_2(2) = 1$. (A similar procedure for $X_1(z)$ yields $x_1(2)$.)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three methods of solving discrete state equations and compare them on the basis of computational complexity and applicability.

**Model Answer:**

**Method 1 ‚Äî Iterative Method:** Direct substitution of $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. Most straightforward, requires no advanced mathematical tools. However, it gives $x(k)$ only for specific $k$ values, not as a closed-form function. Best suited for numerical simulations and short prediction horizons.

**Method 2 ‚Äî Z-Transform Method:** Converts the difference equation into algebraic form in the $z$-domain using $\mathcal{Z}\{x(k+1)\} = z X(z) - z x(0)$. The solution is $X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} [z x(0) + B U(z)]$. The inverse Z-transform gives the closed-form $x(k)$. This method provides deep insight into system stability via pole locations.

**Method 3 ‚Äî Cayley-Hamilton Method:** Reduces $A^k$ to a linear combination of lower powers using the characteristic equation. Particularly efficient for **large or symbolic** $k$ where direct iteration is infeasible. Gives a compact closed-form expression $\alpha_0(k), \alpha_1(k), \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}(k)$.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Iterative** | **Z-Transform** | **Cayley-Hamilton** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Closed-form? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Computational load | Low for small $k$ | Moderate | High setup, low runtime |
| Stability analysis | Indirect | Direct | Direct via eigenvalues |
| Time-varying $A$? | Yes | No | No |
| Implementation effort | Minimal | Moderate | High |

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Summation index error:** Students frequently write the forced response as $\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-j} B u(j)$ instead of $A^{k-1-j}$. This shifts the matrix powers by one and produces an incorrect answer. The exponent MUST be $k-1-j$ so that the latest input $u(k-1)$ is multiplied by $A^0 = I$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $z x(0)$ vs $x(0)$:** In the Z-transform method, the shift property gives $z X(z) - z x(0)$ ‚Äî note the $z$ multiplying $x(0)$. Some students drop the leading $z$, leading to a missing factor.
> 
> 3. **Inverting $(zI - A)$ incorrectly:** The most common error is forgetting to compute the determinant or mixing up the adjugate matrix. Always verify with the property $(zI - A) \cdot (zI - A)^{-1} = I$.
> 
> 4. **Stability criterion confusion:** The condition is $|\lambda_i| < 1$ for ALL eigenvalues, where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of $A$ (not of $A^T$ or $A^{-1}$). Eigenvalues of $A$ and $A^T$ are identical, but this is a common point of confusion.
> 
> 5. **Unit confusion with $T$:** If the original continuous-time system has sampling period $T$, the discrete $A$ matrix depends on $T$. The discrete state equation is **only valid at sampling instants** ‚Äî students sometimes incorrectly interpolate between samples.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **discrete state equation** is $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$; the output is $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$.
- [x] The **state transition matrix** is $\phi(k) = A^k$ for LTI systems.
- [x] The **total solution** is $x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-1-j} B u(j)$ ‚Äî the first term is zero-input, the second is zero-state.
- [x] The **Z-transform method** yields $X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} [z x(0) + B U(z)]$.
- [x] The **resolvent matrix** $(zI - A)^{-1}$ is the Z-transform of $A^k$, and its poles give the eigenvalues of $A$.
- [x] **Stability** requires all eigenvalues of $A$ to satisfy $|\lambda_i| < 1$ (inside the unit circle in the $z$-plane).
- [x] **Key properties of $\phi(k)$:** $\phi(0) = I$, $\phi(k_1 + k_2) = \phi(k_1) \phi(k_2)$, $\phi^{-1}(k) = \phi(-k)$.
- [x] **Cayley-Hamilton** lets us express $A^k = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \alpha_i(k) A^i$ ‚Äî useful for large/symbolic $k$.
- [x] For **time-varying systems**, $\phi(k, k_0)$ depends on two time arguments and $\phi(k_2, k_0) = \phi(k_2, k_1) \phi(k_1, k_0)$.
- [x] The **discrete convolution sum** is the digital analog of the continuous-time convolution integral.
- [x] The summation index $j$ runs from $0$ to $k-1$ ‚Äî never $1$ to $k$.
